content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rddo, -ere 'to roar, bellow' [v. Ill; pf. rudivT] (Varro+)
Pit. *roud-/*rud~.
PIE *h r(e)ud-H- [pr.] 'to weep, roar'. IE cognates: Skt. rudanti [3p.act.], rodisi
3
[2s.act.] 'to weep, bewail', OAv. raosta [aor.], uruddiiata [pr.] 'laments'; OLith.
rausti 'weeps', Lith. rauda 'lamentation', raudoti 'to weep, lament', OE reotan 'to
complain, weep'.
The reconstruction of the (suffix?) *-H- is based on Skt. rodi$i. In view of Skt. ruvati
'roar', Gr. 6puo|iai 'to howl, roar', Lat. rumor < *h ru-, final *-d- may have been a
3
suffix, and the initial laryngeal may be *h -.
3
Bibl.: WH II: 447, EM 579, IEW 867fi, LIV *reudH-. ravus, rumor

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rufus 'red, tawny, red-haired' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: rufulus 'reddish' (P1.+).
Pit. *roupo- 'red'. It. cognates: U. rofu [acc.pl.m.], rofa [acc.pl.fi] 'red'.
PIE *h,roudh-o- 'red'. IE cognates: Olr. ruad, OW rud, MW rhudd, Co. rud, OBret.
rud, Bret, ruz 'red' < PCI. *roudo~, Lith. raudas, Latv. raiids 'reddish brown, bay,
reddish', Ru. rudyj (dial.) 'blood-red', rudoj (dial.) 'red, red-haired, dirty', Go. roups
Ted'.
A borrowing from a Sabellic language with Pit. *-p- > -/-.
Bibl.: WH II: 448, EM 579, IEW 872f., Untermann 2000: 638. ^ raudus, robus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rflga 'crease, shallow groove' [f. a] (Titinius+)
Derivatives: rugare 'to become creased' (P1.+), rugdsus 'full of wrinkles' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *roug-a~.
PIE *hjroug-h - 'wrinkle'. IE cognates: see s.v. -rugo.
2
A connection with runcare and Lith. raukas 'wrinkle' is not directly possible because
of the different velar. Since words for 'wrinkle' and 'crease' are often derived from
'to be rugged', from which also 'to belch' is often derived (cf. de Vaan 2001: 169),
the most obvious connection is with e-rugere 'to belch'.
Bibl.: WH II: 448fi, EM 579, IEW 868-871, Schrijver 1991: 24. -> -rugo
rugiO, -Ire 'to bellow, roar (e.g. of lions or deer)' [v. IV] (Suet.)
IE cognates: Gr. epeuyopai 'to roar', aor. fjpuygv (H.), opupaySot; [m.] 'noise',
cbpuyi] 'noise, roaring', cbpuopai 'to roar'.
Identity with e-rugo 'to belch* seems unlikely, since 'belching* hardly sounds like
'roaring*. There is a PIE root *(H)ru- 'to roar', to which various consonants have
been appended in the different IE branches (cf. rudo). Greek has a g-extension in
cbpuYHi but there is also Epeuyopai 'to roar' (unless the translation is wrong, and we
are dealing with the homophonous verb 'to belch'). For Latin rugid, the late date of
attestation renders it conceivable that it was influenced by mugio.
Bibl.: WH II: 449, EM 579f., IEW 867f, Schrijver 1991: 18, LIV *h reuH-. ->
3
mugio, rudo
-rugo, -ere 'to belch' [v. Ill; ppp. -ructum]: erugere 'to belch' (Enn.+)
Derivatives: ructarefi 'to bring up noisily, belch' (P1.+), ructus, -iis 'belch' (P1.+),
eructare 'to throw up, belch' (Varro+); rumen [n.] 'first stomach of a ruminant'
(Pompon.+), ruminare 'to chew, ruminate, meditate' (Andr.+), rumare 'to chew'
(Paul, ex F).
Pit. *roug-e/o~.
PIE *h|reug-e/o- 'to belch'. IE cognates: MoP a-roy 'belch', Gr. epeuyopai 'to belch
out, vomit', also epuyyavco, Arm. orcam < *orucam < *erucam, Lith. raugeti 'to
belch', raugas 'leaven, tannine' < *h|roug-o-, rdugti 'to leaven, pickle', OHG
ita-r...

de Vaan Latin Etymology: rumor 'noise, rumour' [m. rj (P1.+)
Derivatives: rumitdre 'to gossip' (Naev.); rumiferare 'to carry reports of (PI.).
Pit. *roumos-.
PIE *h3re/ouH-mo- 'roaring'? IE cognates: Skt. ruvdti 'to roar', rdva- [m.] 'roaring,
howling, noise', YAv. uruuant- 'roaring', Gr. tbpuopai 'to howl', OCS rjuti, Is. rovg
'to roar'.
WH and IEW derive rumor from the root of Skt. ruvdti 'to roar'. If correct, the etymology
would imply *re/ou-mo~ 'shouting' > Pit *reu-m-os [coll.] 'shouting, rumour'.
Bibl.: WH II: 450f„ EM 581, IEW 867f, LIV *h reuH-. ravus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rumpo, -ere 'to burst, break open' [v. Ill; pf. rupT, ppp. ruptum] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: riimentum 'breaking off (Fest); abrumpere 'to break, burst' (Enn.+),
corrumpere 'to damage, spoil' (PI.+), corruptela 'corruption, bribing' (P1.+),
corruptor 'perverter, seducer' (P1.+), deruptus 'steep' (Lucr.+), dirumpere 'to break
apart' (PL+), erumpere 'to burst out, sprout' (Cato+), eruptid 'sudden rush, discharge'
(Sis.+), irmptio 'violent entry, assault' (P1.+), interrumpere 'to break up, interrupt'
(PI.+); rvpes, -is 'cliff, crag' (Acc.+), rupex, -icis 'uncivilized person' (Lucil.+).
Pit *m-n-p- *roup- 'to break', *wup-(e)h 'cliff, *rup-to- 'broken'.
s r
PIE *Hru-n-p- [pr.] 'to break', *Hreup- [aor.]. IE cognates: Skt. pr. rupya-, caus.
ropaya- 'to suffer from stomach ache', red.aor. arurupas [2s.act.] 'to rack', ropi- [f.]
'racking pain', OAv. urupaiia- 'to cause racking pain', MP rubudan 'to rob, grab',
OE reofan 'to break, tear', bi-reafian 'to rob'.
Possibly, rupex was built directly on an inherited zero-grade variant *Hrup- of the
root.
Bibl.: WH II: 451, EM 581f, IEW 868-871, Meiser 2003: 203, LIV *reup-.
runcO, -are 'to grub up (plants), weed' [v. I] (Cato+)
Pit. *runk-a-.
PIE *h ru-n-k- 'to dig out'. IE cognates: Skt. luficati 'to pluck' (ep.), Gr. opucoco,
3
Att. opviTCD 'to dig, grub'.
The verb seems to be denominal, maybe to a noun or adj. *runk-o- which in its turn
was derived from a PIE nasal present. Lat. runcina 'a carpenter's plane' (Plin.) and
runcindre 'to plane (as a carpenter)' (Varro), deruncinare 'to plane off (PI.) probably
rest on the borrowing of Gr. puKavrj 'plane' influenced by runcare 'to grub up'.
BibL: WH II: 452, EM 582, IEW 868-871, Steinbauer 1989: 254, LIV *h reuk-.
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rufl, -ere 2 'to churn or plough up, dig out' [v. Ill; ppp. rutum] (Varro+)
Derivatives: rutus, -its 'digging, rushing' (Cato+), nitabulum 'long stick with a
flattened end' (Naev.+), mtellum 'small shovel' (Lucil.+), rutmm 'shovel' (Cato+);
arruere 'to heap up (earth)' (Varro+), dimere 'to pull down' (Ter.+), eruere 'to dig
up, tear out' (Cato+); ruta caesa 'minerals and timber already quarried and felled at
the time an estate is put up for sale' (Paul, ex F., Cic.+).
Pit. *rowe/o-.
PIE *(H)reuH-e/o-. IE cognates: Skt. aor. ravisam [ls.sb.act.], ravcit [3s.sb.act.] 'to
wound, hurt', ppp. mtaLith. rduti 'to tear out, pull', Lith. raviti 'to weed', Ru. ryi \
Is. roju 'to dig', SCr. riti 'dig', OCS rylo 'spade', Ru. rylo 'snout, mug', SeCS rtvciti,
ORu. rbvati, Ru. rvat' 'to tear', OIc. ryja 'to tear out wool'; ToB ru- 'to pull out
(from under a surface [with violence])', pr. V fruwa-f < PTo. *ruwa- < PIE
*ru(H)-eh -.
2
It seems that the derivatives of rud 'to rush' and rud 'to dig' have become mixed up: we
find short-vowel forms meaning 'dig', in spite of the PIE root etymology *(H)ruH-.
Bibl.: WH II: 453f., EM 582fi, IEW 868-871, Schrijver 1991: 234, Meiser 2003:
124, LIV *reuH-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: rus, ruris 'country, land' [n. r] (P1.+; loc.sg. run)
Derivatives: rusticus 'of a farm, rural' (Naev.+), riisticatim 'in a countrified fashion'
(Pompon.+), rurare/i 'to occupy oneself on one's country estate' (P1.+).
Pit. *rowos- [n.] 'open space, land', *rowes-tiko- [adj.].
PIE *Hreu-os [n.] 'open space, field'. IE cognates: Olr. roe 'flat field' < *rouesid-,
Av. rauuah- [n.] 'open space', rauuas-carat- 'moving freely'.
Nom.acc.sg. *rowos > *riios > rus; the -it- may also be regular in ruri if from dat.sg.
*rowesei. The u (or its antecedent, closed *o) then spread through the paradigm.
Bibl.: WH II: 454fi, EM 583, IEW 874, Schrijver 1991: 276, 280.
ruscus 'butcher's broom' [f. o] (Verg.+)
Derivatives: rusceus 'coloured like the berries of butcher's broom' (Cato+).

Pit. *sakro- 'sacred'; *sakraje- 'to dedicate, sacrifice'; *sakrasio- 'dedicatory';

content:

U. sacru [nom.sg.f.], O. octicopo [nom.sg.f. or nom.pi.n.], U. sacr [nom.sg.n.?],
sakra, sacra, sakref [acc.pi.f.], sacre [adv.] 'holy, sacred'; 2. O. sakrasias
[nom.pl.f.] 'sanctifying'; O. sakruvist, sakruvit [3s.pf.] 'has been sanctified'; O.
sakarater [3p.pr.ps.], sakraitir, sak<r>ahiter [3s.pr.sb.ps.], sakrafir [inf.pr.ps.],

*sakra-trix; O. oaKapaKi.5ipai [loc.sg.?] 'kind of priest?'; 3. O. sakrim [acc.sg.],
sakrid [abl.sg.], sakriss [abl.pl.] [m./f.], U. sakre [acc.sg.], sakreu [acc.pl.], sacris
[abl.pl.] [n.] 'sacrificial animal' < *sakri- [adj.]; 4. O. saahtum [nom.acc.sg.n.]
'sanctified, holy' < *sank-to-; U. sahta, sahatam, sahata [acc.sg.], satame [acc.sg. +

*sankta~; 5. U. sa?e, sa?i [dat.sg.], sa?e [voc.sg.] epithet of luppiter and of vestige <
*sak~; U. sansie, sansi, sansi [voc.sg.], sa^i, sansie, sansii, sansi [dat.sg.] 'of the god
Sancus' < *sankio~.
PIE *shbk-ro- 'sacred', *seh k-r-i-, *sh -n-k- 'to make sacred, sanctify'. IE cognates:
2 2
Hit. saklai- [c.] 'custom, rites' (< *seh k-loi-), zankild- / zankil- 'to fine, punish' (<
2
*sh nk-i + *l(o)hl)\ Celtib. sancilistara 'money-fine'?

According to Forssman 1992: 308, sacer is a vfddhi-formation to sacrum 'act of
sacrifice'; but it seems preferable to assume ablaut in the root *s(e)h k-, especially in
2
view of the cognate Hittite forms. The verb continues a nasal present *sank- with
/-suffix in the present; the pf. and ppp. were made from the pr. stem *sank-. The ppp.
underwent the Pit. development to *san/to- > *sa(n)yto-, after which Latin restored

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sacerdos, -otis 'priest' [m. (f.) /] (PI., SCBac.+; gen.pl. sacerdotum)
Derivatives: sacerdotula 'a minor priestess' (Varro).
Pit. *sakro-ddt- 'who has sacred endowments'.
PIE *-deh -t- 'gift'.
3
Earlier etymological works derive -dds from PIE *dhoht- 'who places/performs the
r
sacrifice*, e.g. WH, EM, Leumann 1977, Sihier 1995. But for such a meaning, the
morphology (o-grade in the second member of a governing compound) would be
strange. Strunk 1994 and Nussbaum (apparently independently) 1999 derive -dds
from the root *deh - 'to give', in which an e-grade can be reconstructed. Strunk
3
translates 'he who has the gift of the sacred', whereas Nussbaum posits 'having
contributions intended for the sacrifice', 'having sacred endowments'. Since Latin has
a stem dds, doth 'dowry', it seems most straightforward to interpret sacerdds as
sacer-dot- 'who has a sacred endowment'. But an analysis as an original governing
compound is not excluded. Nussbaum (p. 415, fii. 107) offers philological arguments
to show that the combination of *sakros plus *deh (u-) 'to give' was known in Italic;
r
compare Cato (exta et) vinum dato, and the fact that U. uses the verb purtuvitu
[3s.ipv.II] 'present' when the text speaks about offers which are being presented in
the ceremony.
Bibl.: WH II: 460, EM 586, IEW 235-239, Leumann 1977: 275, Strunk 1994, Sihier
1995: 49, 122, Nussbaum 1999a: 396-398, Lindner 2002: 44, LIV *deh -. -> do,
3
sacer

PIE *seh i-to- or *sh ei-to- 'cord'. IE cognates: Skt. setu- [m.] 'band, fetter, bridge',
2 2

'snare, trap', Cz. sit' 'net', OIc. seidr [rn.J, OHG seid 'cord' [n.].

sSgus 'prophetic, prescient' [adj. o/a] (Statius+)
Derivatives: saga 'witch, wise woman' (Lucil.+), sagTre 'to perceive' (Cic.+), sagax
'keen-scented, perceptive' (P1.+);praesagTre 'to foresee, portend' (P1.+).
Pit. *sag-dk- 'perceptive', *sag-ije/o- 'to seek, perceive'.
PIE *seh g-i- [m./f.] 'tracking'? IE cognates: Olr. saigid, •saig 'to try to reach' <
2
PCI. *sag-(i)e/o~, MW haedu 'to strive, earn', denom. to a PCI. noun *sag-iia [f.];
Hit. sagai- 'omen', sakiie/a-' 'to give a sign, reveal'; Gr. fryeopai 'to direct, lead',
Go. sokjan, MoE seek 'to search' < *seh g-, Go. sakan, pret. sok, OHG sahhan 'to
2
quarrel' < *sh g-.
2
The compound praesagTre is attested earlier than the simplex, which may well be a
back-formation to praesagTre. Since sagus also appears only in post-Republican
Latin, prae-sagire has no obvious derivational basis within Latin, and may reflect a
Pit. verb in *-je~. Kloekhorst 2008: 697 assumes that sakiie/a- is denominal to sagai-;
for the noun, he posits a PIE paradigm *seh g-oi-s, *sh2g-di-m, *sh2g-i-os with
2
generalization of the full-grade root in Hittite. This would mean that Latin sagtre and
Go. solgan cannot be directly compared to the Hit. verb. Instead, they may both be
denominal presents to a noun *seh g-(o)i-. This might also explain why the IE
2
cognates show an ablaut *seh g- / *sh g-.
2 2
Bibl.: WH II: 464f, EM 589, IEW 876f, Schrijver 1991: 97, 338, Schumacher
2004: 555-557, LIV "seh^-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sal, sails 'salt' [m. (n.) /] (P1.+; nom.acc.sg. sale Enn, Cato)
Derivatives: salTnum 'salt-cellar' (P1.+), salinator 'operator of a salt-works' (Cato+),
salillum 'little salt-cellar' (P1.+), saldrius 'of salt' (Varro+); sallere [ppp. salsum] 'to
salt' (Lucil.+), sallTre 'to salt' (Cato+), salsus 'salted, salty' (PL+), salsamentum
'salted food, esp. fish' (Ter.+), salsiira 'salting, pickling' (P1.+); salum 'sea' (Enn.+),
Salacia 'name of a sea-goddess' (Pac.+).
Pit. *sals, *salos 'salt', *sald-e/o- 'to salt', *sa!sso- 'salted'. It. cognates: U. 5alu
[acc.sg.] 'salt'. For a possible indirect attestation of O. *sal-, see s.v. purus.
PIE *s§h -l-s [nom.], *sh -el-m [acc.], *sh -l-os 'salt'; *sh l-d- (or *sh el-d- or
2 2 2 2 2
*seh l-d-) 'salt'. IE cognates: Olr. salann 'salt' [n.], W. halen; Gr. 61c, 'salt' [m.];
2
* V ? 1
Latv. sals 'salt' [f.], Lith. Zem. solymas [m.] 'saltpan' < nom.sg. *seh l-s, OPr. sal,
2
OCS solb, Ru. sol' 'salt' [f.] < *sh el-; Lith. saldus 'sweet', OCS sladbkb, Ru.
2
solodkij (dial.) 'sweet' < *sh el-d-u-, Go. salt, OHG safz 'salt' [n.].
2
The PIE ablaut was retained into Latin. The stem *sal-d-e/o- yielded sallere, and the
ppp. *saldto- regularly developed into *salso~. It is possible that salum is a loanword
from Greek; if inherited, it must be an inner-Italic derivative *sal-o- 'salty' > 'sea'.
Bibl.: WH II: 465f, 471, EM 589, 591, IEW 878f, Schrijver 1991: 98, 111, 114,
Meiser 1998: 55, Untermann 2000: 654f.
>
saliO, -Ire 'to jump, leap' [v. IV; pf. salui/salii, ppp. saltum] (PL+)
Derivatives: sdltus, -us 'jump, leap; narrow passage, woodland' (Naev.+); absilire
'to rush away' (Lucr.+), desiltre 'to jump down, dismount' (P1.+), dissilTre 'to burst
apart' (Lucr.+), insitire 'to jump on' (P1.+), prositire 'to rush forth, gush' (P1.+),
resilTre 'to jump back, recoil' (Lucr.+), subsilire 'to jump up' (P1.+), trdnsilTre 'to
leap over, skip' (P1.+); saltare 'to dance' (P1.+), saltatio 'dancing' (P1.+), desultura
'jump...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: saliva 'salive, spittle' [f. a\ (Varro+)
Derivatives: salebra 'unevenness' (Cic.+).
Pit. * sal-Two-, *sal-es-ro- 'dirty yellow'.
PIE *slH-V- 'dirty yellow'. IE cognates: Ir. sal [f.j 'dirt', salach, W. halawg 'dirty'
< *sal- < PIE *slH-V-\ OPr. salowis, Ru. solovej, SCr. slavuj 'nightingale' < *sal-u-
(< *soIH-uo- 'grey'?); Ru. solovyj 'light bay', solovoj 'yellowish grey', CS slavoocije
'state of having green eyes, glaucitas' [n.]; OHG salo, gen. salwes 'dirt', OIc. sglr
'dirty yellow' < PIE *solH-u~.
If salTva is indeed cognate with BS1. and Gm. *solH-u- 'grey', it may be built on an
earlier adj. *saluus, compare Lat. -Tvus in irrigivus to irriguus, vocTvus to vacuus.
Bibl.: WH II: 468f., EM 590, IEW 879, Schrijver 1991: 212f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: salix, -icis 'willow, osier' [f. lc\ (PI.+)
Derivatives: salictum 'group of willows, osier-bed' (P1.+), salictarius 'of osier-beds'
(Cato+), salignus 'made of willow-wood' (Cato+), saligneus 'of willows' (Cato+).
Pit. *salik-.
IE cognates: Olr. sail, gen. sailech, W. helyg 'willow' < *salik~, Myc. e-ri-ka, Gr.
Arc. eXiicri 'willow' (Boeot. peXiKtQv does not certainly contain the same word); OHG
salaha, OIc. selja < *salkion-, OE sealh 'willow' < *salko~.
Italo-Celtic *salik- may reflect PIE *slH-ik- or *sh l-ik~, but Gm. would require
2
*sh l-k~. The suffix interchange *-ik- vs. *-k- is hardly IE; in combination with the
2
restricted distribution, the possible appurtenance of Greek *helika- with -e- in the first
syllable (which cannot go back to *sh l-) and the fact that it is a tree name, this
2
suggests non-IE origin.
Bibl.: WH II: 469, EM 590f., IEW 879, Schrijver 1991: 103f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: greet'; O. salavs, oaXapc;, Marr. salaus, salas [nom.sg.m.], U. saluom, saluuom, saluo
[acc.sg.m.n.], saluam, salua [acc.sg.f.], salua, saluua [acc.pl.fi] 'whole, healthy';
Pael. praenomen saluta < *saliit-; Pael. salauatur [nom.sg.] a certain official <
*salwa-tor- (not borrowed from Latin).
PIE *slH-u- 'whole'. IE cognates: Hit. salli- 'big, main' < *solH-i- (Kloekhorst
2008: 709ffi), Skt. sdrva- 'whole, all', YAv. kauruua- 'undamaged, whole', OP
haruva- 'whole, all together', Gr. oXoq, Ion., H. otiXoq 'whole, complete', Alb. gjalle
'alive', ToA salu [adv.] 'wholly, totally'.
Salus and salubris seem to presuppose a verb *saluere, ptc. *saliitos, which in turn
would be regular to a w-stem noun or adj. *salu-. Yet Lat. salvus has cognates in other
IE languages which point to *sol(H)uo-. Hence, Schrijver 1991 assumes that Pit. had
*salu- and *solwo~, and that the latter adopted the -a- from the former, yielding
*salwo-. Yet *salwo- would probably yield Latin *salio-, in spite of Schrijver's
efforts to render this uncertain. If Pre-Latin *saluwo- is related to Lat. sollus (cf.
Nussbaum 1997), it may be due to an inner-Italic thematization of the adj. *salu-.
This thematization must post-date the change of *-lw- > *-//-. Both Pit. *salu- and
Ilr.Gr. * so I wo- can be interpreted as deriving from a n. noun *solH-u- / *slH-u-
'wholeness', with an ins.sg. *slH-u-hy from which salu-t- and *salu-pli- were derived
(Pike 2007). *
Bibl.: WH II: ^71fi, EM 591fi, IEW 979f, Giacomelli 1963: 255, Leumann 1977:
349, 554, Schrijver 1991: 295-297, Nussbaum 1997: 186fi, 1999: 386, 410,
Untermann 2000: 651-654. —»solidus, sollus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ishan- 'blood; bloodshed' [n.] (< *h]esh r, *hjesh enos), CLuw. ashar- 'blood', Skt.
2 2
asrj- (asrk nom.sg., asnas gen.sg.), Gr. eap, Latv. as ins, ToA ysar, ToB yasar 'id.'.
It is disputed whether sanguen is the oldest form, or whether *sanguin-s was remade
into sanguen on the example of unguen, -inis 'fat, grease'; the expected outcome of
*-en~s would be *-es. The reconstruction PIE *h]Sh -n-h]gw-o/i- 'shining like blood'
2
by Balles 1999 is too far-fetched. The element san- may well go back to PIE
*hish -(e)n-, the oblique form of the word for 'blood'. The element -guen or -guin is
2
less certain. According to Kloekhorst 2008: 256-260, Hittite shows that the gen.sg.
originally ended in *-en-s, which wotfld be in accordance with the reconstruction
*hish -en- for sanguis.
2
Bibl.: WH II: 474f, EM 593, Sihler 1995: 300, Balles 1999. assyr, sanies

Derivatives: sdnitas 'health, good sense' (P1.+), sandre 'to cure' (Caecil.+); Insanus
'mad' (P1.+), Insanire 'to be mad, rave' (P1.+), Insania 'madness' (P1.+).
Pit. *sano~. It. cognates: maybe U. sanes [abl.pl.f.?] '?', attribute of certain

PIE *seh -no-. IE cognates: Skt. ava-satar- [m.] 'liberator', ava-sana-, OAv.
2
auuaqhana- [n.] 'resting-place' (^'unharnessing') < *seh - 'to tie'.

The reconstruction *seh -no- is purely mechanical. Sanus might be derived from the
2
root *seh - 'to tie', meaning 'which is in place, in order', or maybe from the root
2
*sh - 'to satisfy' seen in satis 'enough', Lith. sotus 'satisfied'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sap a 'new wine boiled down to a proportion of its original volume' [f. a] (Cato+)
IE cognates: Skt. sabarduh- [epithet, esp. of a dairy-cow], OAv. Mbuuant- [adj.]
'juicy?' < *sab-uant-, Arm. ham, OIc. safe, OHG saf1 sap' < *sap- (OE seep, MoDu.
sap < Lat. sapa) .
It seems unlikely that sapa is derived from sapid 'to taste, know', as advocated by
IEW and EM: the Gm. and Arm. cognates also mean 'sap'. If the Ilr. forms belong
here too, there is an interchange between *sap- and *sab- pointing to a loanword. If
Ilr. is excluded, we have *sap- (Latin, Arm.?) against *sab- (Germ.) which still points
to a non-IE loanword.
Bibl.: WH II: 476, EM 594, IEW 880, Schrijver 1991: 104.
sapiO, -ere 'to taste, be intelligent, know' [v. Ill; pf. sapi(v)i(» sapuT)] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: sapor 'flavour, taste' (Varro+); desipere 'to lose one's reason' (P1.+),
Tnsipientia 'folly' (P1.+), insipiens 'unwise, foolish' (P1.+), resipere 'to recall the
flavour of (Varro+), resipiscere 'to regain consciousness, come to one's senses
(P1.+).
Pit. *sap-i-; pf. *sep~? It. cognates: O. sipus [nom.sg.m.], Vol. sepu [abl.sg.]
'knowing' < ptc.pf.act. *sep-wos < *seh,p- to sapere, or with analogical e-grade in
the perfect. Nussbaum (p.c.) suggests that the preform may have been an adj.
*sep-uwo~.
PIE *sh p-i- 'to notice'. IE cognates: OS an-sebbian 'to remark, notice', OHG
1
int-seffen 'to remark, taste', OIc. sefi 'thought' < *saf-i- < PGm. *saf~, *sab-.
Root-internal *hj is uncertain: it hinges on Sabell. *sep~, but this may be analogical.
In that case, the root must be left at *sHp~. Latin pf. sapTvT and Sabell. *sep- might
(very speculatively) be interpreted as proof for earlier ablaut *sehp-i- / *ship-ei~.
!
Bibl.: WH II: 477, EM 594, IEW 880, Schrijver 1991: 93f, Untermann 2000: 677,
Meiser 2003: 244, LIV *seh,p-. sibus
sarcio, -Ire 'to mend, repair' [v. IV; pf. sarsi, ppp. sartum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: exsarclre 'to patch up' (Ter.+), resarcire 'to mend, restore' (Cato...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: satis, sat 'enough, sufficient' [adv.] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: satias, -atis 'abundance, repleteness' (P1.+), satietas 'satiety,
abundance' (P1.+), satiare 'to satisfy, content' (Lucr.+), insatietas 'greediness' (PL),
Tnsatiabilis 'insatiable' (Lucr.+); satisfacere 'to give satisfaction' (P1.+); satur [adj. o]
'well-fed' (PL+), saturitas 'fullness, satiety' (P1.+), saturare 'to fill, satisfy' (Varro+),
satullus 'replete' (Varro+), satullare 'to satiate' (Varro+).
Pit. *sati- 'satiation, satisfaction', *satu-ro- 'well-fed'.
PIE *sh -ti- 'satisfaction, satiation', *s(e)h -tu- 'satiation'. IE cognates: Olr. sdith
2 2
'fullness' < * satis; Skt. asinvd- 'without chewing, without biting, devouring
(greedily)' < *n-sh -nu-o- 'unsalable' (?); Gr. aSriv [adv.] 'to one's fill', &5o<; [m./n.]
2
'satiety' < *sdd-, fipevat (II.), aor. Scat, aoacn3ai (ep.) 'to satiate oneself, Arm.
at-oK 'full, full-grown', Lith. sotus 'satisfied, full', Latv. sats 'satisfied, copious',
OCS sytb, Ru. sytyj, SCr. sit 'satisfied' (the origin of SL *y is obscure), Go. saps
'satisfied, full' < *sh -to-.
2
WH interpret satis as a petrified nom.sg. of a noun *sati- 'satisfaction', while sat
would hail from anteconsonantal * satis > *sati1 > sat. One might also invoke potest <
*potis est, implying a development (with reanalysis) * satis est > satis V >> sat est. In
this way, we need to posit only one pre-Latin construction * satis esse. Meiser
assumes that satis reflects a comparative *sat-is 'more satisfied', but there is no
indication to be found in the meaning. The adj. *saturo- 'well-fed' may contain the
same «-stem as Lith. so tus, although this type of adj. is productive in Lithuanian. The
Latin form may rather be a ra-adj. derived from a u-stem noun.
Bibl.: WH II: 481fi, EM 596, IEW 876, Schrijver 1991: 98, Meiser 1998: 155, 195,
LIV l.*seh (i)-. —»sanus
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: saucius 'wounded' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: sauciare 'to wound, cut into' (P1-+).
Pit. *(k)sawo- 'wound', *(k)saweje/o~ 'to wound'. It. cognates: possibly U. sauitu
[3s.ipv.II], a damaging act in a curse (Vine 2004).
PIE *ksou-o- 'scraping', *ksou-eie/o- 'to scrape'. IE cognates: Skt. ksura- [m.] 'razor,
sharp knife', Gr, ^ilco 'to plane, scratch', ^upov, ^upoq 'razor', Lith. skitsti 'to plane'.
Vine 2004: 624 compares U. sauitu, and posits and adj. *ksou-o- 'scraping', whence a
derivative *ksou-Vk- 'a scrape, wound' would be derived. With unrounding of *ow >
*aw-in open syllable, and addition of *-io-, this would yield *sawVkio- > saucius.
- - 5.,
Vine 2006a: 237 argues that the unrounding would have applied in pretonic syllable,
hence the preform was *ksou-o-.
Bibl.: WH II: 483, EM 597, IEW 585f„ LIV *kseu-. novdcula

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: saxum 'rock, boulder' [n. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: saxeus 'of stones and rock' (Andr.+), saxatilis 'living among rocks' (P1.+).
Pit. *saks-o~.
IE cognates: OIc. sax, OHG sahs 'knife'?
Pit. *saks- cannot go back to the root *sek- of secare, since -a- would remain
unexplained. Saxum can only be cognate with the Gm. words for 'knife' if these
reflect a root *sh k~. However, the Gm. words can also reflect *sok~s-, and there is no
2
other evidence for a root *sh k- 'to cut'. Since the semantic connection between
2
'rock' and 'knife' is also not evident, saxum has no certain etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 484, EM 597, IEW 895f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scabo, -ere 'to scratch' [v. Ill; pf. scabT\ (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: scabies 'roughness of the skin' (Cato+), scaber [o/a] 'rough, scurfy'
(P1.+), scabrere 'to be rough' (Enn.), scabres [f.sg.] 'roughness' (Pac.+); scobis [f.]
'sawdust, peelings' (Varro+), scobina 'tool for scraping, rasp' (Varro+), descobinare
'to scrape off (Varro+). ''
Pit. *skaf-e/o- 'to scratch', *skafro- 'rough', *skof-i- 'sawdust, what is scratched off.
PIE *skbh-ro- [v.] 'to scratch, plane', *skobh-i-. IE cognates: Lith. skobti, Is. skabiii
'to plane', Go. skaban 'to shave', OHG scaban 'to plane'.
There exists agreement on the secondary character of pf. scab-, which was made by
lengthening the vowel of the present, following the same pattern in other verbs.
Meiser 2003 assumes that scab- replaces an earlier perfect *skeb~ < pf.pl. *ske-skbh-,
but it is not certain that this verb originally had a pf. at all. Cognate forms are PGm.
*skabanan 'to plane' and Lith. skobti, which in view of their meaning and the sixth
class of Gm. *skabanan may well continue an iterative stem *skobh~. LIV adds Gr,
emTmfl 'to dig (but)', cncdcprj 'trough, basin', but I agree with Schrijver that these are
semantically mofe remote from 'to plane', so their appurtenance is uncertain. For
scabere, Schrijver posits *skeb- > *skab- by a rule *e> a after pure velar, but this has
rightly been contradicted by Meiser, who points to the many exceptions which this
rule would have. Latin scab- may have originated in the adj. *skbro- 'rough', with
insertion of -a- between two consonant clusters (cf. Kortlandt 1989: 104).
Bibl.: WH II: 484f., EM 597, IEW 930-933, Schrijver 1991: 431, Meiser 1998: 82f.,
2003: 156, LIV *skabh-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scaevus 'left, inauspicious' [adj. o/a] (Vitr.+)
Derivatives: scaeva 'omen; left-hand side' (P1.+), scaevola 'phallus-like charm'
(Varro); obscaevdre 'to constitute a good or bad omen' (PI.).
Pit. *skaiwo
PIE *skeh -i-uo- 'shaded; left'. IE cognates: Gr. cncaio^ 'left' < *skaiuos; Skt.
2
chaya- 'shadow, reflection', YAv. a-saiia- [adj.] 'that does not cast a shadow', Gr.
oKict [f.] 'shadow', Gr. cncrivfj, Dor. cncdva 'tent, booth, stage', Alb. hije 'shadow',
Latv. seja, dial, seya 'face'; OCS senb, SCr. sjen 'shadow', ORu. stem 'shadow,
vision' [f.]; ToB skiyo [f.] 'shadow'; Go. skeinan 'to shine', OCS sijati 'to shine' <
*skiH-.
There are a few rhyming formations meaning 'left, sinister' in the IE languages; Latin
has laevus and scaevns. As per Steinbauer 1996 (unpublished presentation, cited in
Janda 2000: 118 and Stiiber 2006: 68), the root could be identified as PIE *s£eh -(i-)
2
'to shine', from which we find derived words for 'shade'. A derived adj. 'shaded'
may have come to mean 'western' but also 'the shaded hand, the improper hand = left
hand'.
Bibl.: WH II: 485, EM 597f, Schrijver 1991: 270, Beekes 1994: 89, LIV *s£eH(i)-.
laevus, obscaenus, obscurus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scalpo, -ere 'to scratch, carve' [v. Ill; pf. scalpsT, ppp. scalptum] (Sis.+)
Derivatives: scalpurrTre 'to keep scratching' (PI.), scalprum 'tool for scraping'
(Cato+), scalptura 'art of carving' (Carmen Arvale+), sculponeae [f.pl.J 'wooden
shoes, clogs' (P1.+); exsculpere 'to care out, dig out' (P1.+).
Pit. *skaip-l
PIE *sk(e)lp- 'to split off? IE cognates: Lith. sklembti 'slide off, slide down'?;
OHG scelifa 'outer skin', MLG schelver 'peeled off part', schulvern 'to flake off;
OE scielfe 'floor, planks', scielf[m.] 'top rock' < PGm. *skelf- 'to split (off)'.
Latin scalp- might be cognate with Lith. *sklemb- and PGm. *skelf~; Latin could have
a secondary full grade scalp- from *sklp-, or phonetic *sklp- > *skalp- in
preconsonantal position.
Bibl.: WH II: 486f, EM 598, IEW 923-927, Schrijver 1991: 431.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scando, -ere 'to ascend, mount' [v. Ill; pf. scandi, ppp. scansum] (Cato+)
Derivatives: scalae [f.pl.] 'ladder, stairways' (P1.+), scansio 'climbing' (Varro+);
ascendere 'to go up, rise, attain' (Andr.+), ascensid 'ascent' (P1.+), conscendere 'to
go on board, mount, climb to' (P1.+), descendere 'to go down, descend' (P' +X
escendere 'to go up, mount' (P1.+), Tnscendere 'to climb up, mount' (P1.+), Tnscensio
543
'embarking' (PI.), transcendere 'to go beyond, cross' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *skand-e/o- 'to ascend'. It. cognates: maybe O. esskazsium [acc.sg.m.] <
*eks-skand-teio- 'of ascending'?
PIE aor. *skend-/*sknd- 'to jump up, ascend', pr. *sknd-e/o-? IE cognates: Olr.
sceinnid 'to jump', MW ysgeinnyaw 'to splash, strew' < PCI. *skan-n-de/o- W.
t
cy-chwynrm 'to arise, start' < *kom-skendo- Skt. pr. skandati, pf. caskanda, aor. skan
>
'to leap, spring', skanna- 'squirted out, leapt away' < *skend- (with restored sk-).
Lat. scala < *skand-sla~. If Schumacher is right in reconstructing *skand- for Celtic,
we have an Italo-Celtic pr. *skand- vs. Skt. aor. *skend- and a pr. *skend-e/o-. But the
sequence sk- must have been restored in Skt. from either pr. or aor., since the pf. is
judged to be secondary, cf. Kummel 2000: 572. The trigger to restore sk- must have
been a zero-grade form in the aor. (*sknd-) or in the present (*sknd-e/o~), or an
o-grade iterative. Since Italo-Celtic has a present *skand-, it seems more likely that it
was the present which has *sknd-; the -a- could then be explained from vocalization
of *sknd- in preconsonantal position.
Bibl.: WH II: 488, EM 599, Schrijver 1991: 43If., Untermann 2000: 235, Meiser
2003: 211, Schumacher 2004: 574f„ LIV l.*skend-.

A semantic connection between "shoulder-blade' and 'shovel, spade' is quite natural,
due to the form of a shoulder-blade. LIV derives scapiilae from a root *(s)kep- from
which Gr. KOTITCO 'to hit', Slav, kopati 'to dig' are derived, but Latin -a- cannot be
explained from such a root. Scapulae may belong to Gr. OKCLTCTCO 'to dig (out), work

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: the earth', cKcwpr] [f.] 'winnow, bowl, trough, dish', also 'ship', but this root probably
has final *-bh~, as the majority of derivatives within Greek indicate. Beekes (fthc.)
assumes that orKacp-/cncaju- are of European substratum provenance, in which case
scapulae may also belong to such a non-IE root *skaP-.
Bibl.: WH II: 489f., EM 600, IEW 930-933, Untermann 2000: 684f., LIV l.*(s)kep-.
scatO, -ere 'to gush forth, swarm' [v. Ill] (PI.+; also scatere P1.+)
Derivatives: scatebra 'gush of water, spring' (Acc.+), scaturrex, -icis 'gushing
stream' (Varro).
Pit. *skat-
PIE *skt-. IE cognates: Lith. skasti, Is. skantii 'to jump'.
For the semantic link between 'to jump' and 'gush forth', cf. English spring. Schrijver
1991 hestitantly considers original *sket-, but admits that there is no evidence for
e-grade. LIV proposes a root *skHt-, which would in theory yield *skat- in Baltic and
Italic. I think that scat- could also reflect secondary vocalization of the sequence
*skt~, compare pat- < *pt- (s.v. panda).
Bibl.: WH II: 490, EM 600, IEW 950, Schrijver 1991:432, LIV ?*skeHt-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scelus, -eris 'curse, crime' [n. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: scelestus 'doom-laden, wicked' (P1-+), scelerosus 'wicked' (Ter.+),
scelerdius 'accursed' (P1-+).
Pit. *skelos [n.].
PIE *skel-os, -es- [n.] 'curve, bending*. IE cognates: Gr. CTKEXOC [;n.] 'thigh, leg',
CTKOXIO^ 'crooked, bent, twisted, unjust', GKEXkbq 'bow-legged' (Hsch., EM), OHG
scelah, OE sceolh 'oblique, curved, squinting', NHG scheel, OIc. slg'algr 'oblique,
squinting' < PGm. *skel-ha~, -gd- < IE *skel-ko~.
Presupposes a semantic shift 'crooked' > 'wicked, bad', for which see Stiiber. Since
Skt. skhalate 'to stumble, trip' belongs to Gr. otpaXXopai from PIE *skwh el-, there is
2
no need to reconstruct *skh el- with Schrijver 1991. According to Nussbaum 1999a:
}
390, *-el- > *ol- did not take place because the initial cluster sk- fronted the -k-.
Bibl.: WH II: 492, EM 601, IEW 928, Schrijver 1991:433, Stiiber 2002: 183.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scindo, -ere 'to split, cleave' [v. Ill; pf. scicidi, ppp. scissum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: conscindere 'to tear to pieces' (Naev.+), disscindere 'to cut apart'
(Cato+), discidium 'discord, separation' (P1.+), excidium 'military destruction' (P1.+),
excissatus 'cut off (PI.), perscindere 'to cleave through' (Lucr.+), proscindere 'to
plough, slit' (P1.+), rescirtdere 'to cancel, remove by hewing' (Ter.+); scindula (var.
scartdula) 'wooden slat, shingle' (A. Hirtius+).
Pit. pr. *ski-n-d-e/o-, aor. *skeid-» pf. *ski-skid~, ppp. *skisso
PIE *ski-n-d- [pr.] 'to split, tear off, *skid-to- [ppp.]. IE cognates: Olr. sceid, -see*
'to vomit, spit' < PCI. *skei-e/o~; Skt. pr. chindhi [2s.ipv.act.], chinatti [3s.act.], aor.
chedma [lp.inj.act.], pf. cichiduh [3p.act.], cicheda [3s.act.], ta-ptc, chinna- 'to break,
split up', YAv. auua.hisiSiia- 'to split in two', a-sista- 'unsplit, unharmed', Gr. ox^w
'to split, cut through' (with unclear -%-), Arm. c'tim (< *c 'itAm) 'to tear, scratch',
Lith. skiesti, Is. skiedziu 'to separate, divide', OCS cediti 'to strain' < BS1. *(s)koi?d\
OIc. skita 'to shit'.
The absence of Lachmann's Law in the ppp. is ascribed either to the short vowel in
the pf. (Meiser, Sihler), to neutralization of the glottal component of *-d- in the nasal
present, which then analogically spread to the other verb forms (Kortlandt 1999,
Schrijver) or to general shortness of high vowels (Jasanoff 2004). On justifying the
reading excissatus rather than exclsatus in PI., Cist. 383, see Kiimmel 2004b: 355.
Bibl.: WH II: 488f., 493f., EM 599, 602, IEW 919-922, Leumann 1977: 294,
Schrijver 1991: 500, Sihler 1995: 76, Meiser 1998: 210, 2003: 189, Schumacher
2004: 578, LIV *skheid-. scutum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scio, scire 'to know' [v. IV; pf. sci(v)i, ppp. scitum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: scitari 'to inquire about' (P1.+), scitulus 'nice-looking' (P1.+), scilicet
[v./adv.] 'surely, naturally' (P1.+), scitamenta, -drum 'delicacies, refinements' (P1.+),
scius 'cognizant, skilled' (Pac.+), scientia 'knowledge' (Varro+); sciscere (sclvi,
scltum) 'to get to know, approve' (P1.+), sciscitari 'to try to get to know, inquire'
(P1.+); nescius 'unknown, not knowing' (PI.+), nesclre 'not to know; some-'
(Naev.+); conscius 'sharing knowledge, conscious' (P1.+), insciens 'not knowing,
stupid' (PI.+), inscientia 'ignorance' (Caecil.+), inscitus 'ignorant' (P1.+), Inscitia
'ignorance' (P1.+), inscius 'ignorant, unaware' (Varro+); consciscere 'to decree for
oneself, ordain' (P1.+), praesciscere 'to get to know beforehand' (Ter.+), res cis cere
'to get to know of (Naev.+).
Pit. *skije/o- 'to know'.
PIE *skhi(e/o)- 'to incise',. IE cognates: Skt. °chyati [3s.act.] 'to cut open, skin',
r
dva-cchita- 'pulled off (skin)', Bal. sayag 'to shave', Gr. oxaw 'to make an incision,
slit open'. • f
Scio is probably a back-formation on the basis of nescire. If 'to know' developed
from 'to distinguish, make a decision', -scire can belong to a root meaning 'to cut off,
incise'. LIV derives -scio from *sekH- (as in secare) which, in view of its meaning,
may well be the same root as the one given by LIV as *skheh (i)- 'to cut', which is
2
more likely *skh ~. Thus, the ja-present of Skt. -chyati and Lat. -scio may both go
2
back to the same PIE present. LIV (following Rix 1999) assumes that -scire arose in a
compound because a simplex *sekH-ie/o- would yield pre-Latin *sekaje/o-. But if the
suffix was athematic, *skH-i- would yield *-sci-. On the basis of the Hittite paradigm,
Kloekhorst 2008: 695f. argues that the final laryngeal was probably *h|.
Bibl.: WH II: 495, EM 602f, IEW 919-922, Rix 1999: 526, LIV *sekH-, *skheh (i)-.
2
—»seed

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scipio 'stick' [m. «] (PI.+)
Pit. *skeip-1
PIE *skeip-o- 'pole, stick'. IE cognates: Gr. OKIJICOV 'staff, stick', oKoTn;OQ probably
'supporting beam on which the tiles rest' (Hsch.).
The appurtenance of Skt. sepa- 'penis' is a wild guess. Latin cippus and scipio may
belong to the Greek words cited, but the family is isolated, and may well go back to
loanwords.
Bibl.: WH II: 496, EM 603, IEW 543. — cippus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scirpus 'bulrush or similar plant' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: scirpeus 'woven from bulrushes' (P1.+), scirpiculus [adj. / m.] 'of/for
bulrushes; a basket from bulrushes' (P1.+).
Pit. *skerpo-7
No etymology. Formally, it might go back to *skerp- (cf. firmus and inscr. Mirgurios
stircus), in which case it can be derived from the root *skerp- 'to pluck, cut off as in
carpo. This leaves the semantics to be explained: *skerp-o- might mean 'what is
picked' or 'to be picked', but I see no compelling connection with the bulrush.
Bibl.: WH II: 496, EM 603. carpd
scOpae 'branches of a plant, broom' [f.pl. a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: scopio 'stalk (of a plant)' (Cato+), scopus 'stalk' (Varro), scapus 'stem
stalk, shaft' (Varro+).
Pit. *skop-a~.
IE cognates: Gr. CK&noq 'stick' (Hsch.), cyKqKropai 'to support oneself < *skap~;
Latv. skeps 'spear, javelin', CS stapt 'stick' < *skep~?; OHG skaft [m.], OWNo.
skapt [n.] 'shaft'.
Latin scapus might be a borrowing from Gr. GKa7toq, but scop- must be older, and
both could go back to ablaut within a PIE root *sk(e/o)h p-. Yet a verbal root of this
2
form is not attested, and the words might also be (independent?) borrowings.
Bibl.: WH II: 490,497, EM 600,604, IEW 930-933.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scortum 'skin, hide; prostitute' [n. o] (P1.+; 'skin, hide' only in Varro)
Derivatives: scortari 'to consort with prostitutes' (P1.+), scortator 'who consorts
with prostitutes' (Pl>), scortes 'goat-head skins' (Paul, ex F.), scorteus 'made of
hide' (Varro+).
Pit. *skort-o-.
PIE *sk(o)rt-o- 'cutting'. IE cognates: see s.v. cortex.
The word for 'skin' was used as a euphemism for 'prostitute'. Since no verbal forms
of *krt- or *skrt- remain within Latin, the cooccurrence of nominal derivations of
both PIE root variants (cena, cortex from *krt-, scortum from *skrt-) is striking.
Bibl.: WH II: 497, EM 604, IEW 938ff„ LIV *(s)kert-. cena, corium, cortex

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scrlbo, -ere 'to write' [v. Ill; scrTpsT, scriptum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: scriba 'writer, secretary' (P1-+), scriptura 'writing, text, tax' (P1.+),
scrTpturarius 'of a certain tax' (Lucil.+), scrTptor 'writer' (Lucil.+); a(d)scribere 'to
add in writing, reckon' (P1.+), ascriptTvus 'enrolled in addition' (P1.+), conscrTbere 'to
enroll, compose' (P1.+), conscrtbiUare 'to cover with scribbling' (Varro+), describere
'to draw, mark, describe' (P1.+), discribere 'to allot, separate' (Ter.+), exscnbere 'to
write out, transcribe' (P1.+), TnscrTbere 'to inscribe, record' (P1.+), praescribere 'to
lay down, prescribe' (Ter.+), subscripere 'to append, sign, write under' (P1.+)-
Pit. *skreif-e/o- 'to carve', *skripto- 'carved'. It. cognates: O. scriftas [ppp-,
nom.pl.f.], U. screhto [nom.sg.n.], screihtor [nom.pl.n.] 'written' < *skrib to-',
possibly PalU. skerfs '?' [nom.sg.] < *skribh-i-.
PIE *skreibh-e/o- Ho carve', *skribh-to-? IE cognates: Latv. skrlpat 'to scratch,
scribble, write down'; OIc. hrifa 'scratch, tear' < *krTp/bh-. Unclear: MIr. scrip(a)id
'scratches', Gr. oKtxpwpoq 'outline, sketch', cncapTcpdopai 'to tear up the surface of a
body, scratch' (depending on whether -a- is a late anaptyctlc vowel, or old).
The ppp. has adopted the full grade *ei from the pf. and pr.
Bibl.: WH II: 499, EM 604fi, IEW 938-947, Schrijver 1991: 244, Untermann 2000:
685fi, Meiser 2003: 119, LIV ?*s(k)reibh~.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scrobis 'hole in the ground, pit' [m., f. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: scrobiculus 'small planting-hole' (Varro+),
Pit. *skrof-i-.
PIE *skrobh-i- 'hollow, pit'.* IE cognates: Lith. skrebhi, Is. skrebu 'to rattle,
crunch', Ru. skresti, Is. skrebu 'to scratch, plane'; Latv. skrabt 'to scratch, plane', Po.
skrobac; OE screpan 'schaben, kratzen'. j
BS1. points to a root in *-bh-, with which Gm. only partly agrees; but this may be due
to secondary developments of the Gm. stops.
Bibl.: WH II: 500, EM 605, IEW 938-947, LIV *(s)kreb-. scrdfa
scrOfa 'sow' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: scrbfipascus 'that feeds sows' (PI.).
Pit. *skrof-a- 'scratcher, digger'.
PIE *skrobh-h ;. IE cognates: see s.v. scrobis.
2j
WH and IEW's suggestion that scrofa was borrowed from Gr. ypopcpag, -aSo<; 'old
sow' (Hsch.) cannot be accepted: it does not explain the Latin form, and there is no
guarantee that the word existed early enough in Greek. It seems much more likely that
scrdfa is a non-Roman dialect form from *skrof-a- 'scratcher, digger', since pigs are
often denominated on account of the rummaging way they search for food; see s.v.
porcus. If scrofa is a borrowing, we cannot be sure about its original vowel: it might
have been *o, which the Romans borrowed as d. A preform with short *o would be
more in line with PIE morphology.
Bibl.: WH II: 500f., EM 605. -»porcus, scrobis

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scrflta,-orum 'discarded goods, junk' [n.pl. o] (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: scrutillus 'kind of sausage' (P1.+), scrutarius 'second-hand dealer'
(Lucil.+), scrutarl 'to examine, investigate' (P1.+); scrautum 'skin used for storing
arrows, quiver' (Paul. exF.), scrotum 'the scrotum' (Cels.).
Pit. *skrout-7
IE cognates: Go. and-hruskan 'to research' < *krut-sk-; OHG scrdtan 'to hew, cut;
cut clothes', scrot 'cutting', OE screadian 'to peel, cut off, skrud [n.] 'garment', OIc.
skrud [n.] 'valuables, garment', OHG scruton, scrodon, scrutilon 'to investigate'.
Certainly not borrowed from Gr. ypuxr] [f.] 'woman's dressing-case, tool-bag,
frippery' as assumed by WH. In theory, a preform *skrouto~ may have developed to
scriit- in Rome and to scrot- in Latium; scraut- would then be a hypercorrect form for
scrot-. It is not certain, however, that scrautum and scrotum semantically belong to
scrut- 'discarded goods, trifles'. The further etymology is uncertain: only Germanic
contains words matching in form and possibly in meaning.
Bibl.: WH II: 498, 502, EM 604,606, IEW 938-947, Schrijver 1991: 265f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scutum 'shield' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: scutarius 'maker of shields' (P1.+).
Pit. *skoito- [n.] 'shield'.
PIE *skoi-to- 'piece of wood, sheath, shield'. IE cognates: Olr. sciaih (gen. sceith) ,
W. ysgwyd, OBret. scuit, scoet, Bret, scoed 'shield' < PCI. *skeito- [n.]; OPr. staytan
[scaytan] 'shield', Lith. skietas, Latv. skiets 'reed (in a loom)' < *skoi-to-; OCS stitb,
Ru. scit, gen. scita [m.] 'shield', Po. szczyt 'top, summit' < *skeito~, OE skmd, scead,
OHG sceida 'sheath', OIc. skeid f. 'loom', pi. 'sheath' < PGm. *skaid-i-; OIc. skid
jn.] 'billet, snow-shoe, ski', OE scid, OHG scit 'stick, billet', OIc. skidi [n.] 'sheath' <
PGm. *skeid-.
The ablaut *skeito- / *skoito- within BS1. and within Italo-Celtic is striking, and
points to productive derivatives from verbal forms (verb *skeit~, noun *skoito-7). The
root *ski- might be an unenlarged variant of the root *skid- 'to split' found in scindo.
Bibl.: WH II: 503, EM 607, IEW 919-922, Schrijver 1995: 194. scindo
sE '(him, her, it, them)selfves' [pron. reflexive] (Lex XII, Andr.+): acc.abl.sg. se
(OLat. inscr. sed, PI. sed), gen.sg. sui, dat.sg. sibi (inscr. sibei)
Derivatives: suus [adj.poss.] 'his, her, its, their' (OLat. inscr. soueis 'suTs').
Pit. acc, *s(w)e (stressed), *se (unstressed), dat. *sefei, abl. *sed; poss. *sowo~. It.
cognates: O. siom [acc.], sifei, Pael. sef<e>i, U. seso [dat.] 'him-, herself < acc.
*se-om, dat. *sebhei, seso maybe < *soi + (e)psom/d; O. suvefs [gen.sg.m.], suvad,
suvad [abl.sg.f.], suvam, suva [acc.sg.f.], ooupev [loc.sg.n. + ~eri\ , U. svesu, sueso
[loc.sg.n./f.], Marr. suam, Pael. sua [acc.sg.f.], suois [dat.pl.rn.], SPic. suais [abl.pl.f.],
suhuh [abl.sg.m.] 'his, her' < *swo- (U.), *sowo- (O, SPic.); U. surur, suror 'just so'
< *swo-j>vos, suront, sururont, sururo 'just so' < *swos-ont.
PIE *se [acc.], *seue [gen.], *sebhio [dat.] 'oneself,' *suo- 'own'. IE cognates: Olr.
fein,fadein < *sue-(de-)sin 'own, self, OAv. hoi, YAv. he, se 'he, she' ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sebum 'hard animal fat, tallow' [n. o] (P1.+)
The word is attested too early to be a loan from Germanic. WH and IEW suggest that
it and Germanic *saip- 'soap' may reflect a common etymon. LIV reconstructs a root
*seib- 'to drip', but *-b- is extremely rare in PIE, and the meaning of sebum is not
necessarily connected with 'to drip'. No etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 478, 504, EM 607, IEW 894, Leumann 1977: 68.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: seco, -3re 'to cut, sever' [v. I; pf. seem, ppp. sectum] (Naev.-H)
Derivatives: secula 'sickel/scythe' (Varro), sectio 'cutting, castration, buying up'
(Varro+), sector 'who cuts, who buys up' (P1.+), sectura 'cutting, quarry' (Varro+),
securis [fi] 'axe' (P1.+), securicula 'small axe' (P1.+), secespita [fi] 'kind of sacrificial
knife' (Suet., Paul, ex F.); f(a)enisex 'mower' (Var.+); exta, -drum 'upper internal
organs' (Naev.+); circumsecdre 'to cut round; circumcise' (Cato+), dese/icare 'to cut
off, mow' (Varro+), disse/icare 'to cut apart' (Varro+), exse/icare 'to cut out' (P1-+),
insecure 'to make an incision' (Cato+), insicium 'rissole' (Varro+), praese/icare 'to
cut at the end' (Varro+),praesegmen 'a clipping' (PI.+),prose/icare 'to cut off, sever'
(P1.+), prosicies 'severed portion' (Lucil.+), rese/icare 'to cut back, prune' (Cato+),
subsecdre 'to cut away' (Varro+), subsicivus 'left over, spare' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *sekaje/o-. It. cognates: U. prusekatu, prusektu [3s.ipv.II], pruse^etu,
proseseto [ppp, acc.pl.n.], proseseto [gen.pl.n.], pruse^ete, prosesetir, prosesetir,
proseseter [dat.pl.n.] 'to cut out' (the entrails of the sacrificial animal) < *pro-seka-,
sup. *pro-seke-to-\ U. pruse^ia [acc.pl.n.] < *prd-sek-io- '(entrails) which have been
cut out'.
PIE *sekhie/o- 'to cut off. IE cognates: Hit. sdkk-/sakk- 'to know, pay attention
r
to' < *sokh/*skh, OCS sesti, Is. sekg 'to cut', Ru. sec' 'to cut to pieces, beat', SCr.
r r
sjeci 'to cut'.
The long e in secula is striking, and recalls that of regula and tegula. Varro mentions
that secula is found in Campania, which may mean that it is a dialect form, and that
its long e in Latin does not prove long *e in Pit. The noun s(a)cena [f.] 'sacrificial
axe' (Andr, Paul, ex F.) is only attested in quotations by Festus and Paulus, and its
form vacillates; still, the fact that it was borrowed into Celtic (Olr. scian 'knife')
shows that it was alive during the Roman era. The solution put forw...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: side' (P1.+), extrinsecus [adv.] 'from without, on the outside', utrimque()secus [adv.]
'on both sides'. > -
Pit. *sekwos [nom.sg.m.] 'separate'.
PIE *se-lsuo- 'for/by oneself, separate'. IE cognates: Olr. seek (+ acc.) 'past;
without', W. heb, Co. heb, OBret. hep 'without' < PCI. *sekua 'separately' [ins.sg.?].
The older explanation is that secus continues a ppa. *(se-)sekw-os- 'following'.
Klingenschmitt 1980 objects that Celtic 'without' can hardly be explained on the
basis of the meaning of *sekw~, and furthermore that the lenition which the preposition
causes in Olr, W. and Co. suggests a vowel-final form in or *-o. Klingenschmitt
/ i
proposes an adj. *se-kuo- 'separate, by itself, with the element se- 'apart' of Latin
se-, se-, so-, and the suffix *-kuo- from Skt. visva- 'all'. The suffix -ester in sequester
('the third party') follows the example of equester and others.
Bibl.: WH II: 506, 518f, EM 608f, 615f, IEW 896f, Leumann 1977: 352,
Klingenschmitt 1980: 208-214; Meiser 1986: 246ff, LIV l.*sekV procul, sequor

Derivatives: sedes, -is [f.] 'seat, dwelling-place' (Andr.+), sediculum 'seat'

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: seges, -etis 'field of corn, arable land' [f. /] (Lex XII+)
Pit. *seg-et- 'seed' > 'sown land'.
PIE *seg-e/o- 'to adhere, fasten'. IE cognates: MW heu, Is. heaf'Xo sow, throw on,
strew' < PCI. *seg-(i)e/o-; Skt. pr. sajami, pf. sasanja 'to adhere, hang on', asakti- [f.]
'pursuit, devotion, adhering', msangin- 'carrying a quiver', OP fraha(n)jam
[ls.ipf.act.] 'to hang out', Lith. segti, 3s. sega 'to fasten, pin', OCSprisqsti 'to touch',
Sin. s§ci 'hold out one's hand, try to keep up with' < BS1. *seng-, Ru. posjagai' 'to
encroach, infringe', OCz. sahati 'to touch, reach for' < PS1. *s$gati.
Schumacher 2004: 563 justifies the semantic shift from 'to adhere' to Celtic 'to sow'
via the steps 'to imprint' > 'put in' > 'sow'. The tutelary goddess of corn at the time
of sowing Seia (Pliny) could be derived from *Seg~ja.
Bibl.: WH II: 509f., EM 61 If., IEW 887, LIV l.*seg-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sggnis 'slothful, inactive' [adj. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: segnitiaf-es [f.] 'inertia, sluggishness' (P1.+), signitds 'sluggishness'
(Acc.+).
Pit. *sek/g-ni~.
An adj. in *-«/-, cf. lenis, omnis. The root is identified as the root of Gr. t)Ka 'slightly,
softly', fytTCOV, Ion. 'qoccov 'smaller, weaker', sup. fjKicrrog 'least', which can reflect
PGr. *sek-. This might derive from PIE *sek- 'to dry out, coagulate' or from a root
*sehik-. Yet the latter is translated as 'to arrive' by LIV, which does not fit segnis nor
Gr. F)K- 'little'. If segnis is separated from the Greek forms, it may also belong to
*seg- 'to adhere, stick' ('sticky' > 'inert'). In case the root was *sek- or *seg-, the
long vowel is problematic: does it stem from an original root noun? This would be an
ad hoc assumption, since there is no direct evidence for it.
Bibl.: WH II: 510, EM 612, IEW 896.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: senex, senis 'old man' [m. k/i\ (Naev.+)
Derivatives: senior 'older; a man of older age' (P1.+), senatus, -Us 'political
assembly at Rome, senate' (P1-+), senecta 'period of old age' (PI+), senectus 'old'
(P1.+), senectus, -iitis 'old age' (P1.+), senere 'to be old' (Pac.+), senescere 'to grow
old, fade away' (Varro+); senilis 'old man' (Ter.+), senilis 'of an old man' (Naev.+),
senium 'old age, decay, melancholy' (P1.+), seniosus 'affected with weakness'
(Cato+), senica 'old man' (Pompon.+), senecio 'old man' (Afran.+); senator 'senator'
(Cic.+), senaculum 'piace where the senate assembled' (Varro+).
Pit. *seno- 'old; old man', *sen-ek- 'old man'. It. cognates: O. senateis, senateis,
[gen.sg.] 'senate', borrowed from Latin senatus.
AEVAXTM, OEV(XTT]UT
PIE *sen-(o-) 'old'. IE cognates: Olr. sen, W. hen 'old' < PCI. *seno-, Skt. sana-,
Av. hana- 'old', Gr. EVO< ^'old, stale, of last year', Arm. bin, Lith. senas, Latv. s$ns
'old' < *sen-o-, Go. sineigs 'old, elder*, sinista 'the eldest'.
It has been argued that Latin shifted from thematic *seno- to athematic *sen- under
the influence of iuvenis, but this is hardly credible. We may posit thematic *seno- (as
in Celtic), which changed to an /'-stem in Latin under the influence of iuvenis. The adj.
senilis can also derive from an o-stem noun (cf. erilis, servTlis, puerilis; iuvenilis not
attested before Cic.), as can senius, senium. The nom.sg. has introduced a suffix *-ek~.
Bibl.: WH II: 513fi, EM 613, IEW 907fi, Schrijver 1991: 149-153, Sihier 1995: 319^
Untermann 2000: 666.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: senfina 'bilge-water, bilge of a ship; the scum of society' [f. a] (Cic.+)
Derivatives: sentindre 'to bale out' (Caecil.+), sentindsus 'full of bilge-water'
(Cato); persillum 'small pitch-flavoured ladle, used by the flamen Portunalis to
anoint the equipment of Quirinus' (Fest.).
Pit. *sem-t-l *s(e)m-e/o- [pr.]?
PIE *semH- 'to scoop'. IE cognates: Olr. do-essim* 'to pour out' (< *tu-eks-sem-e/o~),

*tu-uo-sem-de/o-); Lith. semti 'to scoop'.
There is no obvious formal or semantic obstacle to the etymological connection with
PIE *semH-, but in the absence of other cognate forms in Latin, it is still basically a
root etymology. The instrument persillum could reflect *per-sem-lo- or *per-sm-elo-
to a verb *per-s(e)m-e/o- 'to pour over'.
Bibl.: WH II: 291, 514fi, EM 614, IEW 901 fi, Schumacher 2004: 569, LIV *semH-.

think', OCS s$stb 'sensible, wise' < BS1. *s(e)nt- 'think'; *sent- 'go' in Olr. set
'road' < *sentu...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: restrains himself, preserves'; hajsi [2s.pr.act.] 'you hold(?)'; Gr. £7tco 'to take care of,
commit, do'.
The presence of -e- instead of -i- in the second syllable in front of I exilis may be due
to assimilation to e in the first syllable (cf. teget-). Whereas sepelire could be a
denom. to an adj. *sepelo~, this cannot be the case for Skt. saparyati', and I find it
unlikely that an adj. *sep-elo- yielded a noun *sepeli- in PIE already from which, also
in PIE, a verb *sepeli-ie/o- was derived. Hence, the formation *sepeI-ie/o- must be
old. Lat. sepultus and sepulcrum probably have analogical sepul- < *sepel-to~, -tlo-
(on the model of, e.g., haurio - haustus).
Bibl.: WH II: 517, EM 615, IEW 909, Meiser 1998: 196, LIV *sep-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: septem 'seven' [num. indecl.] (PI.+)
Derivatives: septemdecim 'seventeen' (P1.+), septimus 'seventh' (Naev.+),
septimanus 'on the seventh day' (Varro+); septingenti '700' (P1.+), September, -bris
[adj.] 'of the seventh month' (Afran.+), septentridnes [m.pl.] 'Ursa Maior; the north'
(P1.+), septentridndlis 'northern' (Varro+), septum, -uneis 'seven-twelfths' (Varro+),
septem [adj.pl.] 'seven apiece' (P1.+); septudginta 'seventy' (Varro+), septuennis
'seven years old' (P1.+).
Pit. *septm, *septmo~.
PIE *septm 'seven', *s(e)ptm-o- 'seventh'. IE cognates: Olr. secht n-, W. saith, Hit.
siptamiia- [n.] 'liquid from seven substances' < *septm-io-, saptaminzu [adj?]
'sevenfold?' < *sptm-in-; Skt. sapta 'seven', saptatha-, saptama- 'seventh', Av. hapta-
' seven', haptada- 'seventh', Gr. em a 'seven', Lith. septyni, OCS sedmb, Go. sibun 'id.'.
In septudginta < *septm-dk(o)mt-, the -a- stems from the other decads, but -u- most
likely arose phonetically: *septma- > *septuma- > *septuwa- (Coleman 1992: 402).
A similar antevocalic development is found in septuennis. Septingenti can be phonetic
from *septengentoi < *septm-dkmt-oi.
Bibl.: WH II: 517f, EM 615, IEW 909, Coleman 1992: 395f, Sihler 1995: 414,422,
431, Beekes 1995: 214-216, Meiser 1998: 171-175.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sequor, sequl 'to follow' [v. Ill; pf. seciitus sum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: sequax 'following closely' (Lucr.+), secta 'course, line, system'
(Naev.+), sectari 'to pursue, accompany' (P1.+), sectarius 'a wether followed by the
flock' (P1.+), seetator 'follower, attendant' (Lucil.+); assequi 'to go after, achieve'
(PI.+), assecue 'attentively' (PI.), assectari 'to follow closely, escort' (P1.+), consequi
'to follow, overtake, achieve' (P1.+), consectdri 'to seek, pursue' (PL+), conseque
'consequently' (Lucr.), exsequi 'to follow, pursue, carry out' (PI.+), exsequiae [f.pl.]
'funeral procession' (Ter.+), insectdri 'to chase, harry', Tnsequi 'to follow closely'
(Lucr.+X obsequT 'to comply with, devote oneself (PL+), obsequium 'compliance
service' (P1.+), obsequella 'compliance, obedience' (P1.+), obsequiosus 'compliant'
(PL), obsecula [f.] 'devotee' (Laevi), pedisequus 'male attendant' (P1.+), pedisequa
'female attendant' (PI.+), perseciari 'to follow up, investigate' (P1.+), persequT 'to
follow persistently, seek out' (P1.+), prosequi 'to accompany, furnish' (P1.+)
subsequi 'to follow close behind' (PL+); secundus 'following, second' (P1.+)
secundare 'to make favourable for travel, prosper' (Lucil.+), secundarius 'of the
second grade or size' (Cato+), insecundus 'next in order' (PL), obsecundare 'to act in
compliance' (Ter.+).
Pit. *sekw-e/o- 'to follow', *sekw-to- 'followed', *sekwondo- 'following'.
PIE *sekw-e/o- [med.] 'to follow, accompany'. IE cognates: Olr. seichidir* 'to
follow' < *sek"-i- « *sekw-e/o-; Skt. pr. sacate, sisakti 'to follow, go after', Av.
haca- 'to accompany', Gr. eitopat 'to follow'; Lith. sekti, Latv. seki 'to follow, go
after'; Go. saihjan 'to see'.
The original ppp. *sekto- yielded the denominal verb sectari and its derivatives, and
was subsequently replaced by secutus on the model of the verbs in -uo. The adj.
secundus < *sekwo-ndo- 'following' came to be used as the ordinal 'second'.
Bibl.: WH II: 519, EM 616, IE...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: serenus 'clear, unclouded' [adj. o/a] (PL+)
Derivatives: serescere 'to become dry' (Lucr.).
Pit. *(k)sero- 'dry'.
PIE *Ksehro- 'dry'. IE cognates: Gr. ^qpoi; 'dry, arid', ^epov [n.] 'dry land', OHG
r
serawen, MHG NHG serben 'to diy out'.
Both forms seem to be built on an unattested stative pr. *sere- 'to be dry'. Lat. *ser~ <
*ser- can be due to shortening in pretonic syllable (Dybo's Law). The explanation for
the short vowel in Gr. ^epov is less clear.
Bibl.: WH II: 520, EM 616fi, IEW 625, Schrijver 1991: 338, Meiser 1998: 75.
sErius 'important, serious' [adj. o/a] (Naev,+)
Pit. *ser-jo-.
PIE *sehi-ro- 'slow,heavy'.
Schrijver suscribes to WH and IEW's etymology as *swer- 'weighty'. This begs the
question of the reflex of *sw- in Latin: *swe- yields so-, *swa- yields sua-, and sitesed
seems to show that *swe~ yields sue-. This means that serius is more likely to reflect
*se- without *-w-. The long vowel can hardly stem from compensatory lengthening of
a lost consonant in front of -r~. In view of the meaning 'weighty, serious', it is
tempting to link serius with severus, and assume contraction across *-vv-: * sewer io- >
*serio~. However, according to the established rule (cf. Meiser 1998: 92), the preform
accented as *sewirio- would not lose The following alternative may be
considered: since 'important' can be derived from 'weighty', serius may derived from
serus 'slow', via a shift from 'slow' to 'tiring' to 'heavy'.
Bibl.: WH II: 521, EM 617, IEW 1150fi, Schrijver 1991: 126. ^ serus
serO, -ere 1 'to plant, to sow seeds' [v. Ill; pf. sevT, ppp. satum] (PI.+)
Derivatives: semen [n.] 'seed, semen, offspring' (P1.+), seminium 'procreation,
breed' (P1.+), seminare 'to procreate; sow' (P1.+), seminarius 'of seed' (Cato+),
sementis [f.] 'sowing, crop' (P1.+), sementivus 'of sowing-time' (Cato+); sator
'sower, founder' (P1.+), sativus 'cultivated' (Varro+), satid 'sowing, planting'
(Cato+); asserere 'to plant near' (Cato+), conserere 'to plant,...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sero, -ere 2 'to link, join' [v. Ill; pf. serui, ppp. sertum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: (1) series 'series, sequence' (Varro+), serilia [n.pl.] 'plaited material'
(Pac,+); asserere 'to lay claim to' (P1.+), conserere 'to join, engage in' (P1.+),
deserere 'to leave, abandon' (P1.+), disserere 'to set out in words, discuss' (Varro+),
disertus 'skilled in speaking, plain' (P1.+), disertim 'plainly' (Andr.+), disertare 'to
discuss, talk about' (P1.+), edisserere 'to expound, relate' (P1.+), edissertare 'to relate
in detail' (Pl.-fc), Tnserere 'to put in, introduce' (Andr.+; Andr. tnserinuntur),
transserere 'to insert and pass through' (Cato+), praesertim 'above all, especially'
(P1.+); (2) sera;'detachable bar, rail' (P1.+), obserare 'to bolt, fasten' (Ter+); (3)
sermo 'speech, talk' (P1.+), sermdcinarf 'to hold a conversation' (Cato+), sermonatus,
-us 'talk, conversation' (PL).
Pit. *sere/o~, ppp. *s(e)rto-. It. cognates: O. aserum [inf.] 'to lay onto' (with object
manim 'hand') < *ad-ser-om.
PIE *ser-e/o- 'to thread, tie together'. IE cognates: Gr. rfpco 'to knit together', aor.
apai, epoai, eppcrax pi. 'earhangers'; OLith. seris 'thread'; OIc. servi [n.] 'collar' <
PGm. *saruiia~, Go. sarwa [n.pl.] < PGm. *sarua-? ToB sarm [n.], ToA surm
'motive, cause; basis, ground' < *s(u)er-mn.
Sermo cannot be connected with O. sverrunei [dat.sg.] because of initial sv-
(*swermon would have yielded Latin *sormo). The derivation from a root 'to link, put
on a string' is not compelling, but can be defended with parallel etymologies of words
for 'speech' in other languages. Barton 1987 argues that PIE had a root aorist and a
le-present.
Bibl.: WH I: 356, II: 520-523, EM 616-619, IEW 911, 1049, Barton 1987,
Untermann 2000: 127, Meiser 2003: 126, LIV 2.*ser-. -> sors

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: serum 'whey; any similar fluid' [n. o] (Cat.+)
Pit. *sero- [n.] 'liquid'.
PIE *ser-o- [n.] 'flowing, liquid'. IE cognates: Skt. sard- 'flowing, liquid' < *sero-,
punah-sara- [adj.] 'running back', pr. sisarsi 'flows, runs'; Gr. opoq [m.] 'the watery
part of curdled milk, whey' < *sord- 'flower, runner'.
Serum could be cognate with Skt. sard- and Skt. si-sar- 'to flow, run', although these
are usually derived from PIE *sel- (see s.v. salid). Otherwise, we have only a Latin
action noun with e-grade and a Gr. agent noun with o-grade.
Bibl.: WH II: 525, EM 619, IEW 909f.
sSrus 'belated, slow' [adj. o/a] (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: serd [adv.] 'late, too late' (P1.+).
Pit. *sero- '(too) late'.
PIE *seh|-ro- 'long-lasting; late'. IE cognates: Olr. sir 'lasting, eternal', W. Co.
Bret, hir 'long'.
The root cannot be identified as the root of Skt. sa-, sya- 'to tie', which is *sh -.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: servus 'slave' [m. o] (Lex XII, Andr.+)
Derivatives: (1) serva 'female slave' (PL+), servolus 'young slave' (P1.+), servola
'slave-girl' (PI.+), servitium 'slavery, slave class' (P1.+), servitiis, -tutis 'servitude'
(Naev.+); conservus 'fellow slave' (P1.+), conserva 'female fellow slave' (P1.+),
conservitium 'fellowship in slavery' (PI.); (2) servTre 'to serve as a slave, be subject'
(P1.+), servTlis 'of a slave' (Lex XII+), servTliculus 'mean and cringing' (PL);
TnservTre 'to attach oneself to, take care of (P1.+), praeservire 'to be in attendance as
a slave' (PI.+), subservTre 'to behave as a servant' (Naev.+); (3) servdre 'to watch
over, look after; observe' (PL+), servdtor 'saviour, preserver' (PL+), servatrTx
'protectress' (Ter.+); asservdre 'to keep safe, watch' (P1.+), conservdre 'to keep from
danger, keep unchanged' (P1.+), observare 'to observe, watch, abide by' (P1.+),
observatio 'surveillance, observance' (P1.+).
Pit. *serwo- [m.] 'shepherd', *serwo- [n.] / *serwa- [£] 'observation'. It. cognates:
(1) O. serevkidimaden [abl.sg.], o£peuict5(|iap [acc.sg.] < *serwV- 'watching'?; (2)
U. Seritu, seritu, serititu [3s.ipv.II] 'to observe' < *ser(w)i- (cf. Martzloff 2006: 634);
U. anseriaia [ls.pr.sb.], aserio [2s.ipv.], azeriatu, aseriatu [3s.ipv.II], anzeriatu,

n. 'watch-tower' vel sim. (not specifically for watching birds), maybe *op-seri-tlo-m.
PIE *ser-u-o- 'guardian', *ser-u-o/h - 'protection'. IE cognates: Av. haraiti 'heeds,
2
protects', haumua- 'id.', hauryua- 'protecting' (pasus-hauruuo spa 'sheep-dog'),

de Vaan Latin Etymology: sgtius 'later, to a lesser degree' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Pit. *seto~.
PIE *sehi -to- 'late, slow'. IE cognates: Olr. sith- 'continuous', sithithir 'as long as',
W. hyd, Co. Bret, hes 'length' < *situ/i- 'length'; OE sTd 'long', OHG slto 'lax' <
*s(e)h]-i-to-, OE sTd, OS sTth, OHG sTd 'since' < *s(e)h,-i-to-.
Setius can be regarded as an io-derivative to an adj. *seto- 'late' or 'slow', which may
be formed from the same root as *sero- '(too) late'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sever us 'stern, strict, severe' [adj. o/a] (P1-+)
Derivatives: severitas 'sternness, seriousness' (Ter.+), severitudd 'seriousness'
(PI.+), asseverdre 'to declare, affirm' (P1.+).
Pit. *se%yvero- 'steadfast'. '
PIE *segh-ur/-un- 'holding'. IE cognates: Skt. sdhuri- 'victorious, strong' (RV) <
PIE *sogh-ur, Skt. sdhvan- 'powerful', Gr. cxopoq 'strong, secure', evexupov 'pledge,
security', oxupoq 'strong, secure', maybe crtfevoq [n.] 'strength, power' < *sgh-uen-
(Nussbaum 1998b: 525).
WH and Meiser 1998 explain severus as a compound of *swe- 'apart, away' and
*wero- 'true' < *'friendly, nice'. However, the semantics are difficult. Lat. verm
means 'real, true', and in view of the Celtic, Gm. and BS1. adjectives for 'true' which
also go back to *uehro-, this meaning must be old. And even if WH's conjecture,
r
viz. that *wero- also meant 'friendly' in a prestage of Latin, were true, a meaning
'stem, strict' hardly equals a description as 'not friendly'. For this reason,
Nussbaum's solution (1998b: 536) is attractive: severus could reflect "PIE
*segh-uer-o- [adj.] 'steadfast, tough', a possessive o-derivative of a hysterokinetic
nom,-acc. *seghuer 'steadfastness, toughness'". The latter formation can be
interpreted as a collective derived from a n. *segb-ur/-uen- 'holding'.
Bibl.: WH II: 528, EM 621, IEW 1165f., Meiser 1998: 82, LIV *segh-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sex 'six' [num. indecl.] (P1.+)
Derivatives: sexdginta 'sixty' (Cato+), sexagesimus 'sixtieth' (Ter.+), sexdgeni
[pl.adj.] 'sixty at a time' (P1.+); sexennis 'six years old' (P1.+), sent [pl.adj.] 'six at a
time' (P1.+), sedecim 'sixteen' (P1.+), sescenti [pl.adj.] 'six hundred; innumerably
many' (P1.+), semestris 'of six months, lasting six months' (Varro+); sextus 'sixth'
(P1.+), sextllis [adj.] 'name of the sixth month' (Varro+), sextans [m.] 'one-sixth'
(Varro+), sextarius [m.] 'measure of one-sixth' (Cato+).
Pit. *seks 'six', *seks-to- 'sixth'. It. cognates: Ven. *segtos in PN segtio[i] 'Sextio',
U. sestentasiaru [gen.pl.f.] '?', maybe name of a day < *seks-to- 'sixth', cf. O.
deken-t-asio-\ maybe O. sehsik[ [nom.sg.?] < *seks- if to Lat. sextarius 'measure'.
PIE *(s)ueks 'six', *uks-o- 'sixth'. IE cognates: Olr. se, MW chwech 'six', Skt. sas-
'six', sodasa 'sixteen', YAv. xsuuas 'six', xsuuas.dasa- 'the sixteenth'; Gr. e^, Dor.
Arm. vecAlb. gjashte, Lith. sesi, OCS sestb, Po. szesc, Go. saihs 'six'.
The forms sedecim, senT and semestris show voicing and loss of *ks in *-ksd~, *ksn,
*ksm. In sexaginta, -a- has been inserted into *seks-g- (which would have yielded
*seginta) on the analogy of quadraginta. The Pit. form *seks has analogically
dropped *-w- from *sweks by analogy with *septm 'seven'.
Bibl.: WH II: 528f, EM 621, IEW 1044, Lejeune 1974: 338, Coleman 1992: 395,
j !., :
Sihler 1995: 413, Beekes 1995: 213-216, Meiser 1998: 171, Untermann 2000: 664,
67 If, Lubotsky 2000.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: si, sic 'if [cj.] (Lex XII, Andr.+; CIL sei, nisei)
Derivatives', sic 'thus, so' (Naev>), sin 'but if (P1.+), sive, seu 'or if, whether'
(P1.+), nisi 'except if, unless, but that, but' (P1.+).
Pit. *sei [loc.sg.] 'in this' > 'thus'. It. cognates: Vol. sepis 'if someone' < * sei pis;
U. sopir 'id.' < *suepis < *suai pis; O. svai, svai, suae, U. sve, sue, SPic. suai [cj.]
'if; whether' < *suai [loc.sg.f.?].
PIE *so- 'this'.
Lat. sive, seu < *sei-we, sin < *sei-ne, sic < *sei-ke. The original meaning of Pit. *sei
was 'so', which developed into a conditional. The older meaning is preserved in the
expression si dis placet, cf. English so help me God. *Sei may be loc.sg. of *so-, since
Sabellic has *swai, which may be loc.sg.f. of *swo-. Lat. seu reflects *sew < *sewe,
with apocope preceding the change of *e (< *ei) > i
Bibl.: WH II: 530, EM 622, IEW 978f., Leumann 1977: 470, Meiser 1998: 59,
Untermann 2000: 667, 721 f., 725f. ipse, so-

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: siccus 'dry' [adj. o/a] (Cato+)
Derivatives: siccare 'to dry (tr./intr.)' (Cato+), siccitas 'dryness' (P1.+), siccoculus
'having dry eyes' (PL); exsiccare 'to make dry, drain' (Enn.+).
Pit. *siskwo-1
PIE *si-sk-u- 'dry'? IE cognates: Mir. sesc 'dry, barren, infertile', MW hysp 'dry' <
*siskuV~; Skt. a-sascu$T- 'not drying up', Av. hisku- 'dry', Khot. skala 'dry places',
Gr. SOKETO (ptovrj 'the voice broke down', icxvoi; 'dry, arid'; Lith. sekti 'to lower
oneself, sink, dry out'.
The other IE languages point to a root *sek- 'to dry up', from which Skt. has, among
others, a ppa. *se-sk-us-, whereas Avestan and Celtic rather point to an adj. *si-sk-u-.
The latter would normally yield *siscus in Latin, or, if thematized (as happened in
Celtic), *siskuwos. How this would result in *sikko- is unclear, so that the whole
etymology must of necessity remain uncertain. Differently, EM and IEW connect
siccus with PIE *seikw- 'to pour', positing *sikw-ko- 'defective as regards water, dried
out' (cf. Fruyt 1986: 164). Yet this is semantically cumbersome, and morphologically
unusual.
Bibl.: WH II: 533, EM 623, IEW 893f., Sihler 1995:200, LIV *sek-.
sld<5, -ere 'to sit down, settle' [v. Ill; sedi/sidi, sessum] (Lucil.+)
Derivatives: assidere 'to sit down' (PL+), considere 'to sit down, settle' (Varro+),
desldere 'to sink, settle down' (Cato+), insTdere 'to sit or settle upon, penetrate'
(Naev.+), obsidere 'to besiege' (Lucr.+), persTdere 'to sink right in' (Lucr.+),
possidere 'to take hold of (Naev.+), residere 'to take one's seat, sink back' (P1.+),
subsidere 'to squat, settle down' (P1.+).
Pit. *sizd-e/o- 'to sit down'. It. cognates: U. sistu, andersistu [3s.ipv.II], sesust,
andersesus<t> [3s.fiit.pf.] 'to sit down', with ander- 'to sit down between'.
PIE *si-sd-e/o- 'to sit down'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. sfdati, Av. hiSa- 'to sit down', Gr.
i^co 'id.', Arm. nsti- (< *ni-sisde-) 'to sit'; OPr. sindants 'sitting', OCS sesti, Is. s$dg
'to sit down' < *sind...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: visible, remarkable' (Ter.+), Insigne [n.] 'personal decoration, mark' (P1.+), Inslgnitus
'marked, remarkable' (P1.+); 'consignare 'to seal (a document)' (P1.+), designare 'to
mark out, plan' (P1.+), dissTgndtor 'who assigns seats' (PI.+), exsigndre 'to certify (by
seal) as authentic' (PI.+), obsigndre 'to affix a seal to' (P1.+); significare 'to indicate
by signs, mean' (P1.+), significaiio 'meaning, sense' (Varro+).
Pit. *sekno- 'statue, sign'. It. cognates: Marr. asignas [nom.pl.] 'cut meat' <
*d-sek-na; O. segunu, oeyovto [acc.pl.], se[g]unum [acc.sg.] 'statue' < *sek-no- [n.];
0. seganatted [3s.pf.J 'signavit', denom. to O. segno-. Possibly also Mars, seino,
seinq [acc.pi.?] 'statue?'.
PIE *sek(h )-no- 'cut' [adj./n.]. IE cognates: see s.v. seed.
2
The etymological appurtenance to seed 'to cut' implies a semantic shift of *sek-no-
'what is cut out', 'carved out' > 'sign'. Sigillum is the diminutive * segno-lo- 'small
sign'. Signare and in-sTgni- are also derived from *signo- 'mark'. There is no certain
trace of a root-final laryngeal. Since in a PIE preform *sekh no- > Pit. *sekano-, the
2
*-a- would not have syncopated, the derivative *sek-no- may have been formed at a
more recent stage from the synchronic root *sek-.
Bibl.: WH II: 534f., EM 624, IEW 895f., Meiser 1998: 81, Untermann 2000: 129,
661 fi, 664f, LIV *sekH-. seed

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sileo 'to be silent, be quiet' [v. II; pf. sihti] (P1.+)
Derivatives: silescere 'to become silent' (Ter.+), silentiitm 'silence, quiet' (P1.+),
silenius 'silent' (Laev. apud Gell.), consilescere 'to fall silent' (P1.+).
Pit. *silo- 'quiet, silent', *sile- 'to be quiet, silent'.
PIE *sh|/2i-lo- 'silent'. IE cognates: Go. cma-silan 'to become quiet* (pret.3s. sa
winds anasilaida 'the wind dropped') < *sil-e~, OE salnes(s) [f.] 'silence' < *sail~.
WH and EM assume that the original meaning may have been 'to come to rest' rather
than just 'to remain silent'. WH ancl IEW connect sileo with Latin sino 'to let', from a
root which is now reconstructed as *shii- (or *sh i-). This requires a trivial semantic
2
development from 'to let go' > 'to come to rest'. If sileo was based on an adj. *silo-
'letting go, quiet', this could be phonetically derived from *sHi-lo- or *siH-lo- (cf.
Schrijver 1991: 248f, 343), with a pretonic first syllable. In Gm, we find a root *sil~,
*sail- meaning 'quiet, silent', which can be from PIE *sHi- or *siH- if in pretonic
position. Hence, Italic and Germanic can both reflect a PIE adj. *sHi-lo-.
Bibl.: WH II: 535f, EM 625, IEW 889-891, LIV l.*seh,(i)-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: silex, -ids 'hard rock, flint, lava' [m. k] (PI.+)
Derivatives: siliceus 'of silex' (Cato+), siliqua 'pod; carob-tree' (Varro+), silicula
'small pod' (Varro+), siligd, -inis [f.] 'soft variety of wheat' (Cato+), silTgineus 'made
from siligo' (Cato+).
WH and IEW assume a dissimilation of *skelik- to silik- in silex and siliqua, but this
is only induced by the comparison with OCS skolbka 'muscle', which rather belongs
to Lith. skald 'match, rod', OHG scala, Alb. hale < *skol-\ cf. Vasmer 1950-1958 III:
647. Since the ablaut grade is different, and the meaning would only match siliqua
(which has an unusual suffix), it is better not to assume an unwarranted dissimilation
on the basis of this comparison. It seems uncertain that even silex and siliqua belong
together, in view of their diverging meanings. For silTgo, the meaning of siliqua is
sufficiently close to suggest that sil-Tgo is derived from (the same root as) siliqua.
Bibl.: WH II: 536, EM 625, IEW 923-927.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: similis 'similar, like' [adj. /j (Naev.+)
Derivatives: simul (var. semul PI, CIL) [adv.] 'together, simultaneously' (Andr+),
simultas 'animosity, quarrel' (P1.+), simulter 'in the same way' (PL), simuldre 'to
pretend, imitate' (P1.+), simulacrum 'likeness, image' (P1.+), simulatio 'pretence'
(Ter.+), similitas 'resemblance' (Caecil.+), similitudd 'resemblance, image' (Pac.+);
assimilis 'closely resembling' (P1.+), assimu/ilare 'to pretend, feign, copy' (P1.+),
consimilis 'similar' (P1.+), dissimilis 'unlike' (P1-+), dissimu/ilare 'to conceal,
disguise' (PI.+), msimulare 'to accuse, charge' (PL+).
Pit. *semali- 'alike, together'. It. cognates: maybe U. sumel [adv.] if
'simultaneously' < *somel (or *smli, Livingston 2004: 34).
PIE *smh -el-i-. IE cognates: Olr. samail, W. hafal 'similar, like', Gr. opaAog
2
'equal, level, smooth', Go. simle 'one time, once', OE simbel, OHGsimble 'always'.
Simul and semul reflect *semali [n.], with more recent sim- probably stemming from
similis. Similis < *semilis < *semalis.
Bibl.: WH II: 538-540, EM 626, IEW 902-905, Schrijver 1991: 218, Meiser 1998:
176. —»sem-, semi-, singuli

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: simpu(v)ium 'earthenware ladle used in religious ceremonies' [n. o] (Acta Arvalia,
Varro+)
Derivatives: simpulum 'earthenware ladle' (Plin.+).
IE cognates: Gr. CITTUT] (-ua) 'box for keeping flour and bread'.
Leumann suggests that simpulum is a remodelling of earlier simpu(v)ium, which
seems likely. If so, the p in simp- cannot be explained from anaptyxis in *sem-lo-, and
the etymological connection with the root *semH- 'to scoop' (thus Meiser 1998) must
be dismissed. The form and meaning of Gr. OUTUT] are close enough to suggest
borrowing from Greek, or from a Semitic language, from which Greek also took the
word. Adoption into the Latin vocabulary would account for the introduction of the
nasal.
Bibl.: WH II: 540f., EM 627, Leumann 1977: 136, Meiser 1998: 81. sentma

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: sincerus 'sound, whole, pure' [adj. o/a] (P1.+) j
Derivatives: sinceris 'pure' (Varro+).
Pit. *sm-kair/so- 'whole, intact'. t
PIE *keh i-r/so- 'whole'? IE cognates: see s.v. caelum.
2
Has often been connected with procerus, but the semantics hardly allow for this
possibility (cf. Hill 2006: 194). The /-stem variant sinceris is argued to be old by
Hiltbrunner 1958: 114f. Formally, sincerus cannot be explained from a dissimilation
of r's, as is the case with procerus. Hiltbrunner 1958: 146-54 returns to an old
suggestion by Corssen from 1868 to connect caeri-mdnia 'purity', and posits an
earlier adj. *caerus 'whole, intact'. Indeed, this seems semantically very attractive,
and formally, it is without problems. The adj. could be Pit. *kairo- or *kaiso-; in
either case, we1 are reminded of *keh i-lo- as attested in PGm. *xaila- and OCS ceh
2
'whole', and maybe in Lat. caelum.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: singull 'one apiece, single' [adj.pl. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: singuldrius 'separate' (P1.+), singularis 'of a particular person;
singular' (Varro+), singilldtim 'one by one' (P1.+).
Pit. *sem-g-(lo~).
PIE *s(e)m-gh- 'single*.
The older etymology as *sem-gno- to the root *genh 'to be born' must be dismissed.
r
Meiser suggests that singuli contains a distributive particle *-gh-, and was later
suffixed with *-lo~.
Bibl.: WH II: 544, EM 628, IEW 373-375, Leumann 1977: 494, Sihler 1995: 436,
Meiser 1998: 177.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: singultus 'sobbing, hiccough' [m. u] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: singultare 'to catch the breath, gasp' (Verg.+).
Pit. *sengw~ol-to-l
PIE *sengw(h)- .
Leumann suggests an original verb *sing-ulare with the same suffix found in other
verbs of sound, e.g. sibilare, and the same kind of derivative as in tumultus. Lat.
singultus cannot represent a ppp. *singulitus under the normal rules of syncope (cf.
Schrijver 1991: 273). Thus, it may have been built directly on the model of other
words in -ultus. The root sing- has been compared with PIE *senglh- 'to sing' (which
shares with sobbing the fact that a sound is produced; but 'sobbing* is seldom referred
to as 'singing'...); but one might also compare PIE *sengw- 'to fall down, sink':
coughing and sobbing are characterized by violent movements of the throat and often
the entire head.
Bibl.: WH II: 542, EM 628, Leumann 1977: 354.

development. If -is- represents the PIE comparative suffix to which an extra
*-tero~ was added, the root can be *sin(H)- or *sen(H)-. WH connect the root *senh -
2

(side)' for *senH-is-tero- > sinister. This remains gratuitous, because the semantics
are not specific enough.
