content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: taceo 'to be silent' [v. II; pf. tacui, ppp. taciturn] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: taciturnus 'maintaining silence' (P1.+), taciturnitds 'the maintaining of
silence' (P1.+), tacitulus 'silent' (Varro); conticescere 'to stop talking' (P1.+).
conticinium 'the quiet of the night' (PI.+), obticere 'to remain silent' (Ter.+),
obticescere 'to answer with silence' (P1.+), reticere 'to keep silent, leave unsaid'
(P1.+), reticentia 'silence' (PI.+).
Pit. *tak-e- 'to be silent'. It. cognates: U. ta?ez, tases tasis [ppp. nom.sg.m.], tasetiir
t
[nom.pl.m.] 'tacit' < *taketo~.
PIE *tHk-eh 'to be silent'. IE cognates: Go. pahan 'to keep secret', OIc. peg/a, OS
r
thagian, OHG thagen < PGm. *paxe-ie~.
LIV follows Klingenschmitt 1982: 78 in assuming that the Latin and Gm. words for
'to keep silent* belong to Gr. 7tn^oocD, jrctbccco 'to duck (for fear)', aor. ejrrfj^a, Dor.
inxalja, and Arm. t'ak'eaw 'he hid himself. This seems implausible. Formally, it is
unknown whether PIE *pt- would yield Germ. *p- and Latin (the case of perna,
Germ. *fersn- < *tspersn-h - 'heel' rather suggests that *pt- would yield Germ. */-,
2
Lat. p-). The Greek and Armenian words have a different meaning, and are better
connected with *pe(- 'to fall*.
Bibl.: WH II: 641 f, EM 673, IEW 1055, Schrijver 1991: 104, Untermann 2000:
73 If, LIV *pteh k-.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: taedet 'to be tired of [v. II; pf. taeduit, ppp. taesum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: taeter [adj. o/a] 'foul, horrible' (P1.+), taetrare 'to make loathsome'
(Pac.), taetritudo 'loathsome condition' (Acc.); pertaedere 'to fill with digust'
[Pompon.+; ppp. pertTsum Lucil.+], pertaedescere 'to begin to fill with disgust'
(Cato).
Pit. *taid-e- [v.], *taid-ro- [adj.]
Taeter may regularly reflect *taidro-, but there is no good etymology for Pit. *taid~.
Bibl.: WH II: 642f„ EM 673. fastus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: talis 'of such kind' [adj. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: talio 'exaction of compensation' (Lex XII+).
Pit. *tdli-.
PIE *teh -li- 'such'. IE cognates: W. talu 'to pay', Gr. rqXiKoq 'of such an age',
2
Lith. tolei 'until', OCS toli 'to such a degree', tolb 'so much, many', toliko 'id.'.
If talid is correctly interpreted as tali-on- 'compensation with the same' (Leumann
1977: 366), it is derived from talis.
Bibl.: WH II: 643f„ EM 674, IEW 1086f, Leumann 1977: 366,483, Schrijver 1991:
145, Meiser 1998: 167. qui

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: talus 'ankle, knuckle' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: taxillus 'a die, cube' (Pompon.+); subtel 'the hollow of the foot'
(Prise.).
Pit. *takslo-.
If taxillus is old, and not a recent analogical form on the model of ala - axilla, mala -
maxilla (but these are a-stems), as EM object, taxillus would prove that talus goes
back to *takslo-. The suggested connection with W. sawdl, Olr. sal 'heel' < PCI.
*statla- is difficult, cf. Schrijver 1995^ 421. It would only work in the case of an
s-less variant of the root PIE *(s)teh -, and an (unknown) suffix *-k- added to it.
2
Cowgill 1978: 38 accepts the explanation of subtel from *subtele < *sub-taksli, n. of
*subtaksli- 'under the heel'.
Bibl.: WH II: 619, 645, EM 675, Schrijver 1991: 145.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tam 'so, so much' [adv.] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: tamen 'yet, nevertheless' (Andr.+), tandem 'after all, at last' (P1.+),
tamquam 'just as, as though' (Naev.+), tantus 'so great, as much' (Naev.+), tantillus
'so small' (P1.+), tantfsper 'for so long as, all the time' (P1.+).
Pit. *tam 'so', *tanto- 'so great'. It. cognates: O. etanto, U. etantu [nom.sg.f.] 'so
great' < *e-tanto-; maybe U. -ta, -tu, -to [postp. + abl.] 'from, away' < *ta, poss. to
PIE *to/ta~.
PIE *teh m, *teh -nt-(o-). IE cognates: see s.v. 50-.
2 2
Tamen < *tam-em, tandem < *tam-dem with the secondary particle -dim. The form
*tam might be an Italic creation, s.v. quam, -dam; but tantus probably reflects a PIE
formation type of adjectives in *-nt(-o)~ derived from pronominal stems.
Bibl.: WH II: 645f„ EM 674f, IEW 1086f, Klingenschmitt 1972: 101,
Sims-Williams 1997: 320f, Sihler 1995: 393, Untermann 2000: 72, 241. —• topper,
turn

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tango, -ere 'to touch* [v. Ill; tetigT, factum; sb. OLat. tag-a-, Pac. also taxis] (P1.+)
Derivatives: dumtaxat 'at most, up to; as long as' (P1.+), taxim 'gropingly'
(Pompon.+), taxare 'to assess (the worth of)' (Sen.+); tagax 'light-fingered, thievish'
(Lucil.+); tactio 'touching' (P1.+), tactilis 'able to be touched' (Lucr.), tactus, -us
'touch' (Lucr.+); integer [o/a] 'untouched, unused' (Naev.+), integrascere 'to begin
anew' (Ter.), integratio 'renewal' (Ter.), integrare 'to renew' (Pac.+); attingere 'to
touch, reach' (P1.+; sb. attiga- in OLat.), contingere 'to touch, reach' (P1.+), obtingere
'to fall as one's lot' (P1.+); contages, -is 'contact' (Lucr.), contagio 'contact' (P1.+),
contagium 'contact' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *tang-e- [pr.], *tag-s- [pr.], *te-tag~ [pf.], *tagto- [ppp.], *ntagro- 'untouched'.
It. cognates: U. antakres, antakre [abl.pl.] probably 'untouched' < *n-tag-ro~;
maybe Marr. taa [3s.sb.J 'to touch' or 'to take away' < * tag-a-1?; maybe Vol. atahus
[3s.fut.pf.]'?' < *ad-tah-us-t or a-. The reflex h < *g is irregular, cf. Untermann.
PIE pr. *th -n-g- 'to touch', ppp. *th g-to-, *-teh g- in cp. IE cognates: Gr. Teraytbv
2 2 z
'having seized', Go. tekan 'to touch', taitok 'touched', OIc. tok 'took', ToB cesam, 3p
ceken- 'to touch'.
The OLat. sb. forms in * tag-a- can be regarded as remains of an earlier aorist
paradigm, either root aor. or redupl. It is likely that tagax (although attested relatively
recent) and integer were also derived from this stem. The finite forms in tax- and
taxare point to an earlier s-present (cf. faxd, faxim to facio), as is the clearest in the
petrified expression dumtaxat. The length in tact- stems from Lachmann's Law. The
long a in contages and contagid and -ium is most disputed: it could phonetically
continue a full grade *teh g- origin, or be due to analogy with other compounds
2
having -ag- in the second membre (indages, compages, etc.).
Bibl.: WH II: 647, EM 676, IEW 183, Schrijver 1991: ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tardus 'slow' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: tarditudo 'slowness' (P1.+), tardare 'to cause to slow down, delay'
(P1.+), tardor 'slowness' (Varro+), tardescere 'to become slow' (Lucr.+),
tardiusculus 'rather slow' (PL+).
On the formation of tardiusculus, the date of which is difficult to ascertain, see
Kummel 2004b: 354. Tardus has no etymology; the connection with Gr. xepti 'weak'
and Skt. tdruna- 'young, fresh' proposed by WH is semantically unconvincing, and
formally impossibly (if the latter forms continue PIE *teru~).
Bibl.: WH II: 648f, EM 677, IEW 1070f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: taurus 'bull' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: taura 'barren cow' (Varro+), taureus 'derived from a bull' (Lucr.+),
taurinus 'of a bull, of ox-hide' (PI.+).
Pit. *tauro- 'bull'. It. cognates: U. turuf, toru [acc.pl.];tures [abl.pl.] [m.] 'a certain
sacrificial animal', 'bull?'. : ;
IE cognates: Olr. tarb, W. tarw, Gr. xcrupoq 'bull' [m.]^ Lith. tauras, Latv. taurs [m.]
'aurochs', OCS tun, 'bull', Ru. tur (gen. turd), SCr. tur 'aurochs'.
It is striking that Latin does not show the change of *-aur- > *-aru- as in parvum, and
with -e- in nervus. This might indicate that taurus is a loanword post-dating the
metathesis *-wr- > *-ru~, but Greek does not appear an obvious candidate for the
lending language, in view of the semantic field. The earlier history of the word is
uncertain: there is no cognate in Ilr. or Tocharian, whereas there are Semitic words
for 'bull' which are conspicuously similar. Hence, it may have been an early
loanword of the form *tauro- into the western IE languages.
Bibl.: WH II: 650f„ EM 677, IEW 1080-1085, Leumann 1977: 101, Schrijver 1991:
266, Untermann-2000: 777f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: taxus 'yew-tree' [f. o] (Enn.+)
IE cognates: Gr. to^ov 'bow', Myc. to-ko-so-wo-ko Itokso-worgosl 'bow-maker'.
Since yews are the usual wood for making bows from Mesolithic times onward, taxus
must be connected with Gr. xo^ov, which was regarded as a loanword from Scythian
*taxsa- 'bow', but is already present in Mycenaean. Yet taxus cannot be a direct
borrowing from Greek, nor (as far as the meaning is concerned) from Scythian.
Watkins (1985: 69) derives Gr. TO^OV as PIE *tok*-so-from the root *tekw- 'to flow,
run, flee', which is some contexts is used as 'to shoot'. Latin taxus could then be from
a zero-grade *tkv-s-o- with secondary full grade -a-. Alternatively, taxus could be a
loanword from a different IE dialect.
Bibl.: WH II: 653, EM 678, IEW 1059f„ EIEC 654f.
tegO, -ere 'to cover' [v. Ill; pf. texi, ppp. tectum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: teges, -etis [f.] 'piece of matting, covering' (Naev.+), tegeiicula 'small
piece of matting' (Varro+), tegimen 'cQver' (Lucr.+), tectum 'roof, ceiling, dwelling'
(P1.+), tector 'plasterer' (Varro+), tectorius [adj.] 'of plaster, stucco' (Varro+),
tectarium 'cover, lid' (Cato), tegula 'roof-tile' (P1.+), tegillum 'piece of rush matting'
(P1.+); toga 'formal outer garment, toga' (P1.+), togalis 'of togas' (Cato), togatus
'wearing a toga' (Varro+), togula 'a (bad) toga' (Titin.+); contegere 'to cover over,
conceal' (Ter.+), circumtectus 'covered' (Naev.), detegere 'to remove the roof,
uncover' (P1.+), integere 'to cover, roof (P1.+), integumentum 'shield, wrapping'
(PI.+), obte/igere 'to cover, protect, conceal' (P1.+), pertegere 'to cover completely'
(P1.+).
Pit. *teg-e/o- 'to cover', *tekto- 'covered', *tege-t- 'covering', *toga- 'cover, cloak'.
It. cognates: SPic. tokam [acc.sg.] 'grave stone' or 'figure' < *toga~; possibly U.
tettome [acc.sg. + -en] '?', a certain locality < *tek-to-l U. tehtefim [acc.sg.] '?' <
*teg-t-ed/lio-7
PIE *(s)teg-e/o- [pr.] 'to cover', *(s)tog-h - [fi], *(s)teg-lo- [n.]. IE c...

Derivatives: meditullium 'the interior of a country, centre' (Cic.+).
Pit. *teln-(us-l).
PIE *telh -o- 'ground'. IE cognates: Olr. talam 'earth' < *telamon-, Gr. xe^apwv
2
'strap' < *teIh -mon-; Skt. tola- [n.] 'surface, bottom' (SrSu,+), Lith. tiles 'bottom of
2

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: a barge, flooring', OCS tbla [pi.], ORu. tblo 'foundation, bottom' < PIE *tlH-o-; OPr.
talus 'floor', Lith. patalas 'bed, (pi.) feather-bed', Ru. potolok 'ceiling' < PIE
*h po-tolH-o-; maybe OIc. pil(i) [n.] 'board',pilja [fi] 'deal, plank', pel [n.] 'floor',
2
OE dille 'deal', OHG dil, dilo 'wall, deal'.
We find an ablaut alternation *e : *o between *tellu- and the compound *medi-toll-io-
which recalls that of terra : ex tor ris. Tellus is unique in being the only polysyllabic
(original) 5-stem with long u in the suffix. If it goes back to PIE, -us- may reflect
*-uHs- (cf. crus), or maybe an old u-stem with case forms in *-e/ou- (> Lat. -w-)
which was extended by means of an s-suffix. The root could be PIE *telh - 'to bear,
2
carry', from which Olr. talam 'earth' can also be derived (cf. Stiiber 1998: I50f.). In
fact, Sabellic shows a pr. *telne/o- where Latin has tollo 'to bear'; this stem *teln-
may have been the derivational basis for tellus (and *toln- for medi-tullium, unless -11-
was taken from tellus), since tell- cannot directly reflect Pit. *tela- < *telHThus, the
f
earth would have been referred to as 'bearer' or 'support' (of the sky, or of the
creatures and objects on the earth); the o-stem *t(e)lH-o- 'ground, bottom' found in
Skt., BS1. and Gm. could have the same origin.
Bibl.: WH II: 655, EM 679, IEW 1061, Stiiber 1998: I50f. tollo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: temere 'blindly, recklessly' [adv.] (P1.+)
Derivatives: temeriter 'thoughtlessly' (Acc.), temeritudo 'recklessness' (Pac.),
temeritds 'recklessness' (P1.+), temerarius 'accidental, reckless' (P1.+), temerare 'to
violate, desecrate' (Verg.+),
Pit. *temesi [loc.sg.]
PIE *temH-os- [n.] 'darkness'. IE cognates: Skt. tdmas- [n.] 'darkness, blinding',
Av. tsmah- [n.] 'darkness'; Lith. temii 'to become dark, to become evening', tcimsa
'darkness'.
A petrified loc.sg. from a noun *ternes- < *temH-o/es- [n.] 'darkness' with clear cognates
in the other IE languages and in Lat. tenebrae. The reconstruction *temH-ro- in Schrijver
1991:105 is probably a lapsus when compared with p. 41J5 in the same book.
Bibl.: WH II: 656f., EM 679, IEW 1063f., Schrijver 1991: 104f., 415, Stiiber 2002:
158f., LIV *temH-. —• temetum, tenebrae

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: temetum 'intoxicating liquor' [n. o] (PI.+)
Derivatives: temulentus 'drunken' (Ter.+); abstemius 'refraining from wine,
self-restrained' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *temo- 'intoxication' or 'intoxicating substance'.
PIE *temH- 'intoxication'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. tamyati [3s.act.], aor. tamat
[3s.sb.act.], tdmrd- 'dark red, copper-red', Arm. t'mrim 'to become stunned' < *tem-;
NHG damisch, damlich < PGm. *pcem-i-.
Lat. abstemius can be regarded as a hypostasis from *abs temo\ together with
temulentus 'containg temo-\ this points to an original o-stem *temo- 'intoxication',
which may continue a PIE root noun. Long e is confirmed by Armenian and
Germanic, but Skt. tamyati probably has secondary -a-.
Bibl.: WH II: 657, EM 679f., IEW 1063, Schrijver 1991: 127, LIV *temH-. temere
temnO, -ere 'to scorn, despise' [v. Ill] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: contemnere 'to despise, scorn' [pf. contempsi, ppp. contemptum] (P1.+),
contemptim 'contemptuously' (Naev.+), contemptrix 'she who despises' (P1.+),
contemnificus 'scornful' (Lucil.).
Pit. *(kom-)tmne/o~.
PIE *tm-n(e)-hr 'to cut'. IE cognates: MIr. tamnaid 'cuts' (prob denominal), Gr.
rapvto, Att. TEJIVCO 'to cut', aor. frcapov, Att. Jhepov, Lith. tinti, 3s. tina 'to whet,
(colloq.) beat', Ru. tjat' (arch.), Is. tmi 'to beat', Sin. t$ti, Is. tnem 'to chop' < BS1.
*tin?tei\ Gr. oteppw 'to shake about, agitate'.
The compound contemnere is the older verb, from which temnere has been
backformed more recently. The etymology is disputed: the meaning 'scom' has
probably developed from a more concrete meaning, for which two candidates have
been advanced: PIE *stemb- 'shake violently, pound' (WH, EM, Szemerenyi 1995:
414; in fact, it is likely that this is a non-IE root, because of *-b~, because of the
unexplained variants in Greek and because it has few reliable cognates outside Greek)
and PIE *temh 'to cut' (LIV, Sihler, Meiser). Semantically, both can be defended:
r
'to shake' or 'crush' > 'despise' for the former (cf. the...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: temfl 'pole or yoke-beam of a cart or a plough' [m. «] (Enn.+)
Pit. *ten(k)smdn- 'pole'.
PIE *ten(K)s(-)m-n- 'to pull'. IE cognates: OHG dihsala, OE pal, OIc. pisl 'pole,
thill' (< PGm. *penxsld-).
The etymology is disputed. In view of the Germanic word for 'pole', which reflects
*tenksla~, Sommer 1914: 259 reconstructs *tenksmo for Latin. Meiser 1998,
comparing the Slavic verb forms in *tengh- reconstructs *tenghsmd-, whereas Eichner
}
1992: 72 posits *tensmd. The question cannot be definitely solved, since PIE shows
various verbal roots meaning 'to pull' which would fit the phonetics: *ten- 'to stretch,
tend' (LJV), *tens- 'to pull' (LIV) and *tengh- 'to pull' (IEW 1067, de Vaan 1999:
15-17).
Bibl.: WH II: 658, EM 680, IEW 1067, Eichner 1992: 72, Isebaert-Seldeslachts
1994: 174, Meiser 1998:118. tendo, teneo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: templum 'area for auspices, shrine' [n. o] (Andr.+; templa, -drum 'plank, purlin'
Lucr.+)
Derivatives: contemplare 'to gaze at, observe' (Naev.+), extemp(u)ld [adv.]
'immediately' (Naev.+); maybe antemna 'yard of a sailing-ship' (P1.+).
Pit. *t(e)mp-lo- [n.].
PIE *t(e)mp-Io-. IE cognates: Lith. tempti 'to pull, stretch', 3s. tempia, OIc. pgmb
'bow', ToB campam, A campas 'is able'.
Probably from the root *temp~ 'to stretch, string', hence 'stretching' > 'measuring'.
The adv. extemp(u)ld might be based on the verbal meaning 'to stretch', as 'tightly'
(cf. Flemish straks 'immediately' to Dutch strak 'tightly'). The appurtenance of
antemna to a root *temp- is disputed; it has also been analysed as *anti-mno- (cf.
Leumann 1977: 322, Forssman 1965).
Bibl.: WH I: 54, II: 659, EM 681, IEW 1064f., Meiser 1998: 81, LIV *temp-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tempto, -are 'to feel, test' [v. I] (P1.+; also tentare)
Derivatives: pedetemptim 'cautiously' (P1.+); attemptare (-«/-) 'to lay hands on,
attack' (Pac.+), pertemptare (-nt~) 'to try out, feel all over' (Ter.+).
Pit. *t(e)mp-to- 'touched'.
PIE *t(e)mp-to-. IE cognates: see s.v. templum.
Probably an iterative based on a ppp. *tempto~. This presupposes a verb meaning 'to
touch, feel', for which the root *temp- 'to stretch' seems a good candidate.
Szemerenyi 1995: 414 reconstructs an iterative *temb-ita- to the root *(s)temb- 'to
shake violently', which is less likely formally, see the discussion s.v. temnd.
Bibl.: WH II: 662, EM 681, IEW 1064f, Leumann 1977: 501, LIV *temp-. ->
templum

metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tempus, -oris 'time, moment' [n. r] (P1.+) ?
Derivatives: tempe/ori [adv.] ';at the right time' (P1.+), temperius [adv.] 'earlier'
(Cic.+), temporalis 'denoting time, temporary' (Varrof), tempestas 'occasion, season,
weather, storm' (Lex XII+), tempestivus 'ready at the proper time, ripe' (Cato+),
tempestus, -litis 'tempestas' (Varro), intempestus 'at the wrong time, nightly' (Enn.+),
intempestmis 'at the wrong time, ill-timed' (P1.+); temperare 'to restrain oneself,
modify, blend' (P1-+), temperantia 'self-control' (Varro+), temperatura 'mixture,
constitution' (Varro+), intemperans 'unrestrained' (PI.+), intemperiae [f.pl.] /
intemperies 'immoderateness, rage' (P1.+), obtemperare 'to obey' (PI.+); tempora,
-drum [n.pl.] 'side of the forehead, temple' (P1-+; rarely sg. tempus).
Pit. * tempos- [n.] 'stretch, measure'.
PIE *temp-os,;-es- [n.] 'stretch'. IE cognates: see s.v. templum.
All forms are based on a PIE s-stem 'stretching, what is stretched' > 'stretch (of
time)' > 'time, occasion'. PIE * tempos- formed the basis for the abstract tempes-tat-,
the adj. tempes-tivus and the verb *tempes-aie- > temperare. The meaning 'to
restrain, modify' of the latter shows the semantic shift from 'stretching' to
'measuring' which we also find in templum. This means that the noun *tempos- still
meant 'measure' in Pit. The noun tempora 'side of the forehead' is also generally
accepted to be the same word, having developed from 'stretchings' to 'stretched skin'
> 'temple'. IEW and Stiiber compare OIc. punn-vangi, OHG dunwangi 'temple', lit.
'thin cheek'.
Bibl.: WH II: 658-661, EM 680-682, IEW 1064f, Schrijver 1991: 384, Stiiber 2002:
159f., LIV *temp-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tends, -ere 'to extend, stretch' [v. Ill; pf. tetendi, ppp. tentum/tensum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: tentipellium 'device for stretching skin or leather' (Afran.+); attendere
'to pay attention, listen' (Ter.+), contendere 'to contrast, contend, assert' (Naev.+),
distendere 'to stretch out, fill' (P1.+), extendere 'to extend, arrange' (P1.+), intendere
'to stretch, strain, direct' (P1.+), ostendere 'to show, reveal' (P1.+), pertendere 'to
continue resolutely' (Ter.+), portendere 'to indicate, reveal' (P1.+), subtendere 'to
stretch out underneath* (Cato+); extentare 'to stretch, exert to the full' (P1.+),
ostentdre 'to exhibit, demonstrate' (P1.+), ostentator 'who displays ostentatiously'
(P1.+); protelum 'tandem of draught animals' (Catoproteldre 'to drive forth, beat
back' (Ter.+).
Pit. *tend- [pr.], *t$to- [ppp.]. It. cognates: U. ustentu, ustetu, ostendu [3s.ipv.II],
ustentuta [3p.ipv.II], ostensendi [3p.fut.ps.] maybe 'to prepare, lay ready'.
Untermann is hesitant about the connection with ostendere because of the semantics.
Meiser (2003: 192) thinks that these forms belong to tollo.
PIE *ten-dch)- [pr.] 'to stretch', *tn-to- [ppp.]. IE cognates: See s.v. teneo. See
Ktimmel 2005 on Skt. tandate as a secondary formation to -tandra
The root *ten- 'to stretch' has yielded two Latin presents, teneo and tendo; both share
the ppp. tentus. The pf. tetim of teneo can go back to PIE *te-tonwhereas tendo has
acquired a novel perfect te-tend-. The root-final dental of the PIE present cannot be
identified. The noun protelum probably represents *pro-tend-slo- 'instrument for
pulling forth'; but *-tens-lo- to the root variant PIE *tens- is also possible, cf. idles.
Bibl.: WH II: 227, 344, 376f„ 662fi, EM 470f., 524, 540, 682f., IEW 1065L, Sihier
1995: 503, Meiser 1998: 193, 2003: 19Ifi, Untermann 2000: 812-814, LIV *ten-.
teneo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tenebrae 'darkness' [f.pl. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: tenebrosus 'dark' (Varro+), tenebrio 'who operates in the dark,
dishonest person' (Afran.+), tenebricus 'shrouded in darkness' (Pac.+), tenebricosus
'dark' (Varro+); contenebrdscere 'to become completely dark' (Varro+).
Pit. *temasrd- 'darkness'.
PIE *temH-s-ro- [adj.] 'dark'. IE cognates: Skt. tdmisra- [f.] 'dark night', YAv.
tq&ra- [pi.] 'darkness', MP tar 'darkness', tang [adj.] 'dark'; OHG demar 'twilight'
(< PIE *temH-so-).
Dissimilated from *temebrai < *temasro- < *temHs-rh -, the f. of a ro-adjective
2
which is a derivative of the PIE j-stem *temH-os~, reflected in Latin temere.
Bibl.: WH II: 664, EM 683, IEW 1063L, Schrijver 1991: 104, Stiiber 2002: 158f.,
LIV *temH-. —• temere

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: transporting images of gods' (Titin.+), tenus, -oris [n.] 'kind of snare' (PI.), terms
[adv., prep.] 'as far as, up to' (Lucr.+); attinere 'to hold, delay, concern' (P1.+),
continere 'to hold together, keep, comprise' (P1.+), continuus 'uninterrupted' (Pl.+X
I
tenuis
613
detinere 'to keep, retain' (P1.+), obtinere 'to maintain, occupy, achieve' (P1.+),
pertinere 'to be concerned with, belong to, reach' (P1.+), pertindx 'stubborn,
tenacious' (P1.+), pertinacia 'stubbornness' (P1.+), retinere 'to detain, retain' (P1.+),
retinaculum 'rein, rope' (Cato+), retentare 'to keep hold of (P1.+), sustinere 'to
support, preserve' (P1.+), sustentare 'to keep from falling, maintain' (P1.+),
transtinere 'to provide a link' (PI.).
Pit. *t(e)ne- [pr.], *tenos- [n.] 'snare, stretch'. It. cognates: U. tenitu [3s.ipv.II] 'to
hold' < *ten-e-.
PIE *tn-eh,- 'to hold', *ten-os- [n.] 'stretch'. IE cognates: MW tannu 'to spread
out', MCo. tan 'take!' < PCI. *tan-nu-\ Skt. pr. tanoti [act.], tanute [med.], root aor.
atan [3s.act.], pf. tatdna 'to stretch, extend', tatd- 'extended, skilled, performed',
OAv. us-tana- 'stretched out', YAv. pairi.tanuiia [Is.opt.med.], pairi .. . tanauua
[ls.sb.act.] 'to keep away (from)'; Gr. tetvcj 'to stretch, pull tight', T&VUTCXI 'to stretch
(intr.)', TOVOC; [m.] 'tension, cord, string, tone, sound', aX,i-T£vri<; 'extending to the
sea'; Go. ufpanjan 'to extend', OHG den(n)en, NHG dehnen 'to stretch'.
Stative present to the root *ten- 'to stretch'. In Latin, ten- may derive from a
full-grade form or from the expected PIE zero grade, but U. has a full grade. Latin
furthermore continues an .s-stem in tenus [n.] and its petrified form tenus. Meiser
(1998: 183) explains tenus from a ppa. *tn-uos 'stretching to', but this seems less
likely: the meaning does not ;point to an original perfect, and reduplication is absent
whereas tetini retains it. The -a- in retinaculum is unexpected: was it influenced by
tendx and tenacial J
Bibl.: WH I:...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tener, -a, -um 'soft, delicate' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: tenellus 'delicate' (P1.+), tenellulus 'tender' (Laev.+), teneritudo
'tenderness' (Varro+), tenerescere 'to become soft' (Lucr.+); terenus 'mollis'
(Favorinus). .
Pit. *terVno- (*teru-no- or *teren-o-l) 'soft'.
PIE *teru-no-\/ *ter-en- 'tender'. IE cognates: Skt. tdruna- 'young, tender, fresh',
Av. tauruna- 'young'; [m.] 'boy', Oss. tceryn, tyryn/tcerna 'boy'; Gr. xepu 'weak,
soft' (Hsch.), xepwrjq 'wom-out ass, infirm old man' (Hsch.), Gr. xepr|v 'soft,
delicate'.
Since Ilr. and Gr. show adjectives *teru-, *ter-n- and *teru-n-, it is tempting to derive
*tenVro- via consonant metathesis from *terVno~. The 'Sabine' word terenus 'soft'
which Macrobius (late 4th c. AD) ascribes to Favorinus would match such a
reconstruction quite well.
Bibl.: WH II: 665, EM 684, IEW 1070f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tenuis 'slender, thin, fine' [adj. i] (PI.+)
Derivatives: tenuitds 'scantiness, thinness' (Cato+), tenuare 'to make thin, reduce,
narrow' (Lucil.+); subtenuis 'rather fine in structure' (Varro); attenudre 'to make
slender, weaken' (Lucr.+), extenuare 'to make thin, diminish' (Varro+).
Pit. *tp(a)u~.
PIE *tnh -(e)u- 'thin'. IE cognates: Olr. tanae, W. teneu, C. tanow, Bret, tanao
2
'delicate, thin' < PCI. *tanauo- < *tnk -eu-o-; Skt. tanu- 'thin, slender', MP tanuk
2
'thin, flat', Gr. xava6<; 'thin, elongated, expanding far', TOVU- [pref.] 'thin, slender' <
*tnHu-; Lith. tqvas 'thin, slender, high (of voices)', Latv. tievs 'thin, slender' <
*tenh -u-o-; OCS tbirbkb, Ru. tonkij, Sin. tanak 'delicate, fine' < *tnHu-k-; OHG
2
dunni, OIc. punnr 'thin' < *tnHu-.
The Pit. w-stem adj. was remade into an /-stem, yielding *tn(a)ui- > *ten(a)ui- >
tenuis. The PIE root form *tnh - might be an extended variant of *ten- 'to stretch',
2
but this cannot be certified.
Bibl.: WH II: 666, EM 684, IEW 1069, Schrijver 1991: 219, Meiser 1998: 110.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tepeo 'to be warm' [v. II] (Cato+)
Derivatives: tepidus 'warm' (Enn.+), tepor 'warmth' (Varro+), tepefacere 'to make
fairly hot' (Varro+).
Pit. *tep-e- 'to be warm'.
PIE *t(e)p-eh[- 'to be warm', *tep-os- [n.] 'warmth'. IE cognates: Olr. te 'hot', tess
'heat', W. tes, Bret, tez 'id.'; Skt. tdpati 'to heat, be/become hot', tapas- [n.] 'heat,
glow', YAv. tafsa- 'to become hot', tdpaiia- 'to make hot, create warmth', hqm.tapta-
'hot', MP tab 'fever' (< PIr. *tapah-)\ OCS teplostb [f.] 'warmth', Ru. teplyj, Cz.
teply 'hot' < *tep-lo-, OCS topiti, Ru. topit' 'to heat, melt'.
Latin only has productive derivatives. Tepor might be a continuation of the PIE n.
*tepos-, but since it is not attested before Varro, it may just as well be a recent
formation. The verb tepeo might be older, but it has no direct cognates in other IE
languages except maybe in Albanian ftoh 'to cool down' if from *tpe-sk~.
Bib!.: WH II: 667, EM 685, IEW 1069f, Stiiber2002: 161f, LIV l.*tep-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tergeo 'to rub clean, polish' [v. II; pf. tersi, ppp. tersum] (P1.+; pr. also ter go, -ere)
Derivatives: abstergere 'to wipe clean, wipe off (P1.+), circumtergere 'to wipe
round about' (Cato), detergere 'to wipe away, trim' (Andr.+), extergere 'to wipe
clean' (P1.+); mantelum 'hand-towel, napkin' (Lucil.+), mantelium (Varro+)
'hand-towel, napkin'.
Pit. *terg~. It. cognates: U. mantrahldu, mantraklu, mandraclo [acc.sg.] 'object
which the priest is holding', maybe < *man-trag-tlo- 'towel' vel sim.
Mantelum < *manu-terg-slo~, LIV opts for tergere as the original verbal formation,
but especially the compounds show that -tergere was more common. Since this is not
a stative verb, one might expect an iterative *torg-e-, but this is not attested. There are
no certain cognates of the form *terg-. One might connect Skt. tarh- 'to crush' < PIE
*(s)tergh- (if *-rgh- > Lat. -rg-); but Kloekhorst 2008: 411 reconstructs the Skt. words
as *stelgh~, in order to connect Hit. is talk-2' 'to flatten'.
Bibl.: WH II: 670, EM 685f, IEW 1071-74, Meiser 1998: 119, Untermann 2000:
451, LIV ?2.*ter(g)- or *(s)tergh-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tergum 'back' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: tergus, -oris [n.] (Enn.+), terginum 'raw-hide whip' (P1.+), tergiversarT
'to show reluctance' (Sis.+).
WH and IEW interpret tergum as *terg-o-, from a root *(s)ter- 'to be stiff with a
suffix *-g~. The underlying assumption is that tergum originally indicated the stiff
hair on the back of animals (IEW). This, however, is not supported by any evidence.
Thus, the etymology remains unclear.
Bibl.: WH II: 670, EM 686, IEW 1022-1027, Leumann 1977: 166.
U. terkantur [3p.pr.sb.] 'to check' or 'to confirm'.
Pit. *derk-e/o- [pr.].
PIE *derk- 'to look'. IE cognates: Olr. ad-con-dairc [pf.] 'saw' « * de-dork-, derc
'eye'; Skt. dars- 'to see', su-darsa- 'easily seen, conspicuous', Av. daras- 'to look',
darasa- [m./n.] 'looking', Gr. Sepicopm, pf. SeSopKcc 'to look', imo-Spa [adv.]
'looking up from below', Go. ga-tarhjan 'to expose, make public', OE torht 'clear'.
Bibl.: IEW 213, Untermann 2000: 747f., Schumacher 2004: 271, LIV *derk-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ter men, -inis 'boundary-stone' [n. «] (CIL, Acc.+)
Derivatives: terminus 'boundary-post' (Naev.+), termo 'finishing-post in a race'
(Enn.+), termindlis 'marking a boundary' (Varro+), terminare 'to mark the
boundaries, limit' (Lucr.+); determinare 'to delimit' (P1.+).
Pit. *ter-mn- [n.] 'boundary stone'. It. cognates: Ven. termonios [acc.pl.m.] 'of the
boundary' < *ter-mon-io-; Presam. xepjiavi [loc.sg.?], O. teremenniu [nom.acc.pl.],
teremniss [dat.abl.pl.] n. 'boundary stone'< *ter-mn-\ O. teremnattens [3p.pf.],
teremnatust [3s.pf.ps.], U, termnas (est) [3s.pf.ps.] 'to delimit' < denom. *termn-d-;
U. termnome [acc.sg. + -en] , termnuco [abl.sg. + -kom] , termnesku [abl.pl. + -kom]
[m.] 'boundary stone' < *ter-mn-o~.
PIE *ter-mn- [n.] 'peg, post'. IE cognates: Hit. tarma- [c.], CLuw. tarma/i- 'peg,
nail' < *t(o)r(hj)-mo~, Gr. teppa [n.] 'goal, end-point', teppcov [m.] 'boundary'.
Latin termen, together with the base *ter-mon- of Venetic and *ter-mn- of Sabellic,
shows that Pit.'still had an ablauting suffix in the n-stem. From this, Latin derived
*termn-o- > terminus, with zero grade of *-mn- in view of Sabell. *ter-mn-o-. The
word termo might have been borrowed from Greek. The Hittite noun and the usage in
Latin suggest that the PIE word denoted a concrete object which came to refer to a
boundary-stone. For this reason, the etymology deriving termen from the PIE root
*terh - 'to pass, cross over' (with a mo-stem in Skt. su-tdrman- 'which provides a
2
good passage') is unattractive. The PIE root is unclear: maybe *ter~, an anit variant of
*terh 'to rub'?
r
Bibl.: WH II: 671, EM 686, IEW 1074f., Lejeune 1974: 339, Leumann 1977: 371,
Untermann 2000: 745-748, Lazzarini-Poccetti 2001, Rix 2002a: 70.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: termes, -itis 'bough cut from a tree' [m. /] (Hor.+)
No etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 670, EM 686, IEW 1070f.
terO, -ere 'to rub' [v. Ill; pf. trivi, ppp. triturn] (P1.+)
Derivatives: tritor 'who rubs or grinds' (P1.+), tritura 'rubbing, threshing' (Varro+),
terehra [f.] 'drill' (P1.+), terebrare 'to drill a hole' (P1.+), exterebrdre 'to extract with
an auger' (PL+), termentum 'sore caused by friction' (PL+), tribulum
'threshing-sledge' (Varro+; Varro trivoluwi), tribulare 'to press, squeeze' (Cato+),
triticum 'wheat' (P1.+), trfticeus 'of wheat' (Cato+); atterere 'to rub, grind, wear'
(P1.+), confer ere 'to pound to pieces, wear out, use up' (Naev.+), deterere 'to wear
down, rub off (Naev.+), detrTmentum 'harm, diminishment' (Cato+), exterere 'to
thresh out, elide' (Varro+), extermentoriutn 'linteum quod teritur coipore' (Varro),
interere 'to crumble on, up' (Cato+), intertrigo 'sore place caused by rubbing'
(Cato+), intertrimentum 'wear and tear' (Ter.+), obterere 'to crush, destroy'
(Naev.+), proeterere 'to rub away the end' (P1.+), retrimentum 'scrapings' (Varro),
subterere 'to wear away below' (P1.+); tarmes, -itis [m.] 'woodworm' (P1.+).
Pit. *tera- [pr.], *trei- [pf.], *terasro- 'drill'.
PIE *terh/*trh]- [aor.?], *trehi-i- [pr.] 'to rub'. IE cognates: Gr. teipco 'to oppress,
r
distress, weaken', -repcTpov 'drill', ppp. TpTyroi; 'bored through', Gr. xpi|3co 'to rub,
wear out'; Lith. tirti 'to investigate', SeCS treti, Is. tbrg, Ru. teret' 'to rub'; OHG
drden 'to turn'.
The Latin present is isolated among the other IE languages, who show a ie/o-present
or, in the case of Greek, the extension -Tb-. Since Latin also shows Tin the preterite, it
seems that PIE had an athematic present *terh i- / *trh ei-, which was either
r r
thematized or otherwise enlarged. The Latin pr. can therefore hardly go back to a PIE
thematic present, but at most to a root present, or, maybe more likely, a root aorist.
Latin -T- in the pf. and ppp. could go back to ...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: terra 'dry land, earth' [f. a] ( Andr.+)
Derivatives: terreus 'earthen' (Varro+), terrestris 'on/of land, earthly' (P1.+),
territorium 'territory' (VarTO+), terrenus 'of dry land, of earth' (Lucr.+); extorris
'exiled, banished' (Acc.+).
Pit. *tersa- 'earth', terso- 'area'. It. cognates: O. teras [gen.sg. oracc.pl.] 'earth'; O.
teer[um, terum [nom.sg.], tereis [gen.sg.], terei [loc.sg.] [n.] 'area' < *ters-o-.
PIE *ters-h - 'dry land'. IE cognates: Olr. tir 'territory; dry' [n.], OW tir <
2
*ters-o/es- (or *ters-r-, Thurneysen 1946: 49).
Lat. extorris goes back to *eks-t(o)rs-i~. Since -torium is a productive suffix only after
verbal stems, the rise of terri-torium is unexplained. Pit. *ters-o/a- is generally
derived from the root *ters- 'to dry out' (s.v. torred): 'dry land'. If Olr. tir, W. tir are
cognate, they represent either an s-stem *ters-os-, or a derivative in *-r-. Nussbaum
apud Livingston 2004: 56 (fn. 31) suggests that terrenus could be derived from a
loc.sg. *terrai.
Bibl.: WH II: 673fi, EM 687fi, IEW 1078, Untermann 2000: 736, 745, LIV *ters-.
—> torreo

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: terreo 'to terrorize, deter' [v. II; terruT, terriium] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: territdre 'to (try to) scare' (P1.+), terribilis 'frightening' (Enn.+),
terriculal-um 'object of terror, bogy' (Lucil.+), terror 'terror, extreme fear' (Naev.+);
terrificus 'terrifying' (Lucr.+), terrificare 'to alarm' (Lucr.+); absterrere 'to frighten
away, deter' (P1.+), deterrere 'to discourage' (P1.+), exterrere 'to scare' (Enn.+),
perterrere 'to terrify' (PI.+), perterrefacere 'to make extremely frightened' (Ter.+).
Pit. *tros-eje- 'to scare'. It. cognates: U. tuse<t>u, tursitu [3s.ipv.II], tusetutu,
tursituto [3p.ipv.II], tursiandu [3p.pr.sb.ps.] 'to scare' < *tors-eje-; U. turse, tuse,
turse [dat.sg.], tursar [gen.sg.], tursa [voc.sg.] f. name of a goddess, invoked in the
curse of foes < *torsa- 'fright'.
PIE *tros-eie- 'to make scared'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. trdsanti [3p.act.], caus.
trdsaya- 'to tremble, be afraid', YAv. draijhaua- 'to scare', tdrssa- 'to be afraid',
tarsta- 'scared, afraid', OP trsa- 'to be scared'; Gr. Tpeto 'to be scared, tremble, flee',
bxpeczoq 'fearless'; Lith. trishi 'to tremble, to shiver'.
Since the other IE languages show an ablaut *trs~ : *tres~, terreo can reflect neither
PIE *ters~ nor *tors- (with replacement of *torr- by *terr- on the model of terror, as
assumed by WH and EM). LIV compares teriius 'third' < *tritio- and reconstructs
*tros-eie- > (post-)PIt. *trseje- > Sab. *tors-eje-, Lat. *terseje- by regular phonetic
development. This seems to be the best option.
Bibl.: WH II: 674, EM 688,. IEW 1095, Nussbaum 1999a: 412f., Untermann 2000:
775-777, LIV *tres-.
t
i

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tescum 'certain augural term; wild land' [n. o] (Acc.+; n.pl. tesca Var. / tesqua Hor.
Luc, dat.abl.pl. tesquis)
The vacillation between tesc- and tesqu- points to *teskw- or *teskw-, which yielded
tesc- in the nom.acc.sg. The meaning is established by Chanut 1980 as 'terrain of
difficult acces, crags, maquis'. He argues that the use of tescum as an an augural term
originates in the designation of the unusable side slopes of the Capitol, where the
main templum was situated. The connection with Skt. tvcchyd- 'empty' is impossible,
since the root has ablaut grade I *teus-. Dunkel 2000b: 21 posits *tues-kwo~, which
would have yielded *teskwo- by dissimilation of the first labial glide; but the ablaut
and the meaning make a connection with *teus- difficult. Alternatively, one might
think of *ters-kwo- to torreo, that is, 'arid soil' vel sim.
Bibl.: WH II: 675, EM 688, IEW 1085, Chanut 1980.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: testa 'earthenware vessel, tile, sherd, shell' [f. a\ (Cato+)
Derivatives: testatim 'in fragments' (Pompon.+), testu, -Us [n.], tesium 'earthenware
pot* (Cato+), testuacium 'cake baked in an earthenware pot' (Varro), testudd 'tortoise,
shell, roof (Lucil.+), testudineus 'of a tortoise; of/with a tortoise-shell' (P1.+).
Derivation from the root *tel<;- 'to build' is unlikely for semantic reasons; also the
outcome of PIE *-tk- (if we would reconstruct a reduplicated form *te-tk-o-) in Latin
is uncertain (s seems most likely, cf. situs)', see also s.v. texo. The word testa is
probably a loanword, as words for vessels often are.
Bibl.: WH II: 675f, EM 688f, IEW 1058f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: testis 'witness' [m. /'] (PI.+)
Derivatives: testes, -ium [m.pl.] 'testicles' (P1.+), testiculi [m.pl.] 'testicle' (Varro+),
testimonium 'evidence in court, testimony' (Lex XII+), testari 'to invoke as a witness,
testify' (Lex XII+), testdmentum 'will, testament' (P1.+), intestatus 'without having
made a will, without having called a witness' (Lex XII+), intestabilis 'disqualified
from calling witnesses, shameful' (Lex XII+); antestdn 'to call as a witness' (Lex
XII+), detestatum 'testatione denuntiatum' (Lex XII), obtestarT 'to beseech, invoke'
(P1.+).
Pit. *tri-sto/i~. It. cognates: O. tristaamentud [abl.sg.] n. 'proof, loanword from
Latin *tristamentom; O. trstus [nom.pl.] 'witness' < *tristo- 'third'.
PIE *trito-sth -o- 'third standing (by)'. IE cognates: Olr. triss, tres 'third' < *tristo~.
2
The direct preform must be *tri-sth -i-, but the generally accepted meaning was 'third
2
standing (by)' > 'witness', which would rather require the ordinal *trito~. Hence,
Italo-Celtic *tristo- may be the result of haplological loss of the syllable *-to- from
the original cp. The verb testari can have been derived directly from testi-. For the use
of 'witness' in the meaning 'testicle', cf. Gr. Jtapaoraiai 5uo. Leumann 1977: 234
explains antestari from *anti-testarT.
Bibl.: WH II: 676f, EM 689, IEW 1090-92, Coleman 1992: 424, Sihler 1995: 411,
Meiser 1998: 80, Untermann 2000: 768-770, LIV *steh -. -+ tres
2

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tetricus 'frowning, stern' [adj. o/a] (Varro+)
The connection with terd 'to rub', proposed by WH and IEW, is semantically
unconvincing. Possible preforms are *tetr- and *tedr-, but there is no good
etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 677, EM 689, IEW 1071-74.
Ven. teuta [nom.sg.], teuta[m?] [acc.sg.?], [teuta]i? [dat.sg.] 'civitas'; O. touto,
TCOFTO [nom.sg.], tautam [acc.sg.], toutad [abl.sg.], Marr. toutai, totai [dat,sg.], U.
tuta, totam, tota [acc.sg.], tutas, totar [gen.sg.], tute, tote [dat.sg.], tote, toteme
[loc.sg.], tutaper, totaper [abl.sg. + -per], SPic. touta [nom.?], tutas [gen.?], toutaih
[dat.sg./pl.?] 'town, society'; Presam. TOFTIS [abl.sg. of *toutio-]; O. tuvtiks
[nom.sg.m.], TOVTIK&; [gen.sg.m.], tuvtik[ud] [abl.sg.m.], ftoutijcas [gen.sg.f.],
tuv[tikaf] [loc.sg.f.], tuv[tikad] [abl.sg.f.], toutico [nom.sg.f], touticom,
tuvftikom] [nom.acc.sg.n.], Vol. toticu [abl.sg.n.], U. totco [nom.acc.sg.n.?],
todcome [acc.sg.n. + -en] , totcor [nom.pl.n.], todceir [dat.abl.pl.n.], toce [adv.] 'of
the touta-'' .
Pit. *toutd- 'town, society', *toutiko/d- [adj.].
IE cognates: Olr. tuath 'people, tribe', W. tud 'land', Co. tus, MBret. tut 'people';
OPr. tauto, Lith. tauta, Latv. tauta; Go. piuda, OHG diot(a) , OS thiod(a) , OE deod,
OIc. pjod 'people' < PGm. *peup6-, OE gepledan 'to translate', OIc. pyda 'to
interpret, mean', Go. piudans 'king', OIc. pjodann, OE deoden, OS thiodan <
*teutono~.
A western IE word (Italo-Celtic, Germanic, Baltic). Gm. and Italic point to *-eu- and
Baltic to *-ou-, whereas Celtic cannot be decided. Beekes 1998 explains * teuta- from
a non-IE substratum language, since it cannot be derived from a known PIE root. The
word denotes a social group larger than any for which a PIE etymon is reconstructed;
furthermore, terms meaning 'people' are often loanwords.
Bibl.: IEW 1080-85, Lejeune 1974: 339, Beekes 1998, Untermann 2000: 779-783,
Lazzarini-Poccetti 2001.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: texo, -ere 'to weave, construct' [v. Ill; pf. texui, ppp. textum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: textilis 'woven, plaited' (Lucr.+), textor 'weaver' (P1.+), textrlnum
'place of weaving, of constructing' (Enn.+), textus, -us 'structure' (Lucr.+), textura
"structure, weaving' (Lucr.+); tela 'cloth on a loom, spider's web, plan' (P1.+),
subtilis [adj.] 'fine in texture, precise' (Lucr.+), subtemen [also subtegmen] 'weft,
threads in a loom' (P1.+); extexere 'to unweave' (PI.+), praetexta 'toga with a purple
border' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *tekse/o~,
PIE *te£-s- [pr,] 'to fashion'. IE cognates: Hit. taks-2' 'to devise, undertake' <
*teks-/tks-, MHG dehsen 'to break flax'; Skt. pr. tdksati [3p.act.], tddhi [3s.ipv.act.],
tasti [3s.act.], pf. tatdksa [3s.act.], ppp. tastd- 'to hammer, form, fashion', tastar- [m.]
'carpenter, master', A v. tasat [3s.aor.inj.], OA v. tost [3s.pr.inj.] 'to fashion', tasta-
'created'; YAv. auui.. . tasti [3s.pr.act.].
Lat. tela < *teks-la~, subtilis <?*subteli-. Meiser 1998 argues that texo does not belong
to the pr. *te-tk- 'to build' but to a root *tek-s~, seen in Gr. te^vr] 'skill', OHG dehsala
'axe', Olr: tal 'axe'; but the latter root may simply be *tek-sthat is, the root 'to
fashion' with an ^-suffix. Meiser 2003 points to the possible PIE formulaic expression
'weaving words' (sermones texere PL), which involves in Skt. and Gr. the verb *te-tk-
f
'to fashion'. For Latin tex-, this implies that it can either go back to PIE *tek-s~, or to
*te-tk- 'to fashion'. Since the outcome of word-intemal *-tk- is uncertain, but might
rather be single -s- (in ursus, where the cluster is preceded by r), I have a preference
for *tek-s-.
Bibl.: WH II:.6I9f., 678, EM 662, 690, IEW 1058f„ Sihler 1995: 225, Meiser 1998:
96f., 2003: 127, LIV 2.*tek-, *tetk-. tignum
i

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tibia 'reed-pipe; shin-bone' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: fibicen, -inis [m.] 'piper' (PL+), tibicina 'female piper' (PI.+).
IE cognates: Gr. auptov, -cavoq [m.] 'tube, siphon'.
The original meaning may have been either 'stalk' or 'shin-bone' (even though the
latter meaning is only attested from Pliny onwards: flutes were often made from
shin-bones). The compounds represent *iibio-kan- 'playing on a reed-pipe'. There is
no good IE etymology; maybe the word is a loanword from the same source as Gr.
auptov.
Bibl.: WH II: 680, EM 691, IEW 1102.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tignum 'piece of timber, building-wood' [n. o] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: tigillum 'small plank' (PL+).
Pit. *teg/k~no- 'plank, timber'.
PIE *(s)teg-no- 'covering' or *tek-no- 'product'.
Lat. tignum probably represents *tegno-. The derived dim. tigillum could phonetically
reflect *tegno-lo- if the raising of *e* in front of a velar nasal preceded vowel
reduction in non-initial syllable (with the inverse chronology, one would expect
*tegillum). As for the PIE etymology, WH and IEW derived tignum from a word
*(s)teg- for 'stake', which is unattractive since this root is not otherwise attested in
Italic. It seems more likely that tignum derives from *(s)teg- 'to cover', which yields
other constructional terminology such as tectum 'roof, ceiling, dwelling' and tegula
'roof-tile'. However, since tignum has no evident connotation of 'cover', one could
also connect it with *tek- 'to fashion', but this has the preform * teles- in all certain
Latin derivatives (see s.v. texd).
Bibl.: WH II: 681, EM 691, IEW 1014, Meiser 1998: 81, 122. tego, texd

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tilia 'lime-tree' [f. a] (Verg.+)
IE cognates: Gr. TixeXea 'elm-tree', Myc. fpterewal.
The only close cognate seems to be the Greek word for 'elm-tree'. Both may be
borrowings from a third language, or Latin has borrowed the Greek word as *telia
and changed its meaning to Time-tree'.
Bibl.: WH II: 68If., EM 691, IEW 847.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: timed 'to fear' [v. II; pf. timuT] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: timor [m.] 'fear' (Naev.+; timos Naev. apud Non.), timidus 'afraid,
timid' (Naev.+), timiditas 'proneness to fear' (Pac.+), timefactus 'intimidated'
(Lucr.+), extimescere 'to be alarmed, dread' (P1.+), pertimescere 'to become very
scared' (P1.+),praetimere 'to worry' (P1.+).
The connection with words for 'stiff (thus hesitatantly WH and IEW) is hardly
possible in view of the modern reconstruction of this root as *stieH- (LIV); if indeed
initial *s- were mobile, we would expect PIE *tiH-m- > Lat. *tim~.
Bibl.: WH II: 682, EM 69If.
tingO, -ere 'to wet, imbue, dye' [v. Ill; pf. tinxi, ppp. tine turn] (Lucr.+; var. tinguo
Varro)
Derivatives: attingere 'to smear on' (Rhet.Her. +), inting(u)ere 'to plunge in, colour'
(PL+).
Pit. *teng-e/o-.
PIE *teng- 'to make wet' [pr.], maybe *teng-s- [aor.]. IE cognates: Gr. leyy00 <to make
wet', aor. xey^ai, OHG thunkon, dunkon 'to immerse', Swiss German tink 'wet'.
The variant tinguo is due to analogy with unguo 'to anoint'.
Bibl.: WH II: 684, EM 692, IEW 1067, Meiser 2003: 110, LIV l.*ten(gJ-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tltio 'piece of burning wood' [m. n] (Varro+)
Pit. *tT-ti- 'heating'.
PIE *tihi-ti- IE cognates: Olr. tinaid, -tin* 'to melt, disappear' < PCI. */i-m"-, Olr.
uathad [n.] 'small quantity', MW odit [m.] 'rarity' < *au-ti-to- 'melting away'; Hit.
ze-a(ri> /z- 'to cook (intr.), be cooked' < *tieho-, zanu-" 'to cook (tr.)' < *tihneu-,
r r
zinni-2' I zinn- 'to end, finish' < *ti-n(e)-h; OE Swan 'to wet; become wet', OIc. piSr
r
'melted, thawed' < *tih to-.
r
Maybe derived from the PIE root *teihr 'to become hot, melt', according to LIV. In
that case, it would be a ti-stem 'heating'. Kloekhorst 2008: 1036f. argues that the
original meaning of the root was 'to end, finish', on account of Hit. zinni-.
Bibl.: WH H: 685f, EM 693, Schrijver 1995: 195, Schumacher 2004: 640f, LIV
*teihr.
tOfus 'volcanic rock, tufa' [m. o] (Verg.)
Pit. *tauf-o-1 It. cognates: maybe O. tavffud [abl.sg.] if'tufa'.
Probably a loanword from Sabellic, because of -f-
Bibl.: WH II: 687, EM 693, Untermann 2000: 736.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: toies, -ium 'goitre' [f.pl. i] (Fest.)
Derivatives: tdnsillae [f.pl.] 'tonsils' (Cic.+).
Pit. *ton-sli- 'goitre, tonsil'.
PIE *ton-sl-i-. IE cognates: see s.v. teneo.
jfc
Generally, idles is explained as the pi. of *tonsli- 'what is/can be stretched' (vel sim.),
and tonsillae as its diminutive *tohsli-la-. Lat. *tonsli- can be regarded as /-derivative
to *tens- 'to stretch', or as an instrument noun in *-slo/i- to *ten~ 'stretch'. It must be
pointed out that the semantic connection to 'stretch' is possible, but hardly
compelling; one might sooner expect a basic meaning 'to swell' to account for
'goitre' and 'tonsils'. Since we often find the meaning 'to expand' for derivatives of
*ten~, not for those of *tens-, the analysis *1on-sli- seems preferable. The o-grade of
the noun remains unexplained, however.
Bibl.: WH II: 688, EM 693, IEW 1068f, Leumann 1977: 208, Meiser 1998: 119,
LIV *ten-. —> teneo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tollo, -ere 'to pick up, raise, remove' [v. Ill; pf. sustull, ppp. sublatum] (Lex XII+;
pr.sb. tulat Acc.) -
Derivatives: tolerare 'to support, endure' (Andr.+), tolerabilis 'tolerant, bearable'
(Ter.+), intolerandus 'unbearable' (P1.+), pertolerdre 'to endure to the end' (Acc.+);
tolutim [adv.] 'at a trot' (PI.+), tolutilis 'moving at a trot' (Varro); abstulds 'you take
away' (PI.), attollere 'to lift, erect' (P1.+), contollere 'to step up' (PI.+), extollere 'to
raise, praise' (PI.+), protollere 'to stretch forth, prolong' (P1.+); opitulus 'who brings
help' (Paul. exF.), opitularile 'to give help' (Andr.+).
Pit. *tolna/o- [pr.], *tela-s- [pr.], *tel(-a)- [aor.], *tetol- [pf.], *tldto- [ppp.]. It.
cognates: Ven. tolar, toler, tuler [3s.pr.med.] 'offers' < *tol-e/ar(V?); Fal. tulom
[ls.pf./aor.] 'I offer' (thus Giacomelli); U. andendu, antentu, atentu [3s.ipv.II] 'to

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: lay onto', endendu, ententu [3s.ipv.II], entelust, entelus [3s.fut.pf] 'to lay into',
pertentu [3s.ipv.II] 'maybe 'to spread out', sutentu [3s.ipv.II]'?' < pr. *te!ne/o~, pf.
*telh .
r
PIE *tl-n(e)-h - [pr.] 'to carry', *tel-h - [aor.], *te-tolh - [pf.], *tlh -to- [ppp.]. IE
2 2 2 2
cognates: Olr. tlenaid*, 'tlen 'to lift, take away' < PCI. *tlina-\ Gr. aor. ET^V, Dor.
trkax 'to endure, endeavour', aor. eraXaaaa 'endeavoured', rnXavza [n.pl.] 'scales',
Go. pulan 'to endure, bear', ToB tallam 'raises'.
The PIE nasal present yielded Latin *toln- > toll-, whereas Umbrian introduced the
e-grade of the root (from the aorist). Thematization may go back to Pit. The aorist
root *tel(a)- seems to be preserved in U. pf. entel-, and is seen in Lat. sb. -tidas, -tulat
< *tel-d-. The pf. tetulT (to fero) may reflect the PIE perfect, whereas latus < *flato-
continues the PIE to-adj. The verb opitulari is regarded as denominal to opitulus,
which is only attested in Paul, ex F.; it may reflect *opi-tolH-o~. To explain tolerdre,
Nussbaum 2007b proposes a Latin sw-present built to the root *telh -, hence *telh -s-
2 2
> *telasa- > tolerd-. Finally, tolutim can be interpreted as 'lifting its feet' > 'at a trot',
and would seem to require a stem *tolu~.
Bibl.: WH II: 688f„ EM 694, IEW 1060f, Giacomelli 1963: 257, Lejeune 1974:
339, Schrijver 1991: 216, 407, 510, Untermann 2000: 742-744, Meiser 2003: 192,
Schumacher 2004: 641 f„ LIV *telh -. fero, tellus
2

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tomentum 'flock, stuffing material' [n. o] (Varro+)
Pit. *towamn?
PIE *teuh -mn [n.] 'swelling'? *touh -eie- [pr.caus.]? IE cognates: see s.v. tueor.
2 2
WH and IEW assume tomentum < *towementom to the root *teuhr 'to swell,
increase' (a caus.pr. *touH-eie- 'to stuff?). A preform *teuh2-mn would yield Pit.
*towamn > *towamen > *tdmen. A preform *towamentom would yield *tumentum,
but *towa/i- could have been restored on the model of still present verb forms (cf.
Schrijver 1991: 279-82). Thus, tomentum can be explained on the basis of earlier
*tdmen, or on the basis of verb forms. Of course, no verb continuing *teuh - is
2
actually attested in Latin, which weakens this explanation. EM hesitantly suggest a
connection with tondeo 'to shear', which is possible, but would be less direct than
from a verb meaning 'to stuff.
Bibl.: WH II: 689, EM 694, IEW 1080-85, Meiser 1998: 168. ^ tueor, turned

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tondeo 'to cut the hair, shear' [v. II; pf. totondf, ppp. tonsum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: tonsllis 'made smooth by clipping' (P1.+), tonsus, -its 'style of haircut'
(P1.+), tonsor 'barber' (P1.+), tonstrix 'female barber' (P1.+), tonstrina 'barber's shop'
(P1.+), tonsiira 'shearing, clipping' (Varro+), tonsitare 'to shear' (P1.+); attondere 'to
strip of, shear' (P1.+), detondere 'to shear, prune' (P1.+).
Pit. *tond-eje
PIE *tond-eie- 'to shear'. IE cognates: Olr. teinnid* 'to break, cut' < *tend-e/o- or
*tan-n-d-e/o~, pret. tethainn < *te~tond-, Gr. tev5to 'to gnaw at'.
The pf. totondi can be recent, or continue an old pf. *te-tond~. It cannot be proven that
the root *tend- was derived from *tem- (as in *temh 'to cut'), but the meaning
r
suggests a connection.
Bibl.: WH II: 689f„ EM 694f, IEW 1062f, Schumacher 2004: 614, LIV 2.*tend-.
tong£re 'to know' [v. II] (Paul, ex F. tongere, Enn. (apud Paul, ex F.) tongeni)
Derivatives: tongitio 'notio' (Fest: Praenestinian).
Pit. *tong-eje- 'to know', *tng-m- 'opinion'. It cognates: O. tanginom [acc.sg.],
tangineis [gen.sg.], tanginud, tanginud, tanginud, xavyivoS, xavyivot [abl.sg.]
'opinion, decision' < *tng-Tn/ion-.
PIE *tong-ei(e?)- 'to think, know'. IE cognates: Go. paglgan 'to think' <
*tong-(e)ie~, OIc.pekfga 'to understand, know', OHG denchen, OE dencan 'to think',
Go.pugkjan 'to appear, think'.
In view of the meaning which is stative rather than iterative, and in view of Gm.
*punk-i- 'to appear (to so.)', it seems possible that tongere continues a PIE /-present
with o-grade of the root.
Bibl.: WH II: 690, EM 695, IEW 1088, Untermann 2000: 733f., LIV 2.*ten(g)-.
tonO, -are 'to thunder' [v. I; pf. tonui, ppp. tonitum] (P1.+; Ip.act, tonimus Varro)
Derivatives: tonitrus, -iis [m.] 'thunder' (P1.+), tonitr(u)dlis 'of thunder' (Lucr.+),
tonescere 'to begin to thunder' (Varro); contonat Mt thunders violently' (PL); Quomne
tonas (Carmen Saliare).
Pit. *tena- [pr./aor.], *tonaje- [pr.].
PIE *(s)tenh ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: topper 'quickly' [adv.] (Andr.+)
Pit. *tod! * to til
Lat. topper is generally regarded as a unfverbation of *tod (nom.acc.sg.n. of the stem
*to- 'that') and the suffix -per. Whether the first element really was *tod remains
uncertain: in view of the meaning, initial tot 'that many' seems equally possible.
Bibl.: WH II: 692, EM 695, IEW 1086f, Leumann 1977: 476, Livingston 2004:
17-22. —* so-, tam, tot, turn
torpeO 'to be numb, paralysed' [v. II] (P1.+)
Derivatives: torpor 'numbness, drowsiness' (Lucil.-f-), torpedo 'lethargy; black ray
(fish)' (Cato+).
Pit. *torp-e- 'to be stiff.
PIE *trp-ehi- 'to be stiff. IE cognates: Lith. tirpti 'to coagulate, grow stiff, OCS
trbpeti 'to suffer'; OHG derb/p, OEpeorf, OIc. pjarfr 'unleavened' < PGm. *perba-
' stiff < *terp-o~.
Bibl.: WH II: 692, EM 696, IEW 1022-27, LIV 2.*terp-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tor queo 'to twist, wind' [v. II; pf. torsi, ppp. torturri] (P1.+)
Derivatives: torques, -is [£] 'collar of twisted metal' (P1.+), torculus 'of a wine- or
olive-press' (Cato+), torculum 'press' (Varro+), torcularium 'pressing-room, press'
(Cato+); tortivus 'obtained from pressing' (Cato+), tortare 'to torture, twist'
(Pompon.+); tormina, -drum [n.pl.] 'griping pains in the bowels' (Cato+), tormentum
'rope made of twisted hair, ballista, torture, pain' (PL+); contorquere 'to make
twisted, turn about' (Enn.+), contortor 'a twister' (Ter.+), contortiplicatus
'compounded in an involved fashion' (PL), distorquere 'to distort, torture' (Ter.+),
extorquere 'to wrench away, sprain' (PL+), extortor 'one who extorts' (Ter.),
pertorquere 'to hurl, twist strongly' (Afran.+), praetorquere 'to twist, wring' (PI.+).
Pit. *torkw-eie- 'to twist', *torkw-to- [ppp.]. It. cognates: maybe O. turumiiad
[3s.pr.sb.] 'may he tremble/suffer' (vel sim.) if denom. to a noun *tor^v-mo- 'vexed'
(but see also s.v. tremo) .
PIE *torkw-eie- 'to twist'. IE cognates: Hit. tar(k)u-zi 'to dance' (*Itarkw-f) <
*terkw-i*trk"-, Skt. tarkaya- 'to consider', ni?-tarkya- 'what can be unscrewed',
tarku- 'spindle', Khot. hataljs- 'to flutter' (< Plr. *fra-tark~) < Ilr. *tark- 'to move to
and fro, turn'; ToB tdrk- 'to turn'.
A causative verb *torkw-eie-; the noun tormentum reflects *torkw-mn-to-.
BibL: WH II: 692f, EM 696, IEW 1077, Untermann 2000: 778, LIV *terkV

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: scorched' (Lucr.), tomis (later torris) [m.] 'piece of firewood' (Acc.+), torridus
'dried, parched' (Acc.+), retorridus 'dried up, shrivelled' (Varro+).
Pit. *tors-eje 'to scorch', *tors-e- 'to be dry', *tors-to- 'scorched'.
PIE *tors-eie- 'to make dry', *trs-eh,-, *trs-to-. IE cognates: Hit. tars- 'to become
dry' or 'to make dry'? < *ters-, Skt. trsyant- [ptc.act.], aor. trsa- 'to be thirsty', root
aor. tfsana- 'thirsty', caus. vi-tar say a-, tjstci- 'sharp, stinging, raw', YAv. tarsu- 'dry,
not fluid'; Gr. tepeo (ion. 'to become dry'; OHG derren 'to make dry', durst 'thirst',
Go.paursjan* 'to be thirsty'.
As M. Weiss points out to me, Lat. torrens can be explained as a ptc. 'the dry one' to
a stative pr. *trs-eh 'to be dried out'. This obviates the need for positing a semantic
r
shift from 'burning' to 'torrential' as assumed by LIV.
Bibl.: WH II: 694, EM 696, IEW 1078f., LIV *ters-. terra

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: totus 'the whole of, all' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
The etymology *touH-eto- 'stuffed' accepted by WH is a moot possibility; the
assumed semantic shift from 'stuffed' to 'whole' is neither trivial nor convincing.
Zimmer 1985 discusses all previous attempts to etymologize totus, and finds them
unconvincing. His own solution *to-ie/ot-o~ 'sich fest dazu stellend, vollstandig' (to
PIE *iet-) is hardly more plausible: the preverb and the verbal root are otherwise
unattested in Latin (see s.v. rdtor for the alleged occurrence of this root), and the
semantic shift to 'all' is not straightforward.
Bibl.: WH II: 695f„ EM 697, IEW 1080ff., Zimmer 1985, Meiser 1998: 168, LIV
*teuh - (?).
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: trabs, -is 'tree-trunk, beam' [f. b/i] (P1.+; nom.sg. trabes Enn., Varro)
Derivatives: trabecula 'small wooden beam' (Cato+), trabica 'kind of boat' (Pac.-)-),
trabalis 'of/for wooden beams' (Enn.+).
Pit. *trab- 'wooden beam' (> Latin), *treb- 'to build, dwell' (> U.), *treb- 'house'
(> Sab.). It. cognates: O. triibum, rpeipfcop. [acc.sg.], tribud [abl.sg.] [fi] 'house' <
*treb-\ O. tribarakavum, trubarakavfum [inf.], tribarakat.tms [3p.pf.sb.],
tribarakat.tuset [3p.fut.pfi] 'to build' < *treb-ark-a- 'to be a house-builder'; 0.
tribarakkiuf [nom.sg.] 'building' < *treb-ark-idn-s. U. trebeit [3s.pr.] 'lives, dwells'
< *treb-i/e/ie-. O. tribuf [nom.sg,] 'tribunus' < *treb-d/un-s. Maybe also U. tremnu
[abl.sg.] 'place from where the augur observes the birds' (< *treb-no-?).
PIE *trb- 'wooden beam', *treb- 'to build'? IE cognates: Olr. treb, W. tref 'hamlet,
village', Lith. troba 'cottage, farmhouse', Latv. traba 'Hut, hovel', Go.paurp 'field',
OIc. porp, NHG Dorf'village'.
The nom.sg. trabes is an innovation with regard to trabs. Sabellic points to a stem
*treb- 'house, building' but it is uncertain whether *treb- and Latin trab- originally
belonged to one nominal paradigm, or whether they represent different derivatives of
a root *trb-. Schrijver 1991 argues that a possible original ablaut nom.sg. *trebs, obi.
*trb- > *torb- may have been replaced by *trebs vs. *trab-, but there are no direct
models for such a replacement; all of Schrijver's examples concern verb forms. Since
Schrijver sets up a rule according to which *RDC developed into Lat. *RaDC, trabs
might stem from a paradigm with *trb- in some case-forms. U. has a present in *treb-
'to dwell'; since the meanings of the Sab. forms are closer to this verb than to trabs,
they may have been derived from the verb. We furthermore find a noun *tr(e)b- in
Celtic, *trb- in Germanic and *trob- in Baltic. Maybe the full grade is also attested by
Sabellic names such as Lat. Trebulanus, U. trebl...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: trahd, -ere 'to pull, drag, haul' [v. Ill; pf. traxi, ppp. tractum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: trahax 'grabbing' (PL); tragula 'spear, sledge, dragnet' (P1.+); tractim
[adv.] 'in a long-drawn-out manner' (P1.+), tractum 'a thin sheet of pastry' (Cato+),
tractus, -us 'drawing, trail, track' (Lucr.+); detrahere 'to pull off, remove' (P1.+),
distrahere 'to pull apart' (P1.+), intertrahere 'to draw away' (PL); tractare 'to drag
along, handle' (Naev.+), attrectare 'to touch, handle' (P1.+), attrectatus, -us 'the
handling' (Pac.+),pertra/ectare 'to pass the handover, handle' (PI.+),pertractate 'in
a hackneyed manner' (PL); trama 'warp' (P1.+).
Pit. *tra/e/o-.
IE cognates: Olr. tethraig* [pret.] 'ran away, receded' < PCI. *te-trag~, Olr. traig
'ebb, beach', MW trei, OBret. tre (< *tragio-), MW treul 'trouble, weakness' (<
*traglo-).
The form trahax appears to be a nonce-formation of Plautus (thus EM). The g in
tragula must have been retained in *tragbla\ it can be compared with MW treul if
from *trdglo-. Long -a- in tragula might be analogical after coagulum, repagula.
Tractare was derived from the ppp. tractus. Trama can reflect *trax(s)ma-.
Semantically, the closest cognates are found in Celtic: Olr. tethraig and the words
for 'ebb' can all be explained from a meaning 'to pull away' (cf. Schumacher 2004:
636). Formally, Go. pragjan 'to walk' (and cognates) and Olr. traig, W. troed, Bret.
troad 'foot' could be cognate; semantically, they require some special pleading (but a
connection is not impossible). This would point to a root *trHgh- or *trlgh-, with a
non-IE root structure *T-Dh and maybe root-internal *-a~. Thus, this could be an
Italo-Celtic (and Germanic?) loanword. A connection with Germanic *dragan 'to
drag, draw' (Go. -dragan 'to carry', OIc. draga 'to pull, ride') is formally impossible,
unless this is another reflex of a European loanword. The connection with Ir. trog
'offspring', SCr. trag 'family, race' is dismissed by Schrijver 1991: ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: trans 'across, through' [prep. +acc., adv.] (P1.+; tra- in front of d-J-, /-, m-, n-, v-)
Pit. *tran(t)s. It. cognates: U. trahaf, tra [prep. + loc.], traha, traf, tra [prep. + acc.]
'on the other side of; U. trah- prefix.
PIE *trh -nt-s t'crossing' [nom.sg.m/f.]. IE cognates: MW tardu, MCo. tardha,
2
OBret. tardom 'to spring up, burst out' < PCI. *tarie/o- < *trh -ie-; Hit. tarhu-' 'to
2
prevail, be able' < *terh -u-ti, *trh u-enti, dTarhunna-, CLuw. ATarhuuant- /
2 r
Tarhunt-, HLuw. Tarhunt-1 Tarhunza-, Lyc. Trqqnt- 'the Storm-God' < *trh -uent-s,
2
*trh -unt-os; Skt. pr. tar a-, -tira 'to pass, overcome', taranta- [m.] PN, turva- 'to
2
overcome', YAv. titara-, tauruuaiia- 'id.', Gr. xpavrjc; *'pervasive' —> 'clear,
distinct'.
Since U. has /traf /, the final cluster must have been Pit. *-ns, which cannot be
directly derived from PIE *-nt (pace Schrijver). Thus, we must assume a m./f. form of
a pr.ptc.act. *trh nts 'crossing' > Pit. *trdn(t)s 'across'. Dunkel 2000b: 24 posits
2
*trh m-skwe, an instrumental in his view, but in view of the meaning I would rather
2
expect an acc. - which agrees with the ending *-m.
Bibl.: WH II: 700, EM 699f., IEW 1075f., Steinbauer 1989: 236f., Schrijver 1991:
224, Untermann 2000: 757f„ LIV *terh -. -> intro
2
tremO, -ere 'to tremble' [v. Ill; pf. fremiti] (P1.+; tremonti [3p.pr.?] Carmen Saliare)
Derivatives: tremor 'trembling, terror' (P1.+), tremulus 'trembling, shaking' (P1.+),
tremebundus 'id.' (Lucr.+), tremescere 'to,tremble' (Lucr.+); contremere 'to tremble
violently' (Pac.+), contremulus 'shimmering' (Varro), contremescere 'to shake
violently' (Enn.+).
Pit. *trem-e/o- [pr.], *trom-eje- [pr.caus.]. It. cognates: U. tremitu [3s.ipv.II] maybe
'to scare' < *trem-eie-t5d, with -e- from the present.; O. turumiiad [3s.pr.sb.] 'may
he tremble/suffer' (vel sim.) (but see also s.v. torqueo).
PIE *tr(e)m- [pr.] 'to tremble'. IE cognates: Gr. Tpepto 'to tremble', Lith. tremti 'to
shake up', trimti 'to tremble...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: trepidus 'fearful, anxious' [adj. o/a] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: trepidare 'to act in a state of alarm, be anxious, hurry' (P1.+), trepidulus
'trembling with apprehension' (Enn.+); aftrepidare 'to bestir oneself (P1.+); trepit
'turns' (Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *trep~.
PIE *trep- 'to tremble'. IE cognates: OCS trepetb 'trembling, fear', trepetati 'to
tremble'.
The appurtenance of trepit here is uncertain: it might be a nonce-formation based on
Gr. TpeTtEi 'turns', or it might go back to PIE *trep- 'to turn', but have nothing to do
with trepidus. In fact, neither the root *trep- 'to tread, crush' nor *trep- 'to turn',
which are acknowledged for PIE by LIV, are obvious candidates for the etymology of
trepidus. In view of its meaning, Latin-Slavic *trep- 'tremble' may rather be
compared with PIE *trem- (see s.v. tremo) and *tres- 'to tremble'.
Bibl.: WH II: 701f, EM 700f, IEW 1094, Nussbaum 1999a: 380, LIV l.*trep-. ->
tremo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tres 'three' [num. adj. m.f. tres, n. tria] (VOLat. (Garigliano Bowl trifos [abl.pl.m.]),
Lex XII+; acc.mf. tris, gen. trium, dat.abl. tribus; tri~, tri- 'three' in compounds)
Derivatives: ter tins 'third' (Lex XII, Andr.+), tertiarius [adj.] 'one-third' (Cato+),
tertiare 'to plough/attempt for the third time' (Cato+); ter 'thrice' (P1.+); term 'three
at a time' (P1.+), trim 'three at a time, three' (Cato+), trlginta 'thirty' (P1.+), trecentl
[pi.] 'three hundred' (P1.+), trecenT 'three hundred each' (Varro+), triens, -ntis [m.] 'a
third part' (Varro+), trientabulum 'property in the value of a third of a debt' (Lex
Agraria+); terruncius 'coin weighing three unciae' (P1.+).
Pit. nom.m.f. *trejes, acc.m.f. *trins, nom.acc.n. *tria, dat.abl.pl. *trifos 'three',
*tritio- 'third', *tris 'thrice'. It. cognates: maybe the Ven. theonym tribusiiati-
contains *tri- 'three'; O. tris [nom.pl.f.], tris [acc.pl.f.], trium [gen.pl.], U. trif, tref,
tre, trif, treif [acc.pl.m.], tref, tre, trif [acc.pl.f.], triia [acc.pl.n.], triiuper, trioper
[ac.pl.n. + -per(t)] , tris [abl.pl.] 'three'; U. terti, tertim [acc.sg.m.n.?], tertiama
[acc.sg.f. + -ad] , tertiame [acc.sg.f. + -en] , tertiu, tertiu, tertio [abl.sg.m.n.?], tertie
[loc.sg.m.n.] 'third' < *tri-tio-; U. tripler [abl.pl.m.] 'three at a time' < *tri-plo~. U.
tribfifu [nom.sg.], tribrisine [abl.sg.] f. maybe 'three, trio' < *tri- + ?
PIE nom.m.f. *treies, acc.m.f. *trins, nom.acc.n. *trih , gen. *triom 'three', *trio-
2
(» *trito- » *tritio-) [adj.] 'third', *tris 'thrice', *tri- (in compounds),
*trih dlcomth 'thirty'. IE cognates: Olr. tri, tri [m.], tre [n.], teoir [f.], W. Bret, tri
2 2
[m.], teir [f.] 'three'; Skt. trdyas [nom.pl.m.], tisras [f.], tri [nom.acc.pl.n.] 'three',
trimsat- [f.] 'thirty', tftiya- 'third', YAv. tfraiio, draiias-ca [nom.pl.m.], tisro
[nom.pl.f.], 19ri [nom.acc.pl.n.] 'three', drisant- [f.] 'thirty', dritiia- 'third', drisuua-
[n.] 'one third'; Skt. tris, YAv. iJris 'thr...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tribus 'division of the people, tribe' [f. w] (Varro+)
Derivatives: tribuere 'to divide, bestow' (Acc.+), tribunus 'magistrate, commander'
(P1.+), tributus 'levy, tax' (P1-+), tribiilis 'fellow tribesman' (Ter.+), tributim 'by
tribes' (Varro+); attribuere 'to assign, appoint' (Varro+), retribuere 'to hand back
duly' (Lucr.+). ?
Pit. *triPu- 'tripartite entity, trinity'. It. cognates: U. trifu, trifo [acc.sg.], trifor
[gen.sg.], trifo [dat.sg.], trefiper [abl.sg. + -per] 'trinity, district'.
PIE *tri-dhhu- 'tripartite division'.
r
Weiss (2007a: 369-376) bases his account of the etymology of tribus on a PIE or Pit.
adjectival compound *tri-cth o- 'rendered in three, tripartite', which could be
r
compared e.g. with Skt. multiplicatives in -dhd < *-dheh such as tridha 'threefold',
r
and with thematized derivatives of it such as Lith. indas 'plate' < *-dhho-. The adj.
r
*tricfhiO- would have been substantivized by conversion to a w-stem *tri-c?h u-
r
'tripartite thing', 'a third', yielding Pit. *tripu- (the w-stem points to a rather old
formation; cf. Lith. vidits 'middle'). Since we have no other certain evidence for the
development of Pit. intervocalic *p in front of vocalic *u, we may as well assume that
it is identical to the development after *w, where it yields Latin -6-. Hence, Pit.
*tripu- would regularly yield U. trifu- and Latin *tribu~. Unlike in tertius and ter,
*tri- was not reduced to ter- because it was supported by other compounds in *tri~.
Bibl.: WH II: 703f, EM 702, IEW 1090-92, Meiser 1998: 194, Untermann 2000:
764f, Weiss 2007a. —> -fariam, tres

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: trlstis 'depressed, unhappy' [adj. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: trlstitas 'gloom' (Pac.+), tristitia 'unhappiness, gloom' (Ter.+).
IE cognates: OHG drlsti, OS thrlsti, OE driste 'audacious'?
Hill 2003: 128 considers the possibility that trlstis goes back to *trenksti- and is
cognate with the Gm. words such as OE thriste. But in view of terno < *tenksmdn-,
the raising of *e > i post-dates the cluster simplification, so that *trenksti~ could at
most yield *tresti~. No good other etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 706fi, EM 703, IEW 1092.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: trudo, -ere 'to thrust, push' [v. Ill; trusl, trusum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: (mola) trusatilis [adj.] 'pushing-mill' (Cato+); contrudere 'to thrust,
cram' (Lucr.+), detrudere 'to push away, compel' (P1.+), obtrudere 'to thrust, push
down the throat' (P1.+), retrudere 'to thrust back' (P1.+).
Pit. *treude/o~.
PIE *treud-(e/o-) 'to push, thrust'. IE cognates: W. cythrudd 'to vex', Lith. triusas
'work, effort, pains', OCS trudb 'labour, work', SCr. trud 'effort, labour, work' <
*troud-o-\ Go. us-priutan 'to maltreat', OIc. prjota 'to lack', OHG -driozan 'to cause
sorrow'; Alb. tredh 'castrates' (uncertain).
The Latin verb can be compared to Gm. *preutan, suggesting a thematic verb with
e-grade.
Bibl.: WH II: 710, EM 704, IEW 1095f., LIV *treud-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: trux, -cis 'harsh, savage' [adj. k] (P1.+)
Derivatives: truculentus 'ferocious' (P1.+), trucidare 'to slaughter, butcher' (Lucr.+),
trucidatio 'slaughter' (Cato+).
IE cognates: MW drwg, MCo. drok, Mir. droch- 'bad' < PCI. *dntko-.
If we follow Hamp 1978 in positing PIE *dr- > Latin tr- also in initial syllable, trux
can be connected with PCI. *druko- 'bad'. The verb trucidare has been variously
explained as a haplologized form of *truki-fddare, a denominal verb to a compound
*truki-kaido- 'hacking savagely' (to caedo); or from *kwtru-kaidare 'hacking in four
pieces', with the reduced form of 'four'. Yet a form *k}Vtra- 'four' is unattested
elswhere in Latin (in itself not really a counterargument), and the meaning of
underlying *kwtru-kaid-o- would be a '(having) four hackings'; why 'to slaughter'
was expressed by means of the numeral 'four' would remain unexplained. Hence, I
derive trucidare from *truk(o)-kaid-o~.
Bibl.: WH II: 709, 711, EM 704f, IEW 1102, Schrijver 1991:496. ^ caedo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: i1) tueor 'to look at, watch over' [v. II; ppp. tuitum, tutum] (P1.+; var. tuor, tui Enn,
'jij'" Lucr.)
Derivatives: tutor 'guardian' (P1-+), tutare/i 'to watch over, protect* (Naev.+),
(;[ Tutanus 'deity giving protection' (Varro+), Tutiixna 'goddess giving protection'
. (Varro+), tiitela 'guardianship, protection' (P1.+); contueri [also contuT PI. to Lucr.]
'to look at, see' (P1.+), contuitus, -us 'gaze' (P1.+), indotuetur '?' (Enn. Ann. 70),
intueri [also intui] 'to look at, consider' (P1.+), obtu(er)T 'to look at' (P1.+).
• I: • Pit, *-tow-e- 'to protect'.
PIE *touh -eie- [pr.caus.]? *te-touh - [pf.]? IE cognates: Skt. pr. ut taviti, pf. tutava
3 2
\, 'to be strong', tavas- 'strong, powerful', OAv. tauua [ls.pr.sb.act.], YAv, fra-tuiia
, i [2s.pr.opt.act.] 'to be able', tuuant- 'capable, competent'; tutauua [pf.] 'is possible';
Gr. xaxk 'great, much' (Hsch.), ctbq, oaoq 'safe and sound' (< *tueh us, *tuh u-os)\
2 r
Lith. tiikti 'to become fat (of cattle)', RuCS tyti 'to become fat'.
Tutus is the old ppp. belonging to tuor (whence tutare, tutor). Tuor and tueor might
reflect earlier *tu(w) V-, or *tewV~, *towV- in non-initial syllable. In view of the old
derivatives, the original meaning was probably 'to watch over, protect', whence 'to
look at' in the compound verbs (see Gamier 2004: 282f.). This renders a derivation
from the root *tuh 'to be strong' attractive: *teuH-ti 'is strong' > 'keeps strong,
r
protects' (thus Gamier 2004: 284, in which case tuor is older), pr.caus. *touH-eie-
'keeps strong, protects' (> *tawe-), or a pf. *(te-)touh -e 'is strong' > 'protects', cf.
2
the Ilr. pf. of this root. The pr. tueor could reflect the introduction of the pr. or pf.
vocalism *tow- into the caus. *tawe~.
Bibl.: WH II: 713f., EM 706, IEW 1079f., Leumann 1977: 544, LIV ?*teuH-. ->
tomentum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: effective', YAv. tumaspana- [PN]; Lith. turned 'to become thick', OIc. pumal-fingr
'thumb'.
The noun tumultus is explained by Leumann 1977: 354 from *tumulitus to a verb
*tumulare, which seems unwarranted. WH II: 716 suggest imitation of singultus
'sighing', but a clear-cut proportion cannot be set up, since there is no verb * singed.
Nevertheless, the meaning of tumultus does suggest that it belongs here. Tumeo goes
back to a root *tum~, which has a closely similar meaning to the root *tuH- 'to swell'.
The short vowel in turn- is explained by some from an o-grade form *touH-m- with de
Saussure's effect, but since we find turn- also in Ilr., Baltic and Celtic, this would
i
require a PIE generalization of a novel laryngealless ablaut variant *tum-, which
seems a lot to ask for. Alternatively, one might think that *tu-h and *tu-m- are two
r
different suffixations of one and the same root *tu- (cf. *gwm- and *gwh ~ 'to come').
2
Bibl.: WH II: 715f, EM 707, IEW 1080-85, Schrijver 1991: 340, 530, Schumacher
2004: 646, LIV *tuem-. —* •contumax, contumelia

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tundo, -ere 'to strike, beat' [v. Ill; pf. tutudi\ ppp. tunsum (tusum)] (P1.+)
Derivatives: tudes, -itis [m.] 'hammer, mallet' (Paul, ex F.), tuditare 'to strike
repeatedly with a hammer' (Enn.+), tudiculdre 'to crush with a small hammer'
(Varro); contundere 'to crush, bruise' (P1.+), detudes [acc.pl.] 'a piece struck off
(Paul, ex F.) extundere 'to extort, wring' (P1.+), obtundere 'to beat, assail, make
t
blunt' (PI.+), pertundere 'to bore a hole through' (P1.+), retundere 'to beat flat,
repress' (P1.+).
Pit. *(s)tund-(e/o~) 'to thrust'. It. cognates: maybe U. tuder [acc.sg.], tuderor
[nom.pl.], tudero [acc.pl.], tuderus [abl.pl.] [n.] 'border' < *tud-es-.
PIE *(s)tu-n-d- [pr.] 'to thrust'. IE cognates: Olr. do-tuii, arch, di-tuitt 'to crumble,
fall' < PCI. *-tud-e/o~, MW kystud ',fate'; Skt. pr. tuddtr, pr. tundate, tundana-
[ptc.med.], pf. (-)tutoda 'to thrust, crush', tunna- 'crushed, ground', Sogd. twnt /tund/
'violent', MP tund 'sharp, violent'; Arm. t'ndam 'I am shattered', Go. stautan 'to
thrust' (?).
With Schumacher 2004: 645, we can interpret U. tuder as the place where two areas
'hit' each other, that is, border on each other.
Bibl.: WH II: 716f., EM 707, IEW 1032-34, Untermann 2000: 77If., Meiser 2003:
192f., Schumacher 2004: 642-645, LIV l.*(s)teud-. studeo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: turba 'commotion, upheaval' [f. a\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: (1) turbulentus 'disturbed, stormy' (P1.+), turbelae [f.pl.] 'petty
disturbance' (P1.+), twrbdre 'to upset, agitate, revolt' (P1.+), turbo, -inis 'spinning-top,
eddy, whirlwind' (P1.+), turbidus 'wild, disordered, murky' (P1.+); conturbare 'to
confound, dismay' (P1.+), disturbdre 'to demolish, upset' (P1.+), obturbdre 'to
interrupt, dazzle' (P1-+), perturbdre 'to upset, disrupt' (P1.+); (2) turma 'small
squadron, company' (Cato+), turmatim 'in troops' (Lucr.+).
(1) The connection with Skt. tvarate 'to hurry' and PIE *tuer-, which IEW and
Meiser adhere to, seems very uncertain. Not so much semantically, but formally: the
suffix (*bl *bhl) would have been added to the bare root, of which no other forms
occur in Italic. There is also no known PIE root *(s)terbh~ with the appropriate
meaning. One might think of Gr. otpecpco 'to turn': a zero-grade form *(s)ifbh~h -
2
'turmoil' > Pit. *trf-a- > pre-Latin *torbd- > *torba, with a dialectal variant turba
(normally, -orb- remains, cf. orbis, morbus, with p torpeo). Yet there are no other
known cognates of Gr. oip&pco, and - as indicated - Latin turba would be deviant for
*torba. Turba seems most similar to Gr. ouppti, Att. tuppi] 'noise, commotion',
cTuppa, Att. TDppa [adv.] 'confused' (< PGr. *kiurb-7 *tuorb-l), which are probably
loanwords. In that case, Latin would have borrowed the word from a Greek dialect, or
both Greek and Latin borrowed it from a third source. In view of the quite
well-developed word family already in Plautus, which suggests that turba had been in
the language for some time, the latter option seems preferable. (2) The noun turma
may or may not be related to turba. If related, it might point to both words being
loanwords, with b and m as different renderings of a labial consonant in the donor
language. Turma is compared by IEW to OIc. pruma, Prymr 'noise, racket', OE
drymm 'troop, crowd', OS heru-thrum 'sword-violence'...

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: turdus 'thrush (or similar bird)' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: turdarium 'enclosure for thrushes' (Varro), turdelice 'magpie (?)'
(Varro).
Pit. *torzdo~.
PIE *(s)tr(o)sdho- 'thrush'. IE cognates: Olr. truit, -d 'thrush' [f.] < *trozdi-, W.
tresglen; Ami. tordik; OPr. tresde 'thrush', Lith. strazdas, Latv. strazds 'thrush,
blackbird, (dial.) starling', Ru. drozd, gen.sg. drozdd 'thrush', cernyj drozd
'blackbird' < BS1. *(s)trozdo- < PIE *(s)trozdho-; OIc. prgstr 'thrush' [m.] (<
*prastu- < *t?-osd(h>-), OHG drosela, OS throsla, MHG drostel, OE prostle (<
*prustld-), OE drysce, MoE thrush (< *pruskjon).
Since *or and *r merge in pre-Latin, turdus could reflect *torzdo- or *trzd-; but since
Celtic, BS1. and Gm. have *-ro~, chances are that turdus continues *tpzdo~. The
hypothesis by Bracchi (1999: 87) that *t(o)rzdo- would reflect a compound *trs-sd-
'sitting (nestling) on the dried (mud)' has little to recommend itself. If thrushes make
their nests from mud and twigs, it seems to me that their nests are less prone to be
called 'dry' than the nests of birds who weave only twigs, without mud.
Bibl.: WH II: 718, EM 708, IEW 1096, Hamp 1978: 188, 1981b: 81, Meiser 1998:
63, Bracchi 1999. —* sturnus
turgeO 'to swell' [v. II; pf. tursi\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: turgescere 'to begin to swell' (P1.+), turgidus 'swollen' (P1.+),
obturgescere 'to begin to swell' (Lucil.+).
The meaning renders a connection with -turare 'to block, impede' possible. A derived
verb from an adj. *tur-ago- 'swelling' (as given by WH as one possible etymology)
seems unlikely, since such adj; always yield verbs in -igare. No clear etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 718f, EM 708, IEW 1080-85. -> obturo

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: turpis 'disgusting, foul' [adj. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: turpiculus 'somewhat indecent' (Varro+), turpitudo 'ugliness,
indecency' (P1.+), turpdre 'to pollute, disgrace' (Enn.+).
WH and IEW compare Epic Skt. trapate 'to be ashamed' from PIE *trep- 'to turn',
and interpret turpis as 'repelling'. This is too constructed a meaning to be credible:
one would expect a different suffix to express the deontic meaning here implied.
*T(o)rp-i- could mean 'turning', which is not 'turning away', which is not 'making
so. turn away from sth.', which is not necessarily 'disgusting'. One would sooner
connect torpeo 'to be numb' or 'stiff, but this too does not yield a smooth
explanation for the semantics of turpis.
Bibl.: WH II: 719, EM 708, IEW 1094.
