content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: tussis 'cough' [f. /] (Ter.+)
Derivatives: tussTre 'to suffer from a cough' (PI.+).
Semantically, the connection with tundo 'to beat' is conceivable, but since the ppp. of
tundo is tusum < *tud-to-, with vowel lengthening according to Lachmann's Law, we
would expect *tusis from an old formation *tud-ti-. Tussis might be explained from
onomatopoeic consonant gemination in an earlier form *tusis. Alternatively, it was
not derived from tundo at all but represents a pure onomatopoeia. Note that the PIE
root *k'veh s- 'to cough' also ends in -s-.
2
Bibl.: WH II: 721, EM 709, IEW 1032-34, Meiser 1998: 80.
u
Qber, -ris 'breast, udder' [n. r] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: uher, -eris [adj.] 'abundant, rich' (PL+), ubertas 'abundance' (Enn.+);
exuberare 'to flow copiously, be abundant' (Acc.+).
Pit. *oup-r~.
PIE *h,ouHdh-r 'udder', *h euHdf,-n-s? IE cognates: Skt. udhar I idhan- [n.]
1
'udder' < Ilr. *HuHctar-, Gr. otnSap, -axoc; [n.], Ru. vymja, gen.sg. vymeni 'udder' <
PS1. *vym?, Ru. udit' 'to ripen, fill (with)' < PIE *HouHdh-?; OHG utrin [dat.sg.],
MHG iiter, inter, OS OE uder [n.] 'udder' < OIcju(g)r, OE ieder, OFr. idder
< *euct~.
According to Schrijver, *HuHdh- would have become *wap~, whereas *He/ouHdh- >
*owaP~ would have yielded *ud-. Hence, we must posit the loss of the second
laryngeal before it could vocalize. For this loss to happen, the root must have had
o-grade. The adjective fiber is mostly explained from the use of the noun as an
adjective (as in vetus 'old' < *uetos 'year'), but Sihier posits an i-stem *ouct-ri-
' abundant'.
Bibl.: WHII: 738fi, EM 715, IEW 347, Schrijver 1991: 327fi, Sihier 1995: 301, 353.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ubl 'where' [adv.] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: alicubi 'somewhere' (P1.+), nuncubi 'whether...anywhere' (Varro+),
necubi 'that nowhere' (Varro+), sicubi 'if anywhere' (Cato+); ubfcumque 'wherever'
(P1.+), ubinam 'where in the world?' (P1.+), ubiquaque 'everywhere' (PI.+), ubique
'anywhere' (Lucil.+), ubivis 'whenever, anywhere' (Ter.+).
Pit. *kwu-p/fei 'where'. It. cognates: O. puf, U. pufe, pufe [adv.] 'where' <
*kwu-b/dhei.
PIE *kwu-dhi/-bhi 'where'. IE cognates: Skt. kitha, OAv. kudd 'where', YAv. kuSat
'where from', OCS kbde 'where, when' < *kwu-dhe.
Theoretically, ubi can reflect *kwubhei or *kwu<^ei. The suffix *-bhi would be the PIE
ins. ending, whereas *-dhei could be an Italic innovation for *kwu-dhe 'where' as
encountered in Ilr. and Slavic. In view of the latter correspondence, a preform Pit,
*kwupei seems slightly more likely. Hit. ku-wa-pi 'where, when' reflects *kwo-bhi, cf.
Kloekhorst 2008: 490.
Bibl.: WH II: 739, EM 715fi, IEW 644-648, Leumann 1977: 149f„ Schrijver 1991:
262, Sihier 1995: 399, Meiser 1998: 99, Untermann 2000: 594. -»• -de (for unde), ut

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ulclscor, -sci 'to take revenge' [v. Ill; ppp. ultum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ultor 'avenger; person avenged' (Acc.+), imiltus 'unpunished,
unavenged' (P1.+); ulld 'I will have avenged' (Acc.).
Pit. *ol-k- 'to destroy, take revenge'.
PIE *h elh[- [aor.] 'to perish'. IE cognates: see s.v. deled.
3
The form ullo is generally reconstructed as *ulso < *ulk-$-o\ this would imply that
extant -Is- in other forms, e.g. the pf. falsi to fulcire, is secondary. The ppp. ultus can
reflect *ulk-to-. LIV's etymology as *h elk- 'to ward off (to Gr. aAxfj 'resistance,
2
courage') is semantically uncompelling. The traditional etymology is a derivation of
ulcus 'ulcer', assuming a semantic development 'to grow sores (against someone)' >
'avenge one's griefs'. This is not compelling either (cf. Keller 1992: 257). Schrijver is
reluctant to accept an equation with Gr. OXEKCO 'to destroy', a ^-enlargement to the
root *h lh; but semantically, this seems the best option. PIE *h lhk- would yield
3 r 3 r
Lat. *lak- according to Schrijver's rules (1991: 304ff.), but *h olh,-k- > *h olk- (with
3 3
de Saussure's effect) would yield ulc-. Since a PIE o-grade is not easy to justify in a
preform *h3olhk-, one could assume *h elh > *-ol- with a more recent addition of
r 3 r
*-k-.
Bibl.: WH II: 810f, EM 743, IEW 310, Schrijver 1991: 70, LIV *h elk-.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ulcus, -eris 'sore, ulcer' [n. r] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ulcerdsus 'full of ulcers' (P1-+); exulcerare 'to make sore, wound'
(Varro+).
Pit. *elkos.
PIE *h]elk-os 'ulcer'. IE cognates: Skt. drsas- [n.] 'hemorroids', Khot. asT 'itch',
Sogd. "rsx'rs 'nx' 'hemorroids', Gr. £XKOC ;[n.] 'wound, ulcer'. Less certain: Olr. olc
'bad' < *h olk-o~, OIc. Mr 'bad' < PGm. *elh-ila-.
}
Pit. *elkos regularly developed into *olkos and then ulcus.
Bibl.: WH II: 811, EM 743f, IEW 310, Schrijver 1991: 37, Stiiber 2002: 190f, LIV
?*h,elk-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ulmus 'elm-tree, elm-wood' [f. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ulmeus 'of elms, of elm-woord' (P1.+); ulmitriba [m,] 'one who wears
out elms'(PI.). 4
Pit. *e/olmo-.
IE cognates: Olr. lem 'elm' < PCI. *limo-, Gaul. Lemo-uices [ethnonym] > Limoges,
W. llwyfen 'elm' < PCI. *lemo- < PIE *h,l(e)im-; Ru. il'em 'common elm', tt'm
'wych elm', gen.sg. il'ma, Cz. jilm, jilma 'elm' < PS1. *jblbm-b < PIE *h]l-mo- (?);
OHG elmboum, MoE elm, OIc. almr (NHG Ulme, MoDu. olm borrowed from Latin
and Old French).
Ulmus can reflect Pit. *elmos > *olmos > ulmus (cf. Leumann 1977: 47). The restricted
distribution (Italo-Celtic, Gm, Slavic) and the difficulty to reconstruct a PIE form
suggest non-IE origin. Schrijver 1997a posits two forms *V-lm- (> Gm, Lat.) versus
*lVm- (Celtic). The Slavic word may also reflect the former, if it is not a loan from
Germanic. In theory, one may try to derive PCI. *limo- and Slavic from a zero grade
PIE *(H)jmowith secondary *leimo- developing in British Celtic. But this would still
imply a PIE ablaut *hielmo-, *hiolmo~, *hilmo~, which is hardly credible.
Bibl.: WH II: 81 If., EM 744, IEW 302-304, Schrijver 1991: 66, 1997: 311.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ulna 'forearm' [f. a\ (Catullus+)
Pit. *olena- 'forearm'.
PIE *Heh l-en- (or *HoHl-en-) or *Hh -el-en- (or *HHol-en-) 'elbow'. IE cognates:
3 3
Olr. itilen 'angle', W. elin, Bret, ilin < PCI. *o/<?«-; Skt. aratni- [m.] 'elbow, ell',
YAv. fraradni.drajah- [n.] 'length of an ell', aradna [du.] 'two elbows', OP arasnis
[acc.pl.] 'ell', Gr. GAEVT] [f.], cbXr|v, -evoq [f.] 'elbow, underarm', oiAXov 'elbow'
(Hsch.) (< *dl-n-om), OPr. woaltis 'ell', woltis 'forearm', Lith. uolektis; Latv. uolelcts
'ell' < *HoHl-ek-ti-, OPr. alkunis, Lith. alkune, elkune (arch, dial.) 'elbow', Latv.
§lkuonis 'elbow' < *HHol-k-, OCS lakbtb [m. i], Ru. lokof 'id.' < *HHolkuti-; OHG
el(i)na [f.] 'ell', Go. aleina < *ol-en-; ToB alyiye 'palm' < PTo. *alen~.
Many languages show an o-stem, but Ilr. and BS1. have different suffixes. Also, there
is ablaut *ol- (acute) vs. *al- (circumflex) within BS1. Hence, the word may have
been a PIE /-stem *Heh -l- (or *HoH-l-), *Hh -eI-, to which an n-stem was made
3 3
already in PIE. It is unclear whether this n-stem followed the regular ablaut rules of
PIE H-stems (as Schrijver assumes), or whether different n-stems were made to
different ablaut forms of the /-stem. The preform *ol-in- shared by Italo-Celtic and
Gm. could have been built on a stem *Hh -el- (or *HH-ol-), or be explained from
3
pretonic shortening of a long vowel in front of a resonant (Dybo's shortening: *5len-
> *olen-).
Bibl.: WH II: 812, EM 744, IEW 307-309, Lubotsky 1990: 131f, Schrijver 1991:
78, 352.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: uls 'on the far side of, beyond' [prep. + acc.] (Varro, Gel, Paul, ex F.)
Derivatives: ulterior (P1.+) 'farther away'; ultro 'to a point farther off, conversely'
(Naev.+); ultra (Cato+) 'on the far side, beyond'; ultimus 'farthest away' (P1.+).
Pit. *ol-tero*ol-tamo-. It. cognates: O. ultiumam [acc.sg.f.] 'last'.
PIE *h ol-tero- 'further', *h ol-tmHo- 'furthest, last'. IE cognates: see s.v. olle.
2 2
The prep, uls is rare, and probably not ancient. According to Leumann 1977; 220, uls
was formed to ultra on the example of cis versus citra. The forms ultra, ultro and
ulterior presuppose a stem *ultero- < *ol-tero- 'more to that side', and ultimus <
*ol-tamo- < *ol-tmHo- 'most to that side'. These words contain the same root as
olle/ollus > ille.
Bibl.: WH II: 813, EM 744, IEW 24-26, Schrijver 1991: 68, Untermann 2000: 794f.
—*• olle / ollus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ululo, -are 'to howl' [v. I] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: ulula 'the tawny owl' (Varro+).
IE cognates: Skt. uluka- [m.] 'owl', Gr. uXaco, utaxKieco 'to bark', Lith. ulula bangos
'the waves are howling', OHG ula 'owl'.
Onomatopoeic verb for a w-like sound. Possibly, *w/- or *ulul- was already PIE, but
this cannot be proven.
Bibl.: WH II: 813f„ EM 744f„ IEW 1105.

metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: umbilicus 'navel, centre' [m. o] (P1.+)
Pit. *omb-(e/o)l- » *omb-elo- (or *ombi- » *ombi-lo~).
PIE *h nbh-(e/o)l- 'navel'. IE cognates: Olr. imbliu 'navel' < PCI. *embli-ion- < PIE
3
*h nbh-li-; Skt. nabhya- [n.] 'hub (of wheel)', ndbhi- [f.] 'hub, centre, navel (of the
3
body or the world), origin, relationship, family', YAv. nabdnazdista- 'next-of-kin',
nafa- [m.] 'navel, origin, blood relationship', MP nafag 'navel', nqf 'family' < Pllr.
*Hnabh-, *Hnabh ia-; Gr. 6pcpaA.6<; [m.] 'navel, navel string; knob, center'; OPr. nab is
'hub, navel', Latv. naba 'navel', OHG naba, OE nafu, OIc. «p/'hub', OHG nabalo,
nabitlo, napulo, OE nabula, nafela, OIc. nafli 'navel' < PGm. *nabulon~.
Latin umbilicus has a complex suffix, which in theory can be explained in several
ways. In view of the /-suffixes in Celtic, Greek and Gm., it seems likely that
umbilicus too contains an original /-stem. This was then thematized to *-(e)lo-, after
which the suffix *-iko- was added.
Bibl.: WH II: 814, EM 745, IEW 314f., Schrijver 1991:61f., Meiser 1998:107. umbo

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: umbo 'boss (of a shield); protuberance' [m. «] (Enn.)
Pit. *omb-n~.
PIE *h nbh-n- 'hub, navel'. IE cognates: OHG amban, ambon [m.], OS ambon
3
[acc.pl.] 'belly' < PGm. *amban- < *h e/onbh-n-.
3
Either an Italic formation oh the basis of a PIE root noun *h nbh-, or a shared
3
innovation with Germanic. i
Bibl.: WH II: 814, EM 745, IEW 314f., Schrijver 1991: 61 f. -> umbilicus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: umbra 'shadow, shade' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: umbraticus 'that lives in the shade' (P1.+), umbraticulus 'lazy-bones'
(PI.), umbraculum 'shelter, shade' (Varro+), umbrdre 'to cast a shadow' (Varro+);
umbrifer 'shady' (Varro+).
IE cognates: Lith. unksmi, paiinksme 'shade', unksne = paunksne, itnksna 'shade',
maybe Latv. uksme 'hiding-place'.
Generally connected with Lith. unksna 'shade', and reconstructed as *unksra~. This is
the only word for which a cluster *-nksr- is reconstructed, but in view of temo <
*tensm- < *tenksmon, it is conceivable that *unksrd- would have yielded > *unsra- >
*unfra- > umbra. This etymology suggests that the root was *(H)unk- (full grade
*(H)uenk~), but the only root of that form in LIV is *uenk- 'to bend', which does not
seem a likely semantic candidate. No other root etymology.
Bibl.: WH II: 814fi, EM 745, IEW 1173f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: umeo 'to be wet' [v. II] (Verg.+)
Derivatives: umidus 'wet, moist' (Pac.+), umiditas 'moisture' (P1.+), umor
'moisture, fluid' (P1.+), inumigdre 'to make wet' (Andr.), iimectus 'moist, wet'
(Cato+), umectare 'to make wet' (Lucr.+); utigo 'waterlogged ground, marsh'
(Cato+), uliginosus 'ill-drained' (Varro+).
Pit. *umo- 'wet'.
PIE *uhmo- 'wet'. IE cognates: see s.v. urina-.
r
WH and EM derive iim- from an extension in *-(s)m- to the root *u(e/o)gw- seen in
Lat. uvidus, Gr. uypoc; 'wet', etc. Weiss 1994 rejects this for semantic reasons, but to
me the semantic difference between ifoidus and derivatives on the one hand, and
umidus and derivatives on the other, does not seem large enough to warrant their
separation. The formal objections are bigger: the root *ugw~ is not well-established,
being based mainly on Germanic forms (OIc. vgkr 'wet'). Weiss therefore derives
Umidus and umor from an adj. *uh mo~, which he also sees in Lith. iimas 'quick',
r
dial, 'fresh, not dried out'. If urTna and Skt. v&r- 'water' are also derived from this
root, it provides a solid etymological basis for *uh moThe appurtenance of idigd is
r
uncertain: according to Weiss (p. 145), ullgo was probably created analogically to
umidus and *umo- on the model of juligo. Yet we cannot exclude that uligo was
grafted on *uwidus > udus, with a change of intervocalic *-d- > -/-.
Bibl.: WH II: 815, EM 745, IEW 1118, Weiss 1995: 143f. uvidus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: umerus 'shoulder' [m. o] (P1.+)
Pit. *omes-o-. It. cognates: U. uze, onse [loc.sg.] lontsel 'shoulder' < *omes(e)i.
PIE *h om-es- 'shoulder'. IE cognates: Skt. amsa- [m.] 'shoulder', Oss. (Iron) on
2
'shoulder-blade' < PIr. *anha~, Gr. to|io<; [m.] 'shoulder, armpit' < *omso-; Arm. ows,
Go. amsans [acc.pl.], ToA es [m.], ToB antse [m.] 'shoulder'.
Hit. anass(a)- 'lower part of the back' cannot be connected with this reconstructed
word for 'shoulder' (Kloekhorst 2008: 178).
Bibl.: WH II: 815, EM 746, IEW 778, Schrijver 1991: 51, Meiser 1998: 83,
Untermann 2000: 798.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: umquam 'ever' [adv.] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: nunquam 'never' (Andr.+).
Pit. *kwam.
PIE *kwom.
Probably from *kwum + -quam, *kwum replacing *kwom 'when' [acc.sg.] (see s.v.
cum) on the model of ubi < *kwu-; a similar replacement took place in uter «
*kwo-tero-, see s.v.
Bibl.: WH II: 821, EM 746, IEW 644-648, Leumann 1977: 482. cum, usquam

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: uncus 'hook' [m. o) (Cato+)
Derivatives: uncus 'curved, hooked' adj. (Lucr.+), aduncus 'id.' (Enn.+); inuncare
'to catch on a hook' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *onko-.
PIE *h onk-o- 'hook'. IE cognates: Skt. ankd- [m.] 'hook, clamp', Gr. 6yico<; 'barb
2
of an arrow', 0710] 'angle, comer' (Hsch.), Lith. (dial.) anka [f.] 'loop, noose, ring',
Latv. (dial.) anka 'line used to fasten a sail to the mast' (connection uncertain on
accentological and semantic grounds), SeCS gkotb [f.] 'hook', ORu. ukotb [f.] 'claw,
anchor' < PS1. *gkotb.
Bibl.: WH II: 816, EM 746, IEW 45-47, Schrijver 1991: 51, LIV *h enk-. -> ancus
2
unguo
641

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: unda 'wave, sea (P1.+), river, running water (Acc.+)' [f. a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: undare 'to rise in waves, gush' (PI.+); abundare 'to overflow, be
plentiful' (P1.+).
Pit. *uddr, *udn-. It. cognates: U. utur [acc.sg.], une [abl.sg.] 'water' <*uddr, *udn-.
PIE *ud-or, *ud-n- 'water'. IE cognates: Olr. itisce [m.] 'water' < PCI.
*uden-sk-io~, Hit. yatar / uiten- [n.] 'water' < *uod-r, *ud-en-, Skt. udan- [n.], Gr.
u5©p, -CLTO^ [n.] Alb. uje, Lith. vanduo, -ens, OCS voda, Go. wato [n.] (dat.pl.
f
watnam) , OIc. vain, OS watar, OHG wazzar 'water', ToA war, ToB war < PTo.
*war (probably < PIE *udrom).
The r-stem nom.acc.n. has survived in U. utur. The w-stem from the oblique cases
survives in une and has probably been anticipated in Latin *ud-n- > *und-n~. The
a-stem inflection and the diverging meaning 'wave' may point to an original
collective *ud-n-om 'body of water', pi. *udna, which was reinterpreted as an
individualized singular 'wave'.
Bibl.: WH II: 816-818, EM 746, IEW 78-81, Meiser 1986: 94, Schrijver 1991: 501,
Untermann 2000: 815f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: unguis 'finger-nail, claw' [m. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ungiticulus 'finger- or toenail, tip' (P1.+), imgula 'hoof, claw' (P1.+).
Pit. *ongu(-i)-, *ongla~. > ;
PIE *h ngh-u- 'nail', *h ngh-t~b 'nail'. IE cognates: 6lr. ingen [f.], MW ewin, Bret.
3 3 r
ivin 'nail'-< *h ngh-u-, Gr. ovu^ -uxo<; 'nail, claw, hoof [m.], Arm. elowngn 'nail',
3
OPr. nage 'foot', Lith. nagas 'nail, claw', naga 'hoof, OCS noga, Ru. noga 'foot,
leg' < h nogh-h -; OHG nagal, OE ncegel 'nail', Go. ganagljan 'to nail on'.
3 2
The PIE w-stem was remade into an i-stem in Latin.
Bibl.: WH II: 818f, EM 747, IEW 780, Schrijver 1991: 62f„ Sihler 1995: 97.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ungulus 'ring (on the finger)' [m. o] (Pac. Plin. Paul. exF.)
Derivatives: ungustus 'crooked stick' (Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *ongeto-:
PIE *h ong-elo- 'angle'. IE cognates: see s.v. angulvs.
2
These words are close in meaning to angulus 'corner' and uncus 'hook'. WH regards
ungulus as a diminutive to uncus, which would have acquired -ng- under the influence
of ungula 'nail'. This cannot be proven or falsified, but there is some internal
evidence that the word was unusual to the Romans: Pliny says that the priscT called a
ring (amihim) unguium, whereas Festus calls it an Oscan word. Alternatively, ungulus
could represent a preform *ongelo~ ablauting with angulus. The noun ungustus recalls
the adjectives in -us-tus, but we have no noun *o/ungos- 'corner'.
Bibl.: WH II: 819, EM 747, IEW 47, Schrijver 1991: 51. angulus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: unguo, -ere 'to smear, grease' [v. Ill; pf. imxl, ppp. unctum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: unguen [n.] 'fat, grease' (Cato+), unguentum 'ointment' (P1.+),
unguentatus 'anointed' (P1.+), unguentarius 'concerned with ointments' (P1.+),
unguitare 'to be in the habit of smearing' (Cato); unctor 'anointer' (P1.+), unctid
'anointing' (P1.+), unctulus 'anointed' (Varro+), unctiusculus 'a little oilier' (PI.),
unctitare 'to anoint habitually' (P1.+); exungui 'to anoint away' (PI.).
Pit. *ongw-e~ 'to anoint', *ongw-en- 'cKntment'. It. cognates: U. umtu [3s.ipv.II] 'to
anoint' < *ombetdd, U. umen [acc.sg.], umne [abl.sg.] 'ointment' < *omben <
*ong en.
PIE *h engw-e/o- [pr.] 'to anoint', *h engw-n [nom.acc.], *h ngw-en-s [gen.] 'fat,
3 3 3
butter' [n.]. IE cognates: Olr. imb, MW ymen-yn, Bret, amann 'butter' < *h ngw-n;
3
Skt. pr. cmdkti, ahjdnti [act.], ankte, anjate [med.] 'to anoint, smear', anjas- [n.]
'anointment', YAv. viidxti- [f.] 'adornment, make-up'; Arm. awcanem 'I anoint';
OPr. anctan, OHG ancho 'butter'.
The thematic present of Pit. may directly reflect a PIE thematic present, but it might
also be built on an earlier nasal present *h n-ne-gw-ti / *h n-n-gw-enti, with
3 3
simplification of the zero grade to *h ngw-. The n. noun shows full grade of the root
3
in Gm., and zero grade in Italo-Celtic.
Bibl.: WH II: 819f„ EM 747, IEW 779, Klingenschmitt 1982: 1801, Schrijver 1991:
50, 62, 1995: 351, Meiser 1998: 100, 2003: 119, Untermann 2000: 796-98, LIV
*h engy- —> omentum
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: one twelfth' (Ter.+), deunx, -ncis [m.] 'eleven-twelfths' (Varro+).
Pit. *oino-.
PIE *Hoi-no- 'one'. IE cognates: Olr. den, W. un, Gr. oivi) 'one (at dice)', OPr.
ainan [acc.s.], Lith. vienas, Latv. viens 'one' < *HoiHno-, OCS im> 'some(one),
other', Ru. inoj 'different, other' < *HiHno-; Go. ains < PIE *Hoi-no-; Skt. eka- <
*Hoi-ko-, A v. aeuua-, OP aiva- 'one' < *Hoi-uo-.
Lat. iiHus < *oinlo- < *oinolo-, nullus < *ne-oino-lo~. Most IE forms can go back to
* Hoi-no- 'one', but the acute intonation in Slavic points to *H(o)iH-no-.
Bibl.: WH II: 815f., 821-823, EM 746, 748f., IEW 281-286, Schrijver 1991: 52,
Coleman 1992: 389f., Meiser 1998: 168.
-uo, -uere 'to put on/off [v. Ill; pf. -ui, ppp. -utum] (P1.+)
Derivatives: exuere 'to take off, strip (a garment, skin), to set free' (P1.+), induere
'to put on, clothe; adopt' (P1.+); exuviae [f.pl.] 'spoils, stripped skin' (P1.+), induviae
[f.pl.] 'garment' (PI., Gel.), reduvia 'a loose piece of cuticle round the nail' (Cic.+),
reduviosus 'full of hangnails' (Laev.+), redivTvus 'second-hand' (Cic.+); indutus, -its
[m.] 'the putting on' (Var.+); indutilis 'that can be put on, detachable' (Cato);
indumentum 'garment' (Gauius Bassus+); inducula 'woman's garment' (PI.),
subucula 'under-tunic' (Varro+).
Pit. pr. *ow-e/o~. It. cognates: U. anouihimu [3s.ipv.II.ps.] 'to put on' < prev. an- +
*owe~. Untermann 2000 suggests that it may be a denominative to a verbal noun with
the same suffix as in Lat. exuviae, induviae.
PIE *h eu-e/o- 'to adorn, wear'. IE cognates: Hit. unu-z' 'to adorn, decorate, lay (the
3
table)' < *h u-neu-; Av. aoflra- 'shoes', Arm. haganim 'to put on', Lith. auti, 3s.
3
auna, 3s.pret. ave, OCS obuti 'to put on footwear' < PS1. *obuti < BS1. *(-)outei.
Compounds of preverbs plus the verb -uo 'to put on'. The ppp. -utus is more likely to
be a productive derivative of the present stem than to reflect the PIE zero grade
*-HBU-to- with laryngeal metathesis. Similarly for the nominal fo...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: upupa 'hoopoe; kind of pickaxe' [f. a] (Varro+; PI.)
IE cognates: Gr. enonoT TTOTIQTIO 'cry of the hoopoe', ETROY, -OJIOI; 'hoopoe', Arm.
popop, Po. hupek, LG Hupphupp, OHG wituhopfo, OS widohoppa 'hoopoe'.
Onomatopoeic word, found in many languages in a similar form.
Bibl.: WH II: 837, EM 754, IEW 325.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: urbs 'city' [f. i] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: urbanus 'of the city' (P1.+).
Pit. *worf-(i~) (> urbs), *werf~a- (> uerfale). It. cognates: U. uerfale 'area for taking
auspices' [loc. or abl.sg.?] < *werf-ali- [n.].
PIE *u(o)rbh-(i-) 'enclosure'. IE cognates: Hit. uarpa- [n.] 'enclosure', uarpae-' 'to
suppress, conquer' < *uorP-o-; ToA warpi, ToB werwiye 'garden' < *uorP-o-, ToA
warp- 'to surround', ToB warp- 'to enclose'.
The three most frequent pre-2000 etymologies (connecting urbs with PIE *ghordh-o-
'enclosure', Latin orbis 'circle', and PIE *bhrgh- 'fortress', respectively) are dismissed
by Driessen 2001, who presents a novel etymology (foreshadowed by a remark in
Meiser 1998). Basing himself on the meaning of U. uerfale, he hypothesizes that urbs
may originally have referred to an 'enclosed area for taking auspices' (p. 50), which
gradually came to indicate the inhabited settlement when this extended more and
more across the original templum. Latin urbs may phonogically reflect PIE
*u(o)rbh/ct'-(i-), whereas U. uerfale can continue *uerbh/<t-h. Since Hittite and
r
Tocharian contain words of the form *uVrP- meaning 'to enclose', 'garden', Driessen
decides that urbs is from a root *u(e/o)rbh~.
Bibl.: WH II: 838, EM 754, Meiser 1998: 64, Untermann 1000: 843fi, Driessen
2001: 60-66.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: urgeo 'to press, push' [v. II; pf. ursT] (P1.+)
Derivatives: exurgere 'to squeeze out* (P1.+).
Pit. *worg-eje/o-. «
PIE *u(o)rgh-eie-? IE cognates: Lith. verzti, 3s. verzia 'to string, tighten, squeeze',
CS otbvresti, Is. otbvrbzg 'to untie*, Ru. otverzt' (poet.) 'to open', OHG wurgen 'to
strangle', OS wurgill, OIc. virgill 'noose'.
LIV assumes that urged belongs to a root *ureg- 'to follow a track', which via 'to
hunt down' would have developed into 'to press, push'. While not completely
inconceivable, this semantic shift seems far-fetched. Note furthermore that the oldest
authors (PI.) use urged in a more literal sense of 'to weigh down on', from which 'to
insist' and 'to impel' are more easily derived. I therefore agree with Schrijver 1991,
who connects urged with BS1. and Gm. words for 'to tie, bind'. Latin could continue
the zero-grade of the root, or possibly an o-grade causative or iterative. If correct, this
etymology would show that PIE *-rgh- yields Latin -rg-.
Bibl.: WH II: 839, EM 754f, IEW 1181, Schrijver 1991: 76, LIV *ureg-, *uergh-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: urina 'urine' [f. a\ (Varro, Cat.+)
Derivatives: urindri 'to plunge under water, dive' (Varro, Cic.+), urmdtor 'a diver'
(Varro, Liv.+).
Pit. *ur-ino- 'watery'.
PIE *u(e/o)hr [n.] 'water'. IE cognates: Olr. fir 'milk', OW gwir(-awt) 'strong
r
drink' < *ueh]r-6- 'milky', CLuw. uar 'water', Skt. var- [n.] 'water' (often disyllabic
in RV), YAv. vara- 'rain' (m.) < Pllr. *uaHr- < PIE *ue/ohr; OIc. itr [n.] 'drizzle'
r
—> yra [w.v.l] 'drizzle' < PGm. *iira-\ OIc. urigr, OE urig < PGm. *uraga- 'dewy';
OIc. aurr 'wet', OE ear 'sea' < PGm. *aura-.
The meaning of the verb shows that 'urine' is a secondary development; the older
word for 'to piss' is meiio, mingo, apart from onomatopoeia like *pisso. According to
WH, the meaning 'urine' of urina may have been influenced by Gr. otipov 'urine'.
The original meaning must then have been 'water' vel sim. Leumann 1977: 552 and
328 regards iirina as a back-formation to the verb, which would be a latinization of
Gr. oupeiv 'to piss'. Yet this does not explain the meaning 'to dive' which the Latin
verb has. Oleson 1976 points out that divers typically suffer diuresis (they produce
more urine) while diving, and that this may explain the meaning 'diver' of urinator.
In that case, 'urine' is indeed the oldest meaning of iirina. Oleson surmises that the
Greek verb was borrowed into Latin via Etruscan, where it lost its original *w-. But if
the verb was borrowed after the fifth century from Attic, this is not necessary. If urina
'water' is old, it presupposes an adj. * ur-mo- 'watery' formed from a noun *ur(o)~
'water'. Similarly Vine 1999c. If the root of urina was indeed *uh (not *uhir-), we
r
find two PIE r-stem neuters meaning 'water', viz. *uod-r and *ue/ohr. These look
r
very similar, and we know that *h can result from *d in anteconsonantal position, as
t
in *dkmtom 'hundred', *-dkomth '-ty' and *dui-dhh 'to divide'.
2 r
Bibl.: WH II: 840, EM 755, IEW 78-81, Oleson 1976.
urO, -ere 'to bum, scorch' [v. Ill...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ursus 'bear' [m. o] (Quinctius Atta+)
Pit. *orsso~?
PIE *h rtko- 'bear'. IE cognates: Olr. art [m.] 'bear, hero, warrior', W. arth, OBret.
2
Ard~, Arth-, Bret, arz, Gaul. Artio [Theonym] < PCI. *arto- 'bear', Hit. hartakka- [c.]
'bear', Skt. fksa- [m.], YAv. arsa-, Khot. arrd, MoP xirs 'bear' < Ilr. *Hrtca-, Gr.
apKtoq 'bear', also 'Ursa maior', Arm. arj, Alb. ari [m.] 'bear'.
The cluster -rs- must be the result of the PIE constellation *-rtlc- that we can
reconstruct on the basis of the other IE branches. Latin ursus instead of *orsus was
explained by Kortlandt 1983c: 12 from analogy with urcare 'to roar'; of course, this
is just a guess. But even *orsus itself would be unexpected: *h rC- normally yields
2
arC-. Thus, some kind of tabooistic distortion probably affected the word for 'bear'.
If the word was subject to PIE ablaut, the preform might have been *h ortko-, but this
2
would be completely isolated compared to the cognate words for 'bear'.
Bibl.: WH II: 842, EM 755, IEW 875, Schrijver 1991: 68-72, Beekes 1995: 134,
Sihler 1995: 225, Meiser 1998: 106.
urvum / urbum 'curved part of a plough' [n. o] (Varro+)
Derivatives: urvare 'circumdare', 'to mark out boundaries by means of a furrow'
(Enn. apud Fest, Sex. Pomponius).
Pit. *worw-or 'plough' or 'furrow'? It. cognates: O. uruvu [nom.sg.f.] 'broad' or
'belonging to the boundary' < *urva.
PIE *u(o)ru-o-?
The etymology of urvum is disputed. The original meaning may have been 'plough'
(thus Rix 1995b, Driessen) or 'furrow', and it has mostly been compared with Gr.
6po<; [m.], oupo<; (II.), oppoq (Core.), wpoq (Cret., Arg.), opo^ (Herakl.), Myc. wo-wo
Iworwosl 'boundary, term, limit' < PGr. *uoruo~, which itself can be derived from
epuco 'to draw, tear'. The semantic shift 'drawing' > 'boundary' would thus have a
parallel in Greek. The actual preform of urvum may have been *worw-o- 'furrow',
*worw-o- 'plough' or even *urw-o- 'plow' (thus Driessen). The appurtenance of O.
uruvu is uncerta...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: usque 'up to, as far as' [adv. (prep.)] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: usquequaque 'everywhere, always' (P1.+).
Pit. *iiskH'e. It. cognates: Ven. u [prep.] < *ud 'out, for'.
PIE *(H)Od(s) 'up, out'. IE cognates: PCI. *ud~, *uss- 'out' < PIE *ud-, *uds-\ Skt.
ucca 'from above, upwards', YAv. usca [adv.] 'above', uskat [adj.] 'high, high up' <
*(H)ud-(s)kwe; Skt. ud, Av. us, uz, OP ud- 'up, out'; OCS Ru. Cz. vy- < PS1. *vy- <
*(H)ud-; Go. ut, OE ut, OHG uz, NHG cms 'out' < PGm. *ud, Go. us (uz-, ur-), OIc.
or, or-, or-, or-, OE or-, OHG ur, ar, ir 'out, out of < *uz < *uds-.
The length of the initial vowel is uncertain: the Romance languages seem to continue
variants with short and long vowel. Since shortening is in this position more
understandable than lengthening, this speaks for usque. Long u may be due to
Lachmann's Law; but it might also reflect PIE *ud, a variant of *ud which is attested
in Germanic and Slavic. Dunkel 2000b proposes *ud-skwe for usque and Av. usca,
which was secondarily remade into instrumentals or ablatives in Ilr. Skt. ucca is in
Dunkel's view from *ud-kwe, with lengthening of Ilr. *-a by analogy with the instr. in
-cd of adj. in -(y)anc. For usque, the analysis may also be *uds-kwe, in view of PCI.
*uss~ 'out' < *ud-s.
Bibl.: WH II: 844, EM 756, IEW 1103f., Russell 1988. -> uterus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ut, uti 'how, as, so that' [adv., cj.] (uti Lex XII+, ut Andr.+)
Derivatives: utinam 'if only' (P1.+), utique 'absolutely, certainly' (Varro+), utpote
'as one might expect' (P1.+), utut 'however' (P1.+).
Pit. *knutV(s) 'how'. It. cognates: O. puz, pus, pous, U. puze, puse, pusi, pusei
'that' [+ sb,], 'as' < *kwuti-s (O.), *kwuti-s-T.
PIE *kwu- 'where, how'.
Whereas Sabellic points to *kwutVs (Untermann probably posits *-i- because of Lat.
uti), Latin uti may reflect *kwutei or *kwuti. The latter form might have -i in analogy
to qui 'how' (see s.v. qui, quae, quod). The original stem may then have been
*k,vu-tos, with *-tos 'from' as in intus and Skt. kutcis 'where from?'; the semantic shift
from interrogative to relative is trivial. But other options are conceivable, e.g. *kwu-ti
'how?' with *-ti as in et < *eti and aut < *auti.
Bibl.: WH II: 846, EM 756f., IEW 644-648, Sihler 1995: 399, Untermann 2000:
627f. —» ubi, usquam, uter

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: uter, utra, utrum 'which of the two' [adj. o/a] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: uterque 'each of the two' (Naev.+), utrimque [adv.] 'on both sides'
(Enn.+), utrimquesecus 'on both sides' (Cato+), utrinde 'from either side' (Cato),
utervls 'whichever of the two you please' (Ter.+), utrubi 'in which of two places?'
(Naev.+), utrubique 'in both places, on both sides' (P1.+), neuter 'not one nor the
other, neither' (P1.+), neutrubi 'in neither place' (PL).
Pit. *kwotero- (» Lat. *kwutero~). It. cognates: O. puterelpid [loc.sg.m.],
puturuspid [nom.pl.m.], puturufmpid [gen.pl.m.J, U. putrespe [gen.sg.?] 'both' <
*potero-kwid.
PIE *kwo-tero- 'which (of two)'. IE cognates: Skt. katara-, Av. katara- 'which of
two', YAv. kataras-cit 'each of the two', Gr. rcoiepoq, Lith. katras 'which', OCS
kotorb(i) , koterbi 'which, what', Ru. kotoryj, Go. fvafiar 'which', OHG hwedar
'who, which from two', MoE whether.
Inherited PIE *kwo-tero- 'which of two' is regularly reflected in Sab. *potero~, but
has been replaced by *kwutero- in Latin on the model of the interrogative stem *kwu-
in ubl, unde, ut.
Bibl.: WH II: 845, EM 757, IEW 644-648, Untermann 2000: 625.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: uter, utris 'leather bag' [m. i] (Varro-t-; nom.pl.m utria in Lucil.)
Pit. *ud-ri-.
PIE *ud-r-i- 'water vessel'. IE cognates: Olr. otre 'goatskin bottle', Gr. uSpia
'water-vessel'.
EM consider this a possible borrowing from Greek, via Etruscan. They assume this
trajectory because of tr, but in our view, tr can have developed regularly within Latin
from *dr. In that case, uter may be an inherited word *udri- 'water vessel'.
Bibl.: WH II: 845, EM 757, IEW 78-81, Sommer 1914: 226, Sihier 1995: 212,
Hilmarsson 1993. —• unda, vitrum

OPr, weders 'stomach, belly', Lith. \4deras, vidaras [m.] 'sausage; intestines,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: stomach, lower abdomen', Latv. [m.] vfdars 'belly', OCS vedro 'barrel', Ru. vedro <
PSI. *vedrd [n.] 'bucket' < *(H)ued-ero- 'belonging to the belly'.
WH suggest that *uderos may have analogically become *uteros when *udris became
* utris phonetically. But according to Hilmarsson 1993: 215, tr can have arisen in the
oblique cases where syncope took place in front of a long vowel: *uderT > *udrT >
*utri Semantically, the shift from 'outer part' or 'protruding' to 'belly' seems to be
PIE.
Bibl.: WH II: 846, EM 757, IEW 1104fi, Lejeune 1974: 339, Hamp 1978: 190,
Hilmarsson 1993: 214f. —• usque, ve(n)sica, venter

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: utor, utl 'to use' [v. Ill; ppp. usum] (P1.+; oitile CIL 586, oeti (2x) 'utT' CIL I2 756,
Furfo, oesus 'usus' Cic.)
Derivatives: iitilis 'useful' (P1.+), inutilis 'useless' (P1.+), iitibilis 'serviceable' (PL+),
utilitas 'quality of being useful' (P1.+), utensilis 'that can be made use of (Varro+);
usus, -its 'use, need, value' (P1.+), usura 'use, enjoyment; interest' (P1.+), usurarius
'provided on loan' (P1.+), usurpdre 'to 4arry out, make use of, take possession of
(P1.+), inusitatus 'unusual' (Lucil.+); abuti 'to use up, exploit, abuse' (P1.+).
Pit. *oit-e/o- 'to use', *oisso- 'used*. It. cognates: Pael. oisa [ppp. abl.sg.f.] 'used' <
*oisso-; O. uittiuf [nom.sg.] f. 'use' < *oit-ion-s.
PIE *h3eit- 'to take along'? IE cognates: CLuw. hizza(i)- 'to fetch' < *h eit-s-e/o-;
3
Gr. oioco, -opai 'I will fetch, take along', oitrroc; 'bearable'.
Tichy 2002 argues that Pit. *oit- 'to use' may have developed from 'fetch (in one's
own interest)' to 'use'. In that case, it might be cognate to Gr. olo- < *oiss~, which
Tichy explains as a possible ^-present *oit-s~. This theory is attractive. Melchert
2007b adds Luwian hizza(i)- to the dossier, which was previously translated as 'to
bring', but rather means 'to fetch'.
Bibl.: WH II: 847f, EM 757f., IEW 293-297, Untermann 2000: 790f„ LIV ?*h eit-.
3

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: uva 'bunch of grapes; raisins' [f. a] (Lucil,+)
Pit. *oiwa~.
PIE *h]OiH-uh - 'kind of tree, probably with berries'? IE cognates: Gr. orj, 6a
2
'elderberry tree' < *oiua; Arm. aygi 'vine'; Gaul, ivo-, Lith. leva, Latv. ieva
'bird-cherry'; Ru. iva 'willow' < PS1. *jbva; PGm. *iwa- 'yew' < hieiH-uh -.
2
We find different tree-names which in Latin, Greek and Armenian go back to *oiwa~,
in BS1. and Germ, to *eiwa~. A PIE origin is difficult because of the root ablaut. This,
in combination with the semantic field of tree names, may point to a loanword from a
non-IE language.
Bibl.: WH II: 849, EM 758, IEW 297f, Malloiy-Adams 1997: 654.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: uvidus 'wet, soaked' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: uvor 'moisture' (Varro), uvescere 'to become wet' (Lucr.+), udus
'liquid, wet, drunken' (Varro+), udor 'moisture' (Varro).
IE cognates: Gr. vrypoq 'wet' < *ugro- < *ugw-ro~, OIc. vgkr 'wet' < *uogw-o~.
The order of attestation suggests that udus is a recent contraction of uvidus, although
Weiss 1995 reckons with the possibility that udus was older, and that uvidus was
back-formed to it as a hyperarchaism. Lat. udus might reflect *we/oglv-ipo~, *ugw-ipo-
or *uh ipo~. Alternatively, Weiss suggests that uvidus acquired u- from its close
r
semantic cognate umidus, thus hiding an earlier stage *uwidos. In any case, uvor
(hapax) and uvescere (rare) were formed secondarily to uvidus.
Bibl.: WH II: 849, EM 758, IEW 1118, Leumann 1977: 330, Weiss 1995: 141ff,
LIV *uegV —» umidus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: uxor, -oris 'wife' [f. r] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: uxorius 'of a wife' (P1.+), uxorcula 'little wife' (P1.+), uxorculare 'to
play the part of a wife' (PI.).
Pit. *uksor-. It. cognates: Fal. uxor, uxo [nom.sg.] 'wife'.
WH explain uxor as *uks-or- 'who is being inseminated' to Skt. uksan- 'bull', which
we can safely dismiss. Moussy 1980 assumes *ugh-sor- 'convey-woman', which is
impossible morphologically and far-fetched semantically. Mastrelli 1980 and van
Windekens 1984 assume that uxor simply continues PIE m. *uks-on~, with a semantic
shift from 'ox' to 'wife', explained by van Windekens from the wish to indicate the
married woman who had to work hard. Final *-n would have been replaced by *-r.
This is another fanciful proposal than can be dismissed.
Bibl.: WH II: 849f, EM 758f, IEW 1118, Giacomelli 1963: 259, Moussy 1980, van
Windekens 1984.
V

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vacca 'cow' [f. a] (Varro+)
Has been connected with Skt. vasa 'cow' < *uek-eh (if PIE), but Latin would require
2
o-grade, and Schrijver has established that *o > a only occurred in open syllables.
Thus, the connection cannot be maintained unless one is prepared to reconstruct Pit.
*waka- which somehow acquired geminate *-kk-. Mulier 1926 suggests a preform
*vaka and connects Skt. vasati 'roars' and Lat. vagid. -If we posit the (onomatopoeic?)
root as *ueh > vacca could be analyzed as *va-ka- 'the bellowing one'.
r
Bibl.: WH II: 111, EM 710, IEW 1111, Schrijver 1991: 463. ^ vagid

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vaco, -are 'to be empty' [v. I] (P1.+; also voco PI.)
Derivatives: vacuus 'empty, hollow' (Pi.; vocuus inscr.), vocivus 'vacant, free' (Pi,
Ter.) (yacTvus Gell.), vacTvitas 'state of being empty' (PI.), supervacaneus 'additional,
extra' (Cato+), vacdtio 'exemption' (Cic.+; vocdtio inscr. 123 BC+); vacefieri 'to
become empty' (Lucr.).
Pit. *wako- 'empty'. It. cognates: U. anter.vakaze, ander.uacose [3s.pr.sb.ps.?] 'to
interrupt' < *-vakato-; U. uasetom est, uasetom est, uaseto est [3s.pf.ps.n.],
va^etumise [supin. + 'to go'], uasetome fust [supin. + ~en + 'to go'] probably 'to
omit, err' < *uaketo-7; maybe U. uas [nom.sg.] 'mistake' < *uakos, *udtis, *uaktis
vel sim.
PIE *hiuh -ko-.
2
The forms in voc- are due to a change vac- > voc- in pretonic syllable (Sommer 1914:
110). For Pit, we can reconstruct an adj. *walw- 'empty', on which the verbs
*wak-aje-, *wak-e- 'to be empty' and the adj. *wako-wo- were built. Nussbaum
1998a reconstructs the root as *hiueh -.
2
Bibl.: WH II: 723, EM 710, IEW 345f., Leumann 1977: 50, Schrijver 1991: 307f.,
Untermann 2000: 11 If., 820f., 825, LIV *hueh -. vanus, vastus
t 2
I
vadO, -ere 'to advance, proceed' [v. Ill, ppp. -vasurn] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: evadere 'to escape, end up, pass' (P1.+); vadum 'shallow water, ford'
(P1.+) (vadus Varro et al.).
Pit. *wape/o- [v.], *wapo- [n.].
PIE *ueh dh- [v.] ' to advance', *uh d -o- [n.]. IE cognates: W, go-di-wawd
2 2
'overtook' < *ueh cf'-, OHG watan, OFr. wada, OIc. vada 'to wade ' < *uhjcOIc.
2
vad, OE weed [n.] 'water, lake', OE gewced, OHG wat 'ford' < *wada~.
Since vadare is rare and evidently denominal to vadum, we must concentrate on
vadum and vadere. The short vowel of vadum matches that of the Germanic verbs,
whereas the long a of vadere corresponds with Welsh -aw-. This suggests a PIE root
present with ablaut e : zero, and possibly the presence of a derivative *uh dh-o- in PIE
2
already.
Bibl.: WH II: 723f., EM 7I0f„ IEW 1109, Schrijver 1991: 170, 339, Meis...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vae 'alas!' [interj.] (P1.+)
Pit. *wai.
IE cognates: Mir.fae, W. gwae 'alas!'; Av. vaiioi, auudi, duuoiia 'alas!', voiia- 'cry
of distress' < *uai, *a-uai(-a), Go. w ai, OIc. vei, vce, OHG OS we, OE wa 'woe!', Go.
wajamerjan 'to slander', waidedja 'criminal', OHG wewo, wewa 'woe, pain', OS we,
gen. wewes, OIc. vce, ve 'woe, pain'.
Probably all independent onomatopoeic forms, although shared inheritance cannot be
excluded.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vafer, -fra, -frum 'clever' [adj. o/a] (Afran.+)
Derivatives: vabrum 'varium, multiformem' as a variant of vafrum (gloss.).
If the gloss form vabrum is trustworthy, it could be the original Roman form, vafer
being a borrowing from a neighbouring dialect (cf. biifo). Lat. *vaber could regularly
reflect Pit. *wasro- 'versatile', cognate with varius 'variegated' which could reflect
*was-io~. Another possible etymology would be *wap-ro- 'moving along' to vado 'to
proceed'.
Bibl.: WH II: 724f., EM 711.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vagina 'sheath, scabbard' [f. a\ (Varro+)
Pit. *wag-ma-?
If cognate with Lith. vozti 'to cover', the original meaning of vagina would be
'cover'. Obviously, this is a gratuitous proposal.
Bibl.: WH II: 725, EM 711, IEW 1110, Schrijver 1991: 146, LIV *ueh g-.
2
vagio, -Ire 'to cry' [v. IV] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: vagor 'loud crying' (Enn.+), vagitus, -us 'cry of distress' (Lucr.+),
vagulatio 'questio cum convlcio' (Fest.), vagulus 'wandering' (or 'crying'?)
(Hadrianus); obvagTre 'to cry importunately' (PI.), obvagulare 'to make a loud noise'
(Lex XII apud Fest.).
Pit. *wag-ije/o-7
Panagl/Lindner 1995: 172 posit a derivational chain vagio —*• vagulus —> *vagulare
vagulatio. The etymology is uncertain: Skt. vagnu- 'sound, call', vagvana-
'talkative' may have *-g-, but are connected with the root *uekw- by EWAia (II: 491).
There is also Lith. vograuti 'to weep, cry'. Greek lays [aor.] 'shouted', fix1!-. D°r- &XP-
'resounding', Go. ga-swogjan 'to sigh' have a voiced aspirated stop, which does not
match Latin -g-. A solution would be to assume a root *ueh - 'to cry', with different
2
enlargements in different IE branches.
Bibl.: WH II: 726f., EM 711, IEW 1110, Schrijver 1991: 146, LIV ?*ueh (g)h-.
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vagus 'roaming, wandering' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: vagare/l 'to wander, roam' (P1.+) (act. until Varro), vagatrix 'female
wanderer' (Sis.).
Pit. *wago~.
PIE *Huog-o-? IE cognates: OIc. vakka, OHG wankon 'to totter, stagger', OHG
winkan 'to waver, stagger, wink', OE wincian 'to nod' < *Huo/e-n-g~.
The Germanic comparanda could reflect a nasal present *Hu-en-g-. Latin vagus
would then continue a PIE form *Huog-o- with *wo~ > *wa- in open syllable. The
Celtic forms adduced by WH (Olr.fan 'slope, declivity, hollow', OWguoun [f.], MW
gwawn, gweun, OCo. guen, OBret. goen, MBret. guetm 'meadow' < PCI. *wagno/d-
'slope, hollow') are not easy to connect semantically.
Bibl.: WH II: 726, EM 711, IEW 1120, Schrijver 1991: 463f„ LIV ?*ueng-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: valefi 'to be strong, healthy' [v. II; pf. valul, ppp. valitum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: valentia 'power' (Naev.+), valentulus 'sturdy' (P1-+), valescere 'to
become sound in health' (Lucr.+), validus 'strong, vigorous' (P1.+), val(i)de 'greatly,
extremely' (PI.+), valetudo 'soundness, good health' (P1.+), valetudinarius
'incapacitated' (Varro+); convalescere 'to grow strong, recover' (Cato+); volae/emum
'large kind of pear' (Cato+).
Pit. *wal-e-. It. cognates: O. faXe, Marr. uali Pael. ualf [2s.ipv.] 'farewell' < *uale
t
(or loanword from Latin); maybe O. ualaemom [nom.acc.sg.n.], valaimas [gen.sg.f.],
SPic. velaimes [gen.sg.m.?] 'best' < *ua/elaimo- (< *ua/elaisVmo-?). The
appurtenance of Presam. poXo<; [adj?] and poXaicnjjiOi; [sup.?] is unclear.
PIE *h ulheh 'to be strong'. IE cognates: Olr. fallnaithir* 'to rule' < PCI.
2 r r
*ualna-, W. gwaladr 'ruler' < *ulh etro~, Olr. flaith 'rule', OW gulat [f.], MW
r
gwlad 'country', Co. gulat, OBret. guletic < PCI. *ulati- 'sovereignty' < *ulHti~, Olr.
fal 'rule', Conall [PN], OW Con-gual [PN], Bret. Conuual [PN] < *kuno-waIo- <
PCI. *ualo- 'prince, chief; Hit. hulle-71 / hull- 'to smash, defeat' < *h ul-ne-h ti,
2 r
*h ul-n-h enti\ OPr. weldisnan 'inheritance' [acc.sg.], Lith. veldeti, 3s. veldi 'to
2 r
inherit, (OLith, dial.) acquire, attain, rule' < *uelH-dh-; Lith. valdyti, 3s. valdo 'to
rule, govern, wield', Latv. valdit, CS vladeti 'to rule', Ru. vladet' and volodet' (dial.)
'to own, control, wield' < *uol(H)-d\ OCS vlasti, Is. vladg 'to rule' < PIE
*uolH-dh-; Go. waldan 'to rule', OHG walten 'to dominate' < *uol(H)-t/dh-; ToA wdl,
gen.sg. lant, ToB walo, gen. lante 'king' < PIE *ulH-nt~.
PIE *(H)ulh eh would directly yield *wale- by phonetic development; for the reflex
r r
of *(H)ul- in Latin, cf. Schrijver 1991: 76. The root can be reconstructed with final
*-h| on the strength of Hit. hull-, cf. Kloekhorst 2008: 358ff.
Bibl.: WH II: 727f, EM 71 If, IEW llllf, Schrijver 1991: 213-215, Unter...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: valgus 'bow-legged, with x-formed legs' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Has been connected with Skt. valgati (AV+) 'to move up and down', Khot. valj- 'to
go astray, be deceived', OE wealcan 'to roll, move to and fro' < *uol(H)g~. Yet the
main characteristic of 'bow-legged' is the crookedness of the legs, not 'going up and
down' or 'to and fro'. In addition, valgus cannot phonetically continue *wolg-.
Bibl.: WH II: 728, EM 712, IEW 1140-44, Schrijver 1991: 464, LIV 1 *uelg-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: valles, -is 'valley' [f. /] (Varro+; nom.sg. also vallis)
Derivatives: vallecula 'small valley' (Paul. exF.).
No certain etymology. The connection with Gr. rHXtc;, -5o<; region in the western
Peloponnese, Elis paXetot; 'Elean, Elans' < *paXi<; 'lowland' (if that is the original
meaning of the toponym) is possible, but requires additional suffixation for Latin.
Several Germanic and Baltic words for 'round' have been connected (OIc. valr
'round', Go. walus 'stick', Lith. apvaliis 'round', Ru. oblyj 'rounded'); if Gm. and
Baltic continue *uol-, Latin vol- would have originated from unrounding in an open
syllable, but vallis < *wals/n- did not have an open syllable. Also, the Gm. and BS1.
words for 'round' can alternatively be connected with Slavic 'wave', and derived
from PIE *ulH- 'to turn' (see volvo). From such a root, it is even more difficult to
explain vallis.
Bibl.: WH II: 729, EM 712, IEW 1140-44, Schrijver 1991: 460. -

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vannus 'winnowing-basket' [f. o] (Col.+)
Derivatives: vannare 'to winnow' (Lucil.), vallus, -V-us (f.) 'implement for
winnowing corn', var. vallum (Varro+); vatillum 'small shovel or pan' (Varro+)?
Connected with ventus by WH, who also derive vatillum from *watnelo- (thus
Leumann 1977: 200). The connection with vatillum is rejected by EM on account of
the different meaning. Schrijver agrees on formal grounds: it is hard to believe that
vallus would be a late formation. Schrijver (1991: 309) proposes to derive vannus
from the PIE word for 'wind' by means of *-no-\ the word 'wind' would have been in
the zero-grade: *h uhnt-no- 'with wind' vel sim. This does not seem very plausible,
2 r
since 'wind' is attested with a fiill grade in the root or in the suffix in all IE languages
(cf. ventus), and even if we concede that the weak cases once had *h uh -nt-, the
2 2
derivation in *-no- would have occurred in PIE already, which is hardly credible.
Moreover, positing 'the one of the wind' is not a compelling semantic explanation for
'winnowing-basket'. The only advantage of positing vannus < *wantno- would be
that the diminutive becomes easier to explain: *wantnelo- > *wannelo- > *wannlo- >
*wanlo- > *wallo~.
Bibl.: WH II: 731, EM 713, IEW 81-84, Schrijver 1991: 308f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vanus 'hollow, devoid' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: vanare 'to use empty words' (Acc.), vanitas 'emptiness, falsity,
foolishness' (Ter.+), vanitudo 'falsehood or emptiness of speech' (P1.+); vanidicus
'chattering' (PL), vaniloquus 'speaking idle words' (P1.+); evanescere 'to disappear,
vanish' (Ter.+).
Pit. *wdno- or * was no-.
PIE *hiueh -no-, *hiueh -sno- or *h|Uh -sno-. IE cognates: Skt. vayati 'to
2 2 2
disappear', nir-vana- 'extinguished, soothed', YAv.frduuaiia- 'to make disappear',
Skt. una- 'empty', YAv. una- 'deficient', uiiamna- 'being deficient', Gr. eaco 'to let,
permit', Go. wans 'missing'.
If cognate with vastus 'empty, desolate', vanus could go back to *was-no-, other
theoretical options are *wano- and * was no-.
BibL: WH II: 73If., EM 713, IEW 345, Schrijver 1991: 146, 308, Nussbaum 1998a,
LIV *h|Ueh-. —> vastus

Derivatives: vapor are 'to cover with a vapour, be hot' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *kwap-6s 'vapour'. ,
PIE *kwuh ep-s- 'smoke'. IE cognates: Gr. tcrazvoq 'smoke' [m.] < *Kpajrvo^, euro ...
2
eKajtuooev 'breathed forth', Kamjocov 'breathing out', Kajruq 'breath'; Lith. kvapas
'smoke' < *kwh uop-o-, Ru. kopot' 'soot, (dial.) smoke, dust, hoarfrost', Ukr. kopit
2
'dust (in the air)', PI. kopiec 'soot, fumes' [m.]; Lith. kvipti, 3s. kvepia 'to inhale,
breathe in', Latv. kvept 'smoke, smell < PIE *kwh uep-; Go. af-hjapjan 'to suffocate,

extinguish', af-hapnan 'to extinguish' < *xwap-n~.
Schrijver revives the comparison with BS1. words for 'to smoke' and Gr. KCUTVOC;
'smoke' by means of a PIE root *k(w)uh ep- > Pit. *kwuap- > *wap- > Lat. vap-. The
2
first part of the root may ultimately be the same as PIE *kweh -s- 'to cough'. Beekes

(fthc.) rejects PIE origin: the root structure is unusual, and Greek does not prove *ku-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vas, vadis 'surety (in court)' [m. d] (Lex XII, P1.+)
Derivatives: vadari 'to accept sureties from' (P1.+), convaddri 'to make a person
give a surety' (PI.); vadimonium 'guarantee, fulfilment' (P1.+); praes, -dis 'one who
acts as surety or security' (P1.+; nom.pl. praeuides CIL 1.585.46); Ipraedium 'estate,
land; pledge consisting of land' (P1.+).
Pit. *woss, gen. *wapos.
PIE *uodh-s [root noun] 'who leads the defendant to court'. IE cognates: Lith. vadas
'guarantee, surety', vadiioti 'to redeem', uzvadas 'hostage'; Go. wadi [n.] 'surety',
wadjabokos 'letter of of surety', gawadjon 'to engage5, OIc. ved [n.] 'surety', OFr.
wed, OE wedd < PGm. *uadia- [p.].
Leumann explains praes from the acc.sg. praedem < fprai-widem < *prae-wad-em\
similarly Szemerenyi 1989: 72: praeuides < *prae-uades 'bondsman' (in public trial).
Bibl.: WH II: 353, 355, EM 532, IEW 1109, Leumann 1977: 136, Schrijver 1991:
75,464, LIV *uedh-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vastus 'empty, desolate' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: vastare 'to make desolate, lay waste' (Naev.+), vastitds 'desolation'
(Acc.+), vas tides 'desolate condition' (PL), vastitudo 'ravaged condition' (Cato+),
vastescere 'to become desolate' (Acc.).
Pit. * was to-.
PIE *hiu(e)h -sto-, IE cognates: Olr. fas 'empty', OHG wuosti, OE wdsti, OE weste
2
'empty, barren' < PGm. *udsti~.
The long vowel is not certain: WH write vas-, EM vas-. Some authors separate vastus
'enormous, wide' from vastus 'empty, desolate'. But the descriptive dictionaries do
not make such a distinction, and it cannot be determined by the metre or by Romance
reflexes. Puhvel 1992 has proposed a connection with Hit. wasta- / wast- 'to sin,
offend', which Kloekhorst 2008: 985f, etymologizes as *uosTh / *usTh -.
2/r 2/3
Obviously, although Puhvel argues that the Hit. verb means 'to miss (a target)' and
the derivative wastai- 'emptiness', the meanings are not compellingly close.
Bibl.: WH II: 737, EM 714f, IEW 345f, Schrijver 1991: 146, 308, 464, LIV
*hiueh -. vanus
2

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vatax 'having a deformity of the feet' [adj. k] (Lucil.)
Derivatives: vatius 'knock-kneed' (Varro+).
Pit. *wat-l
IE cognates: OIc. vgdvi [m.] 'muscles', OHG wado [m.] 'sura, suffrago', MHG
wade 'calf (of the leg)', OS wathan 'calves' < PGm. *wapwan- 'curve, bend' > 'calf
of the leg, knee'.
A stem vat- can be connected with PGm. *uap- found in words for 'calf (of the leg)',
but the further etymology remains unclear. Short -a- makes it less likely that these
words are to be connected with vanus and varus from a root Pit *wa~.
Bibl.: WH II: 737, EM 715, IEW 1113.

Sacra CIL 401 seive, Lex XII+ sTve\ seu (P1.+) 'or if, whether'.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ve- 'faulty, far from' [pref.]: vecors 'mad' (Andr.+), vecordia 'frenzy' (Ter.+),
vegrandis 'undersized, extensive; very great* (P1.+), vesamis 'mad' (P1-+), vescus 'thin,
attenuated' (Lucil.+), maybe Ve(d)iouis 'certain deity of the underworld' (Varro+)
vegeo
657
It. cognates: maybe U. ve(n)persuntra 'withoutpersondro\
It is striking that vecors and vesanus have a very similar meaning, which is literal in
the case of vesanus 'far from healthy', but less transparent in ve-cors 'far from heart'
> 'mad'. The analysis of Guiraud 1975 suggests that ve- meant 'unlike, over- /
under-'. Semantically, it would be attractive to derive ve- from *s(w)ed 'per se' >
'away from', which normally surfaces as the preposition se. In that case, ve-sanus
might be the original form which underwent dissimilation from *sve-sdmis. Yet the
survival of such an isolated form beside productive se seems doubtful, as is the *-w-
in the alleged preform *swe~. Alternatively, we could follow Niedermann (1899), who
argues that vehemens > vemens 'who gets carried away > mad' provided the model
for the extension of ve- to ve-cors and ve-sanus. Re interpretation of ve- as meaning
'abnormal' could then explain the formation of ve-grandis, vescus and ve-pallidus
(Hor.). The appurtenance of vesper here seems unlikely: the vowel has a different
length, and ve- means 'hyper I hypo-', which does not match the alleged meaning of
ve- in vesper.
Bibl.: WH II: 740, EM 716, IEW 72f., Guiraud 1975, Keller 1982: 88f., Schrijver
1991: 122, Untermann 2000: 828. out, edd, se-, vehd
vegeO 'to enliven' [v. II] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: vegetus 'vigorous, lively' (Var+), vegetdre 'to invigorate' (Sen.+).
Pit. *weg-eje/o~. \
PIE *ueg-o- 'active, awake'?, *uog-eie/o- 'to awaken'. IE cognates: Skt. vaja- 'contest,
reward', vajayati 'impels', Go. -wakjan 'to waken', OHG wahhen 'to be awake'.
Lat. vegeo died out after the Republic: Lucretius is the last author to use it. The
meaning 'to be cheerful' is ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: metal bar, lever' (Cato+), vectigal, -alis [n.] 'tax, revenue' (Cato+); vehemens
'violent' (P1.+; vernens Cic.+); advehere 'to convey, travel' (P1.+), advectus, -us
'importation' (Varro+), circumvectari 'to travel round' (P1.+), circumvehi 'to travel
round' (P1.+), convehere 'to collect' (Varro+), evehere 'to carry out, away' (Cato+),
invehere 'to drive or sail in to attack' (P1.+), pervehere 'to transport' (Naev.+),
provehere 'to convey out to sea, carry forward' (P1.+), subvehere 'to convey upwards'
(Lucr.+), subvectdre 'to convey upwards' (PL+).
Pit. *wex~e/o~ [v.], *wekti- [f.]. It. cognates: U. arveitu, arveitu, aveitu, arsueitn,
arueitu [3s.ipv.II] 'to add', kuveitu [3s.ipv.II] 'to put together' < *-ueghetdd.
PIE *uegh-e/o- 'to carry'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. vdhati, s-aor. vaks-, dvat [3s.act,],
root aor. volham [2du.ipv.act.], pf. uvdha [3s.act], ppp. iidha- 'to carry, convey',
OAv. vaziia- [med.] 'to marry, be led to the wedding', vazdra- 'conveyor', YAv.
vaza- 'to pull, ride', vastar- [m.] 'draught-animal', Oss. wcezhvcezce 'weight, load';
Gr. (Pamph.). Fexcto) [3s.ipv.] 'to bring', o%oq [m.] 'cart', Lith. vezti, Is. vezii, OCS
vesti, Is. vezg 'to convey', VOZT> 'cart'; Go. ga-wigan, OHG wegan 'to move', OHG
wagan 'cart'.
The noun vectigal presupposes a compound *vectigo- '(fee for) vehicle-driving' <
*uekti-h2g-o-, cf. Dunkel 2000a: 95. This would contain a noun *wekti~ 'levy, yield',
which was later renewed as vectis 'bar, lever'. The adj. vehement- is explained by
WH as an original pr.ptc.med. *ueghemenos which cannot be upheld anymore.
Guiraud 1975: 86 confirms that vehemens is the older form and not vernens, since it
basically means 'who gets carried away, loses himself in temper', rather than 'who
has lost his mind'. The form veiatura (Paul, ex F.) is interpreted by Leumann 1977:
315 as an error for veiatura 'the business of a carrier' (thus attested in Varro, Plut.)
from *vehela < *wex-ela- 'transport'; this is accepted by ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vello, -ere 'to pull out' [v. Ill; pf. vellT or vitlsi, ppp. virfsiim] (Cato+)
Derivatives: vellus, -eris 'wool, fleece' (Varro+), volsella 'tweezers' (P1.+), vulsura
'plucking' (Varro+), vellicare 'to pinch, peck' (P1.+), vellicdtim 'by picking out here
and there' (Sis.); avellere 'to tear away, pluck out' (Ter.+), convellere 'to dislodge,
shake' (Cato+), devellere 'to pluck bare' (P1.+), evellere 'to uproot' (P1.+),perveUere
'to pinch' (P1.+), praevellere (meaning indeterminable) (Lab.), revellere 'to tear
down, remove' (Enn.-t-), subvellere 'to pluck hair from the under-parts' (Lucil.).
Pit. *weln-e/o-, *wel-. It. cognates: SPic. ehueli [3s.pr.sb.] 'tears down' < *ex-wel-
ed (Weiss 2002:359f.). . -i
PIE *ul-n(e)-h -, aor. *uelh - ;to strike'. IE cognates: Hit. ualh'to hit, strike' <
3 3
*u(e)lh -, Gr. aA.icKop.ai 'to be caught', Thess. p'aXiooKETai, Gr.aor. eaXtov <
3
*f]-pa?,CL)- (*fX.co- before consonant and *paX- before vowel), ToA walldstdr 'dies'.
According to Schrijver, veil- reflects *uel(fh)- because this does not meet with any
definite obstacles, as do in Schrijver's view the reconstructions *ueln~ and *uels~. Yet
the argument against a nasal present (*Huel-n-h) is uncompelling. Schrijver argues
r
that there are no parallels for the introduction of -11- from the present into the pf.
(vellT) if another pf. was originally present (pepulT, tetull, -cult). Yet vello may simply
have had no reduplicated perfect, in which case vellT would be a regular choice for a
new perfect. Thus, Latin may go together with Tocharian in showing a nasal present.
The e-grade in the root is unexpected from a PIE point of view; it might have been
taken from the root aorist continued in SPic. ehueU and in Hittite and Greek,
Bibl.: WH II: 744f, EM 718, IEW H44f., Schrijver 1991: 180f„ Meiser 2003: 214,
LIV *uelh -. —> lana, volnus
3
vElSx 'swift, speedy' [adj. k] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: veldcitas 'speed' (Sis.+).
Pit. *welo- 'wind' (?).

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: velum 'sail; sheet, cloth' [n. o] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: velare 'to cover, clothe' (P1-+), velamentum 'cover' (Varro+), Velabrum
'a low-lying district in Rome' (P1.+), velabra [pl.n.] 'instruments used for ventilating
the com' (Paul, ex F.); vexillum 'miltary standard, banner' (P1.+).
Pit. *wekslo-,
PIE *ueg-slo-? IE cognates: Olr, figid*, fig*, W. gweu, LCo. gwia, Bret, gueaff1 to
weave' <PC1. *ueg-io- 'to weave, compose'; OE wice [m.] 'wick', wdcig [f.] 'trap'.
The dim. shows that at least part of the input for velum had the form *ueKslo~, which
could contain the root *uegh- 'to convey' (semantically less attractive) or *ueg- 'to
weave' attested in Celtic and Germanic. Peruzzi 1969 argues that in velum may have
merged two different preforms, *ueKslo- and *ueslo- 'sail, cloth', to *ues- 'to clothe'.
Bibl.: WH II: 745f., EM 718f., IEW 1117, Schumacher 2004: 660f., LIV ?*ueg-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vena 'blood-vessel' [f. a] (Cato+)
Pit. *we$-no/a- [adj./f.] 'blood-vessel'.
PIE *ues-no- 'of blood'. IE cognates: Av. vatjhu-tat- 'blood', varjhwSfia- [n.j
'bloodshed', vohuna- [m.] 'blood' < *ues-u-\ Skt. vdsd, vasa 'fat, lard' (hesitantly
EWAia II: 533).
No agreed etymology. Muller 1926: 531 points to an old belief that the veins may be
compared to air pipes (Cato Agr. 157, 7: venae ubi sufflatae sunt ex cibo, non possunt
perspirare in to to corpore), and suggests that vena might be derived from *h ueh 'to
2 r
blow'. Alternatively, one might think of a root 'to convey', for instance PIE *uecf -
'to lead' or *uegh- 'to convey', with a suffix *-sno-. Yet in both cases, we face the
semantic objection that it would be unclear why a 'blood-vessel' would come to have
been denominated as the 'conveyor' par excellence. Alternatively, one might connect
vena with Av. *vahu- 'blood', and posit *uesnd-.
Bibl.: WH II: 746, EM 719, IEW 1134.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: venenum 'potent herb, poison' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: venenare 'to bewitch, poison' (P1.+); veneficus 'of sorcery, of poison'
(PI.+), veneficium 'sorcery, poisoning' (P1.+), trivenefica 'treble-dyed witch' (P1.+).
Pit. *wenes-no-.
IE cognates: see s.v. venus.
The form suggests Pit. *wenes-no~, a derivative of venus 'desire, love' (cf. catena <
*kates-na-). WH interpret the original meaning as 'love drink', which would imply
that the term arose in the context of sorcery. If the original meaning was a more
neutral adj. *wenes-no- 'poisonous', it might have developed from 'with desire' via
'addicting' to 'pernicious, poisonous'.
Bibl.: WH II: 747, EM 719.
I

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: coming'. It. cognates: U. menes [2s.fut.] (uncertain because of m-), benus [2 or
3s.fut.pfi], benust [3s.fUt.pf.], benurent, benurent [3p.fut.pfi], benuso [?], O.
kumbened [kom- + 3s.pf.], O. cebnust [ke- + 3s.fut.pfi] 'to come' < *ben- < *gven~.
Note pf. iben-l, not Iben-L O. kumbennieis [gen.sg.] 'certain town council' <
*kom-ben-io- < *kom-gwm-io- 'gathering'.
PIE *gwm-ie/o- 'to come', *gwm-to-, *gwm-ti- [fi], *gwem-tu- [m.] 'coming'. IE
cognates: Skt. pr. gac.chati, aor. agan [3s.act.], pf. jagama [3s.act.] 'to move, come',
gata- [ppp.], gati- [fi] 'going, motion', gantu- [m.] 'walk, road', YAv. jasa- [pr.],
OAv. aor. j§n [3s.ind.act.], gamsn [3p.ind.act.], YAv. gata- [ppp.], aifiigaiti- [f.]
'coming towards', Gr. paivw 'to go' (< PGr. *paji-i-a)), PaoKE [2s.ipv.act.] 'go!', Go.
qiman lto come' (< PIE sb. *gwem-e-\ ToB smanToA smds [sb.] 'will come'.
The present and the ppp. are inherited; the same could be true for the nouns -ventus
[u\ and -ventio (« *-venti-), but these could also be recent formations following the
productive pattern. Lat. advena and convena cannot be recent derivatives from venio,
but their -n- does betray them as are secondary creations of the type incola.
Apparently, the phonetic rise of Pit. *gwn-je/o- and *gwn-to- with n for PIE *m caused
the replacement of *m by *n in the other derivatives of the root too, esp. the Italic pf.
Bibl.: WH II: 747fi, EM 720, IEW 463-465, Untermann 2000: 143-145, 412, LIV
*gyem- —> cdntio

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: venter, -tris 'belly, abdomen' [m. i] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ventriosus 'pot-bellied' (P1.+).
Pit. *we(n)d-ri-l
PIE *(H)ued-ro- 'belonging to the belly'.
Possible preforms include *wentri- and *wendri~. Hamp 1978: 190 suggests that it is
originally the same word as uterus < PIE *(H)u(e)d-er-o- 'sticking out, belly'.
Semantically, this is of course attractive; and formally, especially BS1. *(7)ued-ero-
'(belonging to the) belly' is close to venter. Hamp explains the internal nasal as
analogical from vesica 'bladder', which is ad hoc and uncertain since the nasal is a
recent addition to vesica (see s.v.).
BibL: WH II: 751, EM 721, IEW 1104f. ^ uterus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ventus 'wind' [m. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ventosus 'windy' (Cato+), ventulus 'light breeze' (PI, Ter.), ventilare
'to expose to a draught, ventilate' (Var.+), ventildbrum 'winnowing-shovel' (Var.+).
Pit. *wen to-.
PIE *h uehnt-o- 'wind'. IE cognates: W. gwynt 'wind', Hit. huuant- [c.] 'wind' <
2 r
*h uhi-ent-, Skt. vdta- [m.] 'wind, god of wind' (several times disyllabic in the RV),
2
OAv. vata- /va?ata-/ [m.] 'wind', YAv. vdta-, Gr. arjoi 'to blow', Go. winds, OHG
wint, ToByente [n.f.], ToA want (~ want) 'wind'.
A derivative of the original pr.ptc.act. of *h ueh 'to blow (of the wind)' (nom.sg.
2 r
*h uehnt-s, acc.sg. *h uhent-m, gen.sg. *h uh,-nt-os). PIE *h uehnt-o- has first
2 r 2 r 2 2 r
yielded pre-Italic *uento-, whence with Osthoff s shortening *wento-. According to
Schrijver, the development might also have been of PIE *h uehnt-o- to pre-Italic
2 r
*HueHento-, with syllabification of the nasal, and then contraction of *-eHe- >*-e~.
This would match the Ilr. development of 'wind'. Yet since Germanic and Celtic
unambiguously show the retention of consonantal *n throughout, it seems safest to
assume the same for Latin.
Bibl.: WH I: 751-752, EM 721, IEW 81-84, Schrijver 1991: 159f., LIV *h ueh 'to
z r
blow (of the wind)'. —• v annus, velox

sold' (Varro+), vendax 'addicted to selling' (Cato+), venditare 'to offer for sale'

Pit. *wesno~. It. cognates: Marr. uenalinam [acc.sg.f.] maybe 'of the sale', deriv. to

PIE *ues-no- 'price'. IE cognates: Hit. usniie/a- 'to put up for sale' < *us-n-ie/o~,

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: ver 'spring' [n. r\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: vernus 'occurring in spring' (Andr.+), verculum 'a little spring' (PL),
verno 'in spring5 (Cato+); ver sacrum 'sacrifice of all living beings born in the spring
of a certain year5 (Sis.+).
Pit. *wes-or [nom.acc.], *wes-n- [gen.].
PIE *ues-r/n- [n.] 'spring'. IE cognates: Skt. vasantd-, Av. vatjri [loc.sg.], Gr. eap
[n.] 'spring', Etapivoq 'belonging to spring', Arm. garown 'spring', Lith. vasara,
vasara 'summer', OCS vesna 'spring', OIc. vdr 'spring'.
An original paradigm *uesr, *uesnos would yield Latin *veror (since the vocalization
of *r to *or was Proto-Italic), gen.sg. * venos. The gen.sg. was replaced by *veros,
and subsequently, long e has been introduced into the nom.sg. and final -or was
analogically removed. Schrijver, differently, reconstructs nom.sg. *uesr, but an ablaut
*e : *e in a n. word seems unlikely. Lat. vernus may reflect *uesinos, or, with
Nussbaum, be delocatival from *veri-no-. Not to PIE *h ues- 'to spend the night;
2
dawn' because of the initial laryngeal. Skt. vasar-ha (RV), of the wind (meaning
uncertain), and vasard- 'of the morning' may belong here too, their meaning having
been influenced by usra- 'matutinal', usar 'at dawn' (Alexander Lubotsky, p.c.).
Bibl.: WH II: 755, EM 722, IEW 1174, Leumann 1977: 206, Schrijver 1991: 129.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: verbera 'twigs for flogging, whip, lashes' [n.pl. r] (P1.+; sg. in quotations, and
nom.sg. uerber in glossaries.)
Derivatives: verbero 'one who deserves flogging' (P1.+), verbereus 'associated with
flogging' (P1.+), verberetillus 'quite associated with flogging' (PI.), verberare 'to
flog, hammer' (P1.+), verberabundus 'occupied in flogging' (PI.), verberabilis 'ready
for beating' (PI.), verberitdre 'to frequently flog' (Cato apud Paul, ex F); deverberare
'to flog soundly' (Ter.), diverberare 'to cleave' (Lucr.+), subverbustus 'loaded with
floggings' (P1.+); verbena 'leafy branch or twig' (Naev.+).
Pit. *werb/fos [n.] 'twig, whip'.
PIE *uerb(h) -es-? IE cognates: Lith. virbas 'stick, twig', virbalas 'small staff,
needle', virbinis 'snare', Latv. virbs 'small stick', virba 'pole', Ru. verba 'willow
twig' (Ru. dial, verba), voroba 'cord', voroby 'spool'.
The pi. verbera, the adj. -verbustus and the derivative verbena < *werbes-na- all point
to an j-stem *werbos, -es-. The BS1. words for 'twig' and 'stick' may be derived from
the same root. Slavic seems to point to a root *urb-, but Baltic to *urbh-.
Bibl.: WH II: 756, EM 722f., IEW 1153.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vermis 'worm, maggot' [m. /] (Cato+)
Derivatives: vermiculus 'larva, maggot' (Lucil+), vermiculatus 'arranged to give a
wavy effect' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *wormi- 'snake, worm'.
PIE *urmi- 'snake, worm'. IE cognates: Go. waurms [m.] 'snake', OIc. ormr, OE
wyrm, OFr. wirm 'snake', OS wormo, OHG wurm [m.] 'worm' < PGm. *wurma/i-.
It is striking that other branches of IE continue a form *kwrmi- 'worm': Olr. critim,
MW pryf, Bret, pryf'worm'; Skt. kfmi- 'worm, maggot', Sogd. kyrm- 'snake', MP
klm /kirm/ 'worm, dragon, snake', MoP kirm, Lith. kirmis 'worm, snake', OCS crtvb
'worm', Alb. krim(b) 'worm, maggot, catterpiller'. This distribution might be the
result of a development of *kwrmi- > *wrmi- in Latin and Germanic.
Bibl.: WH II: 760, EM 724, IEW 1152.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: veru, -us 'spit (for roasting)' [n. it] (P1.+)
Derivatives: verutum 'short throwing spear' (Enn.+), vervina 'spit (or similar
pointed instrument)' (P1.+). '
Pit. *gweru- 'spit'. It. cognates: U. berva [acc.pl.], berus [abl.pl.] n. 'utensil used
during the sacrifice' < *gweru-.
PIE *gwer(H)-u- 'spit'. IE cognates: Olr. bi(u)r [n. > f.], W. Co. Bret, her [f., m.]
'spear, spit' < *gweru~.
In theory, Italo-Celtic *gweru- could reflect PIE *gwerh -u- 'the heavy one', from
2
which it is not inconceivable that a meaning 'pole, spit' would derive. Nor is it
compelling, of course. The Go. word qairu n. 'pole, sting' must be read as paint (see
Ebbinghaus 1977: 188f.) and does not belong here.
Bibl.: WH II: 766, EM 727, IEW 479, Untermann 2000: 145.
O. veru [acc.pl.], veruis [loc.pl.], U. vera [acc.pl.], verufe, verofe [acc.pl. + -en] ,
veres, iterir [abl.pl.], uerisco [abl.pl. + -com] 'city gate'.
Pit. *wero- 'gate' [n.].
PIE *Huer-o- 'cover, protection'. IE cognates: Skt. vala- [m.] 'cave, enclosure', Av.
vara- [m.] 'fenced place, artificial cave' < Ilr. *ffuara-, ORu. obora 'area for cattle',
Cz. obora 'game preserve', SCr. obor 'courtyard' <ob + *Huor-h ~.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: verus 'real, true' [adj. o/a] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: verax 'truthful' (P1.+), verare 'to speak the truth' (Enn.+), veratrum
'poisonous or medicinal plant, hellebore' (Cato+).
Pit. *wero~.
PIE *uehro- 'true'. IE cognates: Olr. fir, W. gwir < PCI. *wiroOCS vera, Ru,
r
vera, Po. wiara 'faith, belief < PSl. *vera [f,], OHG war 'true'. Possibly also Gr. rjpa
in (eitt) r|pa (pepetv 'to please somebody' [acc. sg. (pi. n.?)] if from *iter-.
The appurtenance of veratrum here is uncertain. It would suppose that it was derived
from verare, in the sense that hellebore was used to restore mental health in patients
(this was Isidor's etymology); cf. Serbat 1975: 318f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vervex, -ecis 'wether, castrated male sheep' [m. k] (PI.+)
Pit. *werw-e~.
PIE *ueru-? IE cognates: Gr. eipog [n.] 'wool' < *ueruos~, Myc. /werwes-ieiaf
'women who work the wool', Att. eu-epog 'with beautiful wool', fiTi-epog 'sheep'.
Has been connected with PIE *urhen- 'lamb' (to Skt. iiran- [m.] 'Iamb' < Ilr.
r
*urH-an-, Sogd. wr'n 'lamb' < PIr. *varan-am, Gr. aprjv [m.], Kret. fapTjv 'Iamb',
Gr. jioX.u-ppt]v-e(; 'possessing many lambs', Arm. garn 'Iamb'), but it is hard to think
of a derivational scenario by which this could have happened: *uerhu- > *ueru- +
r
*-e- (on the model of verres) » *-ek- is conceivable, but all other IE languages have
the zero grade *urh of the root in this word. A zero grade *urh it- would probably
r r
yield Latin *rdw- or possibly *raw-. Instead of with PIE 'lamb', vervex might be
connected with Gr. eipoc; [n.] 'wool' < *ueruos~, which is semantically less attractive,
but possible. We could assume *ueru-e- 'the male woolly one' (on the model of
verres), built on an older w-stem or on the truncated s-stem. The fc-suffix must, under
any scenario, be a later Latin addition.
Bibl.: WH II: 767f, EM 727, IEW 1170.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vescor, vescl 'to make use of, enjoy' [v. Ill] (Pac.+)
Pit. *wes-(s)ke/o- 'to feed, pasture'. It. cognates: Ven. vesces [nom.sg.], vesketei
[dat.sg.] 'pupil' (male and female reference) < *wesk-et~; possibly U. veskla, vesklu
[acc.pl.], veskles, uesclir, Vol. uesclis [abl.pl.] < *wesk(V)lo- [n.] 'kind of vessel'.
PIE *ues- 'pasture, food'. IE cognates: Oh. fess 'food' < *ites-th -, Hit. uesi- /iiesai-
2
[c.] 'pasture' (< *ues-(e)i-), Skt. svasara- 'pasture, meadow' (< *su-uas-ra-), Av.
vastra- [n.] 'pasture', vdstar- 'herd', OIc. fist 'nourishment' < *uesti-, ToA wdsri
'pasture' < *uesri~.
WH's derivaton from *ve-escor 'to eat off is unconvincing because of the semantic
gap with 'to enjoy', and because of the passive conjugation of vescor. Mulier 1926
and Keller 1982 assume a merger of two verbs, one being PIE *ues- 'to wear', and
another verb meaning 'to use, enjoy'. Yet the contexts where vescor means 'to wear'
are very rare, and the oldest attestations adduced by Mulier and Keller (vescor armis)
can just as well mean 'to use'. The verb vescor can be compared with IE terms for
'pasture' which go back to a root *ues~. Latin could reflect *ues-ske/o-, with
introduction of e as (possibly) in Av. vastravastar-. In view of the absence of verbal
derivatives of the root outside Latin, this -e- would probably have a nominal origin
(e.g. from a root noun *ues, *uesos). The suffix -sk- would be a Latin addition, or an
earlier Italic one, if U. vesklo- 'vessel' is cognate, or an even older one (maybe only a
suffix *-k~) if Ven. vesket- is cognate.
Bibl.: WH II: 769, EM 727f, IEW 72f, Lejeune 1974: 340, Untermann 2000: 846f,
LIV 3.*ues-. —> edd, vestibulum

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vesica 'bladder' [f. a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: ve(n)sTcula 'small bubble' (Lucr.+).
Andre 1984 argues, to my mind convincingly, that the original form was vesica,
which is sometimes spelled vensica because the sequences -es- and -ens- started to
merge in VLat. Andre compares the fact that the 'bladder' is often referred to using
words for 'to blow, inflate' in different languages, e.g. in Germanic (NHG Blase, En.
bladder), which is due to the fact that bladders were often recycled as balloons,
lanterns or musical instruments involving air bags. Andre compares Lat. visire /
vissTre 'to fart softly' (Lucil.+) and regards the root *vi/es(s)- as onomatopoeic; the
sound of a deflating bladder would thus have been compared to the sound of a fart.
Indeed, words for 'to fart' often show a sequence of a labial obstruent plus a sibilant,
cf. Lat. pedo < PIE *pesd-. The new Latin formation *ve/is(s)~ is comprehensible in
the light of the development *pesd- > ped-, which obscured the sound symbolism
originally present in that word.
Bibl.: WH II: 750f„ EM 728, IEW 1104f, Andre 1984.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vespa 'wasp' [f. a] (Varro+)
Pit. *we/op-s-h - 'wasp'.
2
PIE *(h )uobh-s-h - 'wasp'. IE cognates: OCo. guhi-en 'a wasp', MW gw(y)chi,
1 2
OBret. guohi 'wasps'; Av. vafizaka- 'scorpion', MP vafiz, Bal, gwabz 'wasp' < Pir.
*wabza-\ OPr. wobse, Lith. vapsva, vapsa (dial.), Latv. vapsene 'wasp', RuCS osa,
Ru. osa, SCr. osa (< BSI. *uapsa); OE wcefs, weeps, wcesp OHG wefsa, wafsa, waspa,
Bav. webes.
It is questionable whether 'wasp' was derived from PIE *(hi)uebh- 'to weave', since
wasps are not typically known for 'weaving'. On the other hand, the way in which
they build their nests from paper pulp could be described as 'weaving', or its
appearance as a net.
Bibl.: WH II: 770, EM 728, IEW 1179.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vesper, -erf 'evening' [m. o] (PI.+; also vespera)
Derivatives: vesperna 'the evening meal' (P1.+), vesperascere, advesperascere 'to
become evening' (Ter.+), vespertinus 'of the evening' (Varro+), vesperugo 'the
Evening Star' (P1.+), vespertilid 'bat' (Varro+).
Pit. *we(k)spero~.
PIE *ue-k(w)sp-er-o- 'evening'. IE cognates: W. ucher 'evening', Hit. ispant- [c.]
'night', Skt. ksap- [f.], Av. xsapan- I xsafn-, YAv. xsap- [fi], xsapar-, OP xsap- [f.J
'night' < PIE *k(w)sep(-r-/-n-); Gr. eortepoq [m.] 'evening' (< *uekwsp-ero-)\ Arm.
giser 'night' (< *uekwsero-), Lith. vakaras, Latv. vakars, OCS vecerb, Ru. vecer
'evening' < *uekspero-.
The PIE word for 'evening' is clearly a compound of an element *ue- and a word for
'night' *}Jw)sep-. The zero grade of the root brought about a consonant cluster
*-k(w)sp- which was simplified in different ways in the IE languages. The first element
*ite- is difficult to expain. It has been equated with Lat. ve-, but this has a very
restricted distribution, and chances are that it is an inner-Latin development; see s.v.
Also, it means 'hyper / hypo-', which does not match the alleged meaning of ve- in
vesper. Ve- in vesper has been compared with Latin au-iem, Gr. au 'again', but PIE
*h ue- would leave other traces in Greek than e-. Of course, one might assume that
2
the initial laryngeal was dropped in the compound. It is possible that *ue- in
*ue-k(w)sp-ero- has a completely different origin, e.g. the reduction of an earlier noun
or adjective.
Bibl.: WH II: 770fi, EM 728, IEW 1173f. ve-
ve/ispillO [m. H] 'one employed to bury those too poor to afford a funeral* (Mart.+)

PIE *uos-p-l IE cognates: Hit. {lyiC)uaspa- jc.] 'clothing', CLuw. uaspant- 'wearing
shrouds (?)' < PIE *uos-bho-l
As argued by Watkins 1969, the original function of a vespillo was to provide the
dead with the right clothing for their burial. Since vesp- may reflect earlier *vosp-
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vestibulum 'fore-court' [n. o] (PI.+)
Pit *westV-plo- 'place of feeding'.
PIE *ues-ti- 'feeding' or *ues-to- 'fed'. IE cognates: See s.v. vescor.
Many different etymologies have been proposed. The most popular ones have been
reviewed by Serbat 1975: 50-53, who points out that all of them are semantically
unsatisfactory. Serbat himself argues that the vestibulum may have referred to the
courtyard of the pre-classical -farm, where the domesticated animals were kept and
fed. This leads him to derive vestibulum from the root *ues- 'to pasture, graze', its
original meaning being 'which permits feeding'. For the suffix, compare Lat.
sessibulum.
Bibl.: WH II: 774, EM 729, Leumann 1977: 314, LIV 3.*ues-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vestigium 'footprint, track' [n. o] (P1.+)
Derivatives: vestTgare 'to follow the trail, search out' (P1.+), vestigator 'who tracks
down' (Varro+), investigare 'to track down, search out' (P1.+).
Pit. *w(e/o)rsti-Hg-.
PIE *u(e)rs-ti- 'furrow'. IE cognates: see s.v. verro.
i
It is often assumed that vestigium was primary and vestTgare secondary, but this is
uncertain. Since a connection with vestis 'clothes' does not make much sense, the
suggestion that vestigium continues *verstigium, which was defended by Muller
1926: 539 but rejected outright by WH, seems attractive. If connected with verro 'to
sweep' < *urs-, vestigium may originally have meant a 'track' or 'furrow'. The verb
vestTgare can reflect *w(e)rsti-Hg- 'to draw a furrow', from a noun *urs-ti- (or
*uers-ti-) 'furrow'.
BibL: WH II: 774f„ EM 729, Dunkel 2000a: 95, LIV l.*uers-. vo/erro

content:
