content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: clothed', vayhana- [n.] 'clothing', vastra- [n.] 'garment, cloth', Gr. ewupi 'to clothe,
put on' < *ues-n(e)u-, aor. £o(o)cu, pf.med. elpai, eipevoi; < *ues~; eipaxa [pi.]
'clothes' (< *ues-mn~), Arm. z-genowm 'clothe oneself < *ues-nu-, z-gest 'cloth(es)'
< *ues-tu~, Alb. vesh 'to clothe', Go. and-wasjan 'to take off, OE werian 'to clothe'
< PIE *uos-eie-\ ToB was- 'to wear; put on'.
Probably an independent formation of Latin, since Skt. vasti- is only attested in the
Taittiriya Aranyaka.
Bibl.: WH II: 774f, EM 729, IEW 1172f, LIV l.*ues-.
ve/ot5, -are 'to forbid' [v. I; pf. vetui, ppp. vetitum] (P1.+; void in Nonius)
Pit. *we/ot-a(je)- / *wotaje~.
PIE *uet(h )- / *uoth -eie/o- / *ue/ot-eh - 'to say'. IE cognates: OW guetid 'says',
2 2 2
MW dy-wedaf'l say' < PCI. *uete/o-; MW gwadu 'to deny', gwad [m.], OBret. guad
'denial' < *uot-.
If the hapax vo- in Nonius is the oldest form, we must reconstruct Pit. *wot-; since
this did not undergo unrounding to *wa-, it would imply a Pit. preform *wotH- with a
closed initial syllable (Schrijver 1991). The verb could be equated with MW gwadu
'to deny' < *uot~. Rix 1999 argues that the original construction was vetare ne 'to say
that not', which came to mean 'to forbid that'; this development might have been
Italo-Celtic, in view of the MW meaning of gwadu 'to deny. But other solutions are
equally possible. Lat. voto could be a deliberate archaism; compare the change vo- >
ve- in front of s,tyC from the second century onwards. Or Lat. voto could be a
phonetically regular remnant of the change *e > o after *w and before a back vowel in
the next syllable (as in vomo, soror, etc.). In that case, we can reconstruct Pit. *wet-d-
'to say' to an anit root *uet-.
Bibl.: WH I: 776, EM 730, Schrijver 1991: 472f„ Rix 1999: 520, Schumacher 2004:
679f, LIV *ueth -.
2
U. vetu, ahauendu,preuendu [3s.ipv.II, + a-, + *prai~] 'to turn'.
Pit. *wende/o~.
PIE *(H)uendh-e/o- 'to wind'. IE cognates: Skt. vandhur- [m.] 'sea...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vetus, -eris 'old' [adj. r] (P1.+; Enn. nom.sg. veter)
Derivatives: vetulus 'elderly, ageing' (P1.+), veteranus 'mature, experienced'
(Varro+), vetustus 'ancient' (PI.+), vetustas 'being old, age, antiquity' (P1-+), veternus
'torpor, lassitude' (P1.+), veternosus 'lethargic' (Cato+), veterator 'an experienced
practitioner' (Ter.+); inveterascere 'to grow old' (Ter.+); veterinus [adj.] 'used for
draught, draught-animals' (Cato+).
Pit, *wetos- 'year', *wetes-ino- 'old', *wetso- 'of the year'. It cognates: U. vesune,
Mars, uesune [datsg.] 'the goddess *wetsona 'lady of the year'; maybe O. vezkei
[dat.sg.j 'a certain deity'.
PIE *uet-os- 'year' [n.], *uet-s-o- 'of the year'. IE cognates: Hit. uitt- (< we/-) [c.]
'year', CLuw. ussa/i-, HLuw. usa/i- 'year' (< *ut-s-o~), Hit. saudist- / sauitist-
'weanling, young calf (< *so-ut-es-t- i * so-uet-es-t- 'the one of this year'), Skt.
vatsara- [m.j 'year', Gr. ETO< ;[n.], dial, peroq 'year', Myc. we-to, we-te-i (/uetos,
ueteh-i/) [n.] 'year', Alb. vit, pi. (also sg.) vjet 'year'; Lith. vetusas, OCS vettcht
'old', Ru. vetxij 'old, ancient, decrepit' < PIE *uetus-o~. Go. wiprus 'year-old lamb',
OIc. vedr, OHG widar 'wether'.
Adams 1992 observes that vetennus usually refers to equines, and suggests that these
came to be seen as 'the old stock' because by nature they lived longest of all cattle,
and because the Romans did not as a rule eat horse meat Hence, especially equines
could be referred to as 'old',
Bibl.: WH II: 776f, EM 729f, IEW 1175, Untermann 2000: 851-4, Weiss 2007b.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: via 'road' [f. a] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: vialis 'of roads' (PI.+), viaticus 'of/for a journey' (P1.+), viaticalus
'furnished with supplies for a journey' (PI.), viator 'traveller, agent' (CIL I.583+);
obviam 'in the path of, facing up' (Naev.+), obvius 'in the path of, confronting'
(P1.+),pervhts 'passable' (P1.+), praevius 'leading the way' (P1.+); viocurus 'who has
charge of roads' (Varro+); veia 'plaustrum' in Oscan (Paul, ex F.), veiar(i)i 'stipites
in plaustro', veiatura 'vectura' (ibidem).
Pit. *wijd- 'road'. It. cognates: O. viu, viu [nom.sg.], viam, via [acc.sg.], viai
[loc.sg.], viass [acc.pl.], U. via, vea, uia [abl.sg.], SPic. viam [acc.sg.] f. 'road' <
*uija; O. amviannud, amvianud '?' probably < *am- or an-via-ndo-, denom. verb to
*vid- 'road'.
PIE *uiheh - 'pursuit'. IE cognates: Skt. viydnti [3p,act.] 'they pursue', vitd-
r 2
'turned to', YAv. viia- 'to pursue', vitar- [m.] 'pursuer'; Gr, lepai 'to strive after,
wish'; Lith. vyti 'to drive, pursue'.
According to Nieto 1988, Paul, ex F. veia 'waggon* is a VLat. development from via,
and the following form in the same gloss veidri represents *viari 'of the road'; both
would have VLat. lowering of i in hiatus. This is confirmed by the form veham 'viam'
given by Varro as a 'rustic' form. The meaning 'waggon' could be directly preserved
in Italian veggia 'waggon; barrel'. This, in turn, means that the etymology *ueghid
can be discarded, and we may rather posit Pit. *wija-. This may derive from PIE
*ueih,~ 'to strive after, try to get', as has been suggested by WH and others. The same
root is reflected in Latin vis 'you want' anb invitus 'unwilling'.
Bibl.: WH II: 778fi, EM 731, IEW 1123f„ Untermann 2000: 93, 860f„ LIV *ueih.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vibro, -are 'to move to and fro, flash' [v. I] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: vibrissare 'to sing with vibrato' (Titin. apud Paul, ex F.).
Pit. *wib-ro- 'trembling'.
PIE *uip/b-ro-. IE cognates: Skt. vipra- [m. / adj.] 'trembling, seer', YAv. vifra-
[adj.] 'ecstatic'; Skt. pr. vepate 'to tremble', vipdya- 'to become inspired', aor.
vipana- [ptc.med.], pf. vivipre [3p.med.] 'they have become excited', vip- [f.]
'inspired speech', OAv. vaepiia- 'catamite, pathic', YAv. apa vaepa- 'to yield, give
off, vaepaiia- 'to be homosexual', Go. weipan 'to crown \ faur-waipjan 'to bind',
OIc. veifa 'to swing'; ToB wpyp- 'to shake, swing', 3s. wlpd(ssam) , ToB waipe*
[m.sg.] 'banner', obLsg. waipe, ToB waipalau 'vertigo'.
Derived from an adj. *wibro- 'swinging, agitated', which can be connected to IE
words of the same meaning from *ueip- (Gm. *weib- may also go back to PIE *ueip-,
cf. LIV). The outcome of PIE *-pr- in Latin can be seen in caper, capra, although
these words may be loanwords. The PIE root-final consonant apparently varied in
voicing, consider also the root 'to swing' in Skt. ksipati 'to hurl', Av.
xsuuaefiaiiat.astra- 'swinging the whip', Skt. ksipra- 'quick, fast', YAv. xsuuifira-
'quick', xsuuifii-isu- 'with fast arrows'. The Ilr. root
looks like a
compound with *uib/p- as its second member.
Bibl.: WH II: 780fi, EM 73If., IEW 113If., LIV *ueip~-
vicis [gen.sg.] 'turn, occasion' [f. &] (P1.+; acc. vicem, abl. vice, nom.pl. vices,
dat.abl.pl. vicibus\ not attested in nom.sg. and gen.pl.)
Derivatives: vicissdtim 'in turn(s)' (Naev.+), vicissim 'in turn' (P1+), vicissitas
'alternation' (Acc.), vicissitudd 'reversal, alternation' (Ter.+), vicdrius [adj./m.]
'substitute, deputy' (P1.+).
Pit. *wik- 'turn'.
PIE *uik- 'a turn'. IE cognates: see s.v. vincio.
As to the PIE root, this may well have been *uik- 'to tie, turn'. All simplex forms
point to a root noun vik-, but for vicissim, an /-stem is mostly assumed. Schrijver
judges the etymology *viki-dtim ...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: victima 'sacrificial animal' [f. a] (Naev.+)
Pit. *w iktV-ma-. It. cognates: U. eveietu, e.veietu [acc.sg.m. of ppp.] '?'.
If vie-is 'turn, occasion' derives from a root meaning 'to turn', the application of
'turn' in a context of exchange can easily lead to a meaning 'exchange', as in the Gm.
cognates. The victima could then be the 'exchange' with the gods, hence *uikti-
'exchange' + *-ma~. But there are other options. Victima may also be connected with
PIE *ueik- 'to select, sift', as in Skt vec- 'to sift, to separate, to winnow',prd-vikta-
'selected', vivici- 'separating', YAv. hqm.visiia 'to separate completely', YAv.
xvaecaiia- [caus.], MP wextan/weztan 'to sift, select, winnow', Khwar. prwycy- 'to
sift'. One could connect Go. weihs, OHG wTh 'holy', in the same field of religious
terminology as victima.
Bibl.: WH II: 782, EM 732, IEW 1128, LIV ?l.*ueik-.
vTcus 'village, block of houses', [m. o] (P1.+; OLat. micas, uecus, uecos CIL)
Derivatives: vicatim 'street by street' (Sis.+), vicimts [adj./m.] 'neighbour,
neighbouring' (Naev,+), vicinia 'adjoining land, neighbourhood' (P1.+), vicinitas
'proximity, neighbourhood' (Cato+), villa 'rural dwelling, farm' (P1.+), villaticus 'of
a farmstead' (Varro+), vTlicus 'farm-overseer' (P1.+), vilica 'wife of a farm-overseer'
(Cato+), vilicare 'to perform the duties of a farm-overseer' (Cato+).
Pit. *weik-o- 'settlement', *weik-sla- 'farm'.
PIE *ueik- / *uik- 'settlement'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. vesa- 'to sit down, go in', Av.
visa- 'to get ready', Skt. vesa- 'inhabitant', YAv. vaesa- [m.] 'servant (who lives in
the master's house)', Skt. vis- [f.] 'settlement, dwelling-place, people', YAv. vis- [f]
'homestead, community', OP vW- [f.] 'royal house, palace, house, farm' < Ilr. *uic- <
PIE *uik-; Gr. poiKog, oucoq [m.] 'house, household', Myc. wo-i-ko-de /woikon-def
'home', Lith. viespats 'lord', OPr. waispattin 'mistress' [acc.sg.] < *uoik-poti-; OCS
vbst. [£], Ru. ves' (obs.) 'village' < PIE *uik~,...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: video 'to see' [v. II; pf. vidi, ppp. visum] ( Andr.+)
Derivatives: videlicet [adv.] 'evidently, of course' (P1.+); prudens 'well aware,
clever' (P1.+), impritdens 'ignorant, unaware' (P1.+), imprudentia 'ignorance' (Ter,+);
invidere 'to be jealous of, refuse' (P1.+), invidus 'malevolent, envious' (P1.+), invidia
'dislike, jealousy' (P1-+), invidiosus 'unpopular, enviable' (Cato+), pervidere 'to
discern, see fully' (Varro+), providere 'to foresee, see to it' (P1-+), improvisus
'unexpected' (P1.+), revidere 'to pay another visit' (P1.+); viso, -ere 'to go and look,
visit' (P1.+), visus, -its 'sight, vision' (Lucr.+), visitdre 'to see frequently, visit' (P1.+),
circumvisere 'to look round at' (P1.+), convisere 'to watch, visit' (Acc.+), intervisere
'to go and see' (P1.+), provisere 'to be on the look-out' (P1.+), revisere 'to pay
another visit' (P1.+).
Pit. *wid-e- 'to see', *weid-s- 'to see (in future)'. It. cognates: U. uirseto auirseto
[ppp. nom.sg.] 'seen unseen', SPic. videtas [2p.pr.?] 'to see' < *uid~e~; U. revestu
[3s.ipv.II] 'to check' < *re-ueid-s-e-tdd.
PIE *uid-eh 'to see'. IE cognates: Olr. ro-finnadar* 'to find out' < PCI.

de Vaan Latin Etymology: vinda- 'to find' [3s. vinasti] ; OP vindafarna [PN], MP windadan/wind- Ho find,
desire', Parth. wynd- 'to find'; Gr. oiSa 'I know' [pi. i5pev (Att. fapev), ipv. ica5i, pte.
eifidw;] , iSnToi [m.pl.] 'witnesses', aor. iSeiv 'to behold, recognise', ei5opai 'to seem,
appear', ei5oq 'species, appearance'; Arm. egit 'he found', 3s.aor. gt-i, pr. gtanem <
*he-uid-et, git em 'to know' < *uoid-; Lith. veizditi, 3 s. veizdi 'to look (for)' < BS1.
t
*uei?d-; OCS videti, Ru. videt\ SCr. vidjeti 'to see' < PS1. *videti; OPr. waist 'know',
OCS vedeti, Is. vemt, 3s. visit 'to know', Ru. vedat' 'to manage, (obs.) know', Cz.
vedeti 'to know' < PS1. * vedeti < BS1. *uoi?d-; Go, wait, witum 'I know, we know',
weitwops 'witness\fraweitan 'to take revenge', in-weitan 'to worship', OE gewltan
'to die', Go. fairweitjan 'to observe, heed'; Go. witan, -aida 'to heed'.
Priidens regularly reflects *prowidens, *prowident- > *proud- > prudThe pr. viso
reflects an s-desiderative *weid-s-o. The pf. vidi could formally reflect a root aorist
*ueid- or a pf. *uoid-\ in view of the perfective meaning ('I saw'), LIV opts for a root
aorist. The stative pr. *uide- is confirmed by Umbrian and South Picene; we also find
it in Gothic and in Lith. pavydSti, Is. -vydziii 'to envy', but these can easily be
independent formations.
Bibl.: WH II: 378, 784f, EM 541, 733f, IEW 1125-27, Schrijver 1991: 276,
Untermann 2000: 634f., 854f, Schumacher 2004: 664f, 690-695, LIV *ueid-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: viduus 'deprived of husband or wife' [adj. o/a] (P1.+)
Derivatives: vidua [f.] 'widow' (Naev.+), viduitds 'state of being deprived,
widowhood' (P1.+), vidertds 'dearth' (Cato+), viduare 'to deprive of (Lucr.+).
Pit. *wipowo- [adj.].
PIE *hiui-dhheu-o- 'separated'. IE cognates: Olr.fedb, Skt. vidhdva- [f.] 'widow',
r
a-vidhava- [f.] 'not a widow', YAv. viSauua [nom.sg.f.], MP wewag, MoP bewa, Oss.
idcez 'widow' (< PIr. *uidauacT) < Pllr. *Huicf auaH- 'widow, deprived of a partner';
Gr. f)ti3eoq [m.] 'unmarried young man'; OPr. widdewu, OCS vbdova, vbdova [f.], Ru.
vdova (acc. vdovu) < PS1. * vbdova; Go. widuwo, OHG wituwa 'widow'.
Latin and Greek show a them. adj. used for both sexes, whereas the other languages
have a fern. noun. Since the word came to be used for women more often than for
men (in most IE societies), it is unlikely that the f. noun was original, and adjectivized
without any suffix in Latin and Greek. I therefore assume the primacy of the o-stem
adj. in PIE. Initial *du- was dissimilated to *hu- in front of*-ct-; cf. Lubotsky 1994.
}
Bibl.: WH II: 785f,~EM 734, IEW 1127f, Beekes 1992, Lubotsky 1994. divido

formed as an 'inchoative to vied but with the meaning of vietus, thus 'to become

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vigeo 'to be active, flourish' [v. II; pf. vigui, no ppp. attested] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: vigor 'vigour' (Caes.+), vigescere 'to acquire strength' (Lucr.+), vigil,
-is [m.] 'guard, sentry' (P1.+) [gen.pl. vigilum], vigil, -is 'awake, wakeful' (Lucr.+),
vigilia 'the act of keeping watch, patrol' (P1.+), vigilium 'the action of keeping watch'
(Varro), vigilare 'to stay awake' (P1.+); advigilare 'to be on the watch* (PL+),
obvigilare 'to be watchful' (PL), pervigilare 'to stay awake all night' (PL+).
Pit. *weg-e-, *wegli-.
PIE *ueg-eh,- 'to be strong', *ueg-l-i- 'strong'. IE cognates: Skt. vdja- 'contest,
reward', vajayati 'impels', Go. -wakjan 'to waken', OHG wahhen 'to be awake'.
The PIE etymology with *ueg- is adopted by all scholars. The i in vigeo may be
explained as analogically adopted from the vigil (LIV adduces "Homonymenflucht
von vegere" as a possible impetus), or ^rom raising of *e to i after a preceding labial,
as in firmus, vitulus, villus, fiber, pinna, according to Watkins 1973b: 196. The latter
explanation seems more likely, since vigil and vigeo are not closely associated in the
synchronic semantics of Latin (thus also Watkins 1973b: 197), so that analogical
introduction of vi- from vigil seems less likely. Together with vectigal, vigil is the
only stem in *~lis which shows the regular development *-lis > *ls> -I. The nom.sg.
vigil must have caused the (partial) switch from /-declination to a consonant stem
(gen.pl. vigilum), which in its turn explains why we find vigilare already in the
earliest literature. Initial vi- in vigil can be explained from assimilation to the / of the
second syllable, as in nihil, similis, cinis. Final -// instead of *-ul in the nom.sg. must
be due to analogy with other case forms (*veglim, *vegleis, *veglT), where I was
exilis, yielding a stem *vegili~.
Bibl.: WH II: 788, EM 735, IEW 1117f., Sihier 1995: 40, 497, Niedermann 1945:
69, LIV *ueg~. —> vegeo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: viginti 'twenty' [num. indecl.] (Lex XII, P1.+)
Derivatives: vlce(n)simus 'twentieth' (P1.+; <vlg- > Sail., Col.), vicie(n)s 'twenty
times' (Cic.+), viceni 'twenty at a time' (Varro, Cic.+); duodeviginti 'eighteen' (P1.+),
undeviginti 'nineteen' (Cic.+).
Pit. *wlkenti (» *wigentT), *wIkenssamo~.
PIE *dui-dkmt-ihi 'twenty'. IE cognates: Olr. fiche, fichit [dat./acc,], OW uceint,
MW figgit; Skt. vimsati- [fi], YAv. visaiti, Khot. bista, MP wist, Oss. ysscez/inscej, Gr.
E'IKOOI, H. also eeiKom, Dor. pvtcaTi, Arm. k'san, Alb. zet, ToB ikdm, ToA wiki
'twenty'.
The unexpected voiced g in Latin viginti is explained by Kortlandt 1983a: 101 from
voicing after a nasal in the words for '70' and '90', and subsequent analogical
introduction in the other decads. The i of -int- must be secondary for *-ent~, it may
have arisen phonetically between the fs of the surrounding syllables (thus Coleman).
Lat. vices imus from vlkensumo- (CIL 6.7872) shows the same PIE suffix *-tmHo- as
Indo-Iranian. Originally, 'twenty' was formed as a dual form meaning 'two teens'.
PIE *dui-dkmt-ihi is redundantly marked as a pair by *dui~ 'two' and the dual ending.
Bibl.: WH II: 788fi, EM 735, IEW 1177, Kortlandt 1983a, Schrijver 1991: 83, 182,
Coleman 1992: 397f„ 414, Sihier 1995: 418ffi, Meiser 1998: 172f. centum, decern,
-ginta

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vllis 'cheap, worthless' [adj. /] (P1.+)
Derivatives: vllitas 'cheapness' (P1.+).
Pit. *wesli-.
PIE *ues-li- [adj.], IE cognates: Hit. uas- 'to buy' (< *uos- / *«s-).
A deverbal adj. in *-li- with passive meaning: *ues-Ii- 'what can be bought'. The
expected outcome *velis was raised to vilis, possibly under the influence of the the
preceding labial (thus Watkins 1973b: 196).
Bibl.: WH II: 789f., EM 736, IEW 1173, LIV 2.*ues-. venus/m
vincid, -Ire 'to tie up, bind' [v. IV; pf. vTnxi, ppp. vinctum] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: vicia 'vetch* (Cato+), vinc(u)lum 'bond, chain' (P1.+), vinctio 'binding
together' (Varro+), vinctura 'which binds' (Varro+), vinctus, -us 'tie' (Varro+);
circumvincTre 'to bind round' (P1.+), devincire 'to tie fast, bind' (P1.+); provincia
'special task of a magistrate, command, district' (PI.+), provincialis 'of or belonging
to a province' (Varro+).
Pit. *wink-(e/o~) 'to tie, bind'. It. cognates: U. preuislatu, preuilatu [3s.ipv.II] 'to
tie' < *prai-uinkela-tod, denom. to *ui(n)k(e)lo~.
PIE *ui-n-k-. IE cognates: see s.v. vinco.
Lat. vicia 'vetch' (< 'winding plant') may continue the nasalless root *uik~. The noun
vinculum is derived from the verbal stem *uink- (either with *-(e)lo- or with *-tlo~).
The nasal spread from the present to the pf. and ppp., whereas vincid itself can be a
derivative of the earlier pr. *uink- (cf. vinco), or from a nominal derivative
*uink-i(o)-; Meiser 2003: 122 explains it as a back-formation to the pf. vwxT. Lat.
provincia seems based on an adj. *pro-wink-io- 'load, burden, charge' > 'task', cf.
Hamp 1997: 124f. The reconstruction of the root with a final labiovelar is based on
the very uncertain appurtenance of Gr. l\|/ov 'ivy; prison' here; but Lat. vicis, U.
preuislatu (thus Meiser 2003; 122) and the Gm. forms cited s.v. vinco point to a
non-labialized velar.
Bibl.: WH II: 781, 791, EM 732, 736, IEW 1130f.,' Untermann 2000: 576f., Meiser
2003: 122, LIV *uieku-, ?l.*ueik-. —» vicis, vinci...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vinco, -ere 'to conquer, overcome' [v. Ill; pf. vici, ppp. victum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: victor 'winner, conqueror' (Naev.+), victoria 'victory' (Naev.+),
victoridtus 'with an image of Victory' (Cato+), victrix 'victorious' (of a woman)'
(P1.+), vincibilis 'that can be won' (Ter.+); convincere 'to prove wrong, find guilty'
(P1.+), pervicax 'stubborn, determined' (Ter.+), pervicacia 'resolution' (Enn.+),
pervicus 'stubborn' (Acc.), pervincere 'to prevail over' (P1.+), invictus 'invincible,
not overcome'^ (PI.+)•
Pit. *wink-(e/o-) 'to tie, bind' > 'tie in, overcome', aor. *weik~. It. cognates: O.
uincter [3s.pr.ps.] 'to prove someone's guilt', probably borrowed from Latin; Pael.
uicturei [dat.sg.] 'winner' < Lat. victor-; O. vikturrai [dat.sg.] 'Victoria', loanword
from Latin.
PIE *ui-n-k- [pr.] 'to bend, tie', *uik-to- [ppp ]. IE cognates: Olr. fichid* 'to battle'
< PCI. *uike/o~, Olr. fecht [f. a] , MW gweith [f.], OBret. gueth 'battle' < *uik-td-;
Skt. pr. viviktds [3du.act.], pf. vivyaca [3s.act.] 'to contain, embrace', vyacas- [n.]
'extent, content', MP winj-, ps. winfih- 'to be contained' < Ilr. *uik/c~, *uiak/c-; Gr.
Eucco 'to give way, yield' < *ueik-\ Lith. veikti, Is. veikiu 'to work; overcome', Latv.
vikstu, vTkt 'to give way, bend', viksts 'yielding, soft', Lith. vikriis 'agile, swift,
skilful', veikus / veikus 'quick, strong, firm', Latv. veiks 'diligent, agile, skilful,
limited', OIc. vega 'to battle, kill', Go. -waih, OIc. vd 'battled', Go. wigana [dat.sg.]
'battle'; Go. waihsta 'corner', MHG weigen 'to waver', OE wicga 'beetle', OFr. OE
wag, OE weg 'wall' (< *uoiko-); OIc. gjqfa-vixl 'gift-exchange', OE wehsal
'commerce, money', OHG wehsal, wehsil 'exchange, commerce'; OHG wehha,
wohha, OS wika, OE wucu, wicu, OIc. vfka 'week' (< ""change') < *uik-n~.
Probably from the same root as vincio, pace LIV: 'to bend' can easily develop into
'overcome, conquer' (one bending the other) or 'give in, yield' (the one being bent by
the ot...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vlnum 'wine' [n. o] ( Andr.+)
Derivatives: vinea 'vines in a vineyard; moveable penthouse' (Lex XII, P1.+),
vineaticus 'connected with vine-growing' (Cato+), vinetum 'vineyard' (Varro+),
vinaceus 'the refuse from wine-pressing' (Cato+), vTndlia, -ium 'the name of two
wine festivals' (Varro+), vinarius 'for/of producing wine' (PI.+), vinolentus 'of
immoderate wine consumption' (P1.+), vinosus 'immoderately fond of wine' (P1.+),
villum 'small quantity of wine' (Ter.); vinibua 'female tippler of wine* (Lucil.).
Pit. *wino-. It. cognates: Fal. uinom, uino [acc.sg.], U. vinu [acc.sg.], vinu, uinu,
Vol. uinu [abl.sg.] 'wine'.
PIE *ueih(6)n, *uihe/on-m, *uihn- [m./f.] 'vine; wine'. IE cognates: Hit.
r r r
uiian- [c.] 'wine' < *uihon-, (d)Uiniiant- 'wine (deified)', CLuw. uiniia- 'of wine',
r
HLuw. wiiana/i- 'vine'; Gr. oivoq, dial. poivo<; [m.], Arm. gini (< *uoiniio~), Alb.
Tosk vere, Geg vene 'wine' < *uoihno~.
}
For vindemia, see s.v. emo. The IE words for 'vine' and 'wine' go back to an w-stem
derived from the root 'to weave, wrap', hence 'vine' was referred to as 'the weaving
one* (Beekes 1987b: 24). Whereas Hittite preserves the «-stem, it has been thematized
outside Anatolian, either with (Gr, Arm, Alb.) or without (Latin) introduction of an
o-grade into the root. This explanation renders Oettinger's explanation for the
Anatolian forms (2003: 143) superfluous. He assumes a development *uoih/no- >
*uoino- > *ueno~ > *uiyana~, with diphthongization of *e, but the Italic forms would
remain unexplained in his scenario.
Bibl.: WH II: 792f, EM 737f, IEW 1120-22, Giacomelli 1963: 258, Beekes 1987b,
Untermann 2000: 857. —> emo, vied, vit is

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: violo, -are 'to violate, disturb' [v. I] (Lex Sacra CIL 1.366, P1.+)
Derivatives: violentus 'violent' (P1.+), violenter 'violently' (Ter.+), violentia
'violence' (P1-+), inviolabilis 'indestructible' (Lucr.+).
Pit. *wijelo- 'chasing, violating'.
PIE *uiH-elo-. IE cognates: see s.v. via.
Probably derived from a deverbal adj. in *-elo- to the root *uiH- 'to strive after,
chase'. Since the root *uiH- of vis 'power' is only nominal in the IE languages, it is
less attractive to derive *vielo- from that root. Still, the Romans must have associated
violo with vis, since derivatives in -(u)lentus are characteristically made from nouns
(cf. opulentus).
Bibl.: WH II: 801, EM 740, IEW 1123f., LIV *ueih. via, vis
r
vTpera 'poisonous snake' [f. <?] (Cic.+)
Derivatives: vlperlnus 'of a viper' (Acc.+).
Pit. *weip-es-a-7
PIE *ueip-es-?
There are two competing etymologies. Pliny's explanation as *vTvo-para 'who gives
birth to living young* has been adopted by WH and Leumann, but it has several
drawbacks. Semantically, *vTvo-para would mean 'who gives birth to a living one'
(cf. puerpera 'who gives birth (to a child)', but this goes for all mammals too, and is
therefore hardly a specific description of snakes. It is clear from Pliny, Nat. 10, 62
(82) that the Romans knew that snakes lay eggs (ova pariimt serpentes), but observed
the hatchlings as coming out of their eggs in the snake itself (dein singulis diebus
singulos parit). Formally, since the stress would be on the penultimate syllable,
*wTwo-para would not contract (cf. Meiser 1998: 92); the loss of *-wo- could be due
to haplology, but this is unattractive since vTvus was always present in the language,
and Apuleius even has an adj. viviparus 'giving birth to live young'. Thus, this
explanation is better dropped. The alternative etymology is that vTpera continues
*weip~es-a to the root *wip/b- 'to swing, agitate' (cf. vibrdre), which makes semantic
sense. Formally this solution is not completely tra...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vir 'man' [m, o] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: virilis 'of a man, male' (P1.+), virago, -inis [fi] 'strong or warlike
woman' (P1.+), virdcius 'manly' (Varro), virltim [adv.] 'man by man, individually'
(P1.+), virosus 'having an excessive sexual craving for men' (Lucil.+), virtus, -iutis
'manly qualities, valour' (Andr.+); evirare 'to unman' (Varro+).
Pit, *wiro-. It. cognates: U. uiro, ueiro [acc.pl.] 'company of men, troops' [n.] <
*wiro~.
PIE *uiH-ro- 'man, young man, warrior'. IE cognates: Olr. fer, Skt. vlra- 'man,
hero', Av. vTra- 'man, human', Lith. vyras 'man, husband', Latv. virs, Go. wair 'man'
< *uiro- < *uIro-, ToA wir 'youthful, young, fresh*.
The short vowel in Latin is due to Dybo's law, cf. Schrijver 1991: 340. Since nouns in
-ago are usually built to nouns or verbs in -a-, Leumann 1977: 369 suggests that
virago might be built to the form vira 'femina' found in Paul, ex F. Alternatively, WH
wonder whether virago 'warlike woman' was formed after imago 'image' - thus
meaning 'man-like'. The adj. virilis may be formed on the basis of the gen.sg. viri;
thence was formed virl-tim.
Bibl.: WH II: 796fi, EM 738fi, IEW 1177f., Schrijver 1991: 235, 340, Untermann
2000: 858f. vis

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vireo 'to be green' [v. II; pf. virul] (Lucr.+)
Derivatives: virescere 'to turn green' (Lucr.+), viridis [adj.] 'green' (Cato+),
viridare 'to be green' (Acc.+), viridulus 'young and tender' (Naev.); praeviridans
'very green' (Lab.). ,
Pit. *w(e)is-1
IE cognates: OPr. weisin 'fruit' [acc.sg.], Lith. veTsti, 3s. veisia 'to breed, rear', visti,
3s. vysta 'to multiply, breed', vaisius 'fruit'; OIc. visir 'sprout' [?]; OE wise 'sprout,
stem' [f.]; OHG wTsa 'meadow' [f.].
Uncertain etymology. None of the adduced set of cognates (Lat. 'green', Baltic
'multiply, fruit', Gm. 'sprout, meadow') undoubtedly belong together.
Bibl.: WH II: 797, EM 739, IEW 1133, Schrijver 1991: 238, LIV l.*ueis-.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: virga 'shoot, twig, rod' [f. a\ (P1.+)
Derivatives: virgeus 'made of twigs' (Cato+), virgator 'who wields a rod' (P1.+),
virgulta, -drum 'brushwood' (Cato+); virgidemia 'a harvest of rods' (P1.+).
Pit. *wizg-l
PIE *uis-g-? IE cognates: OIc. visk 'wisp', Swed. viska 'small broom', OHG wise
'wisp', OE wiscian, weoskian (< *wiskon) 'to plait a hurdle'.
Virga has been derived from *wizg-a- and connected with other IE words of the form
*uis-K- which refer to sticks, bundles or similar objects. Yet the connection with the
root *uiH- 'to weave', upheld in prelaryngealistic studies, must now be abandoned.
Also, the velar consonants do not always agree, and the meaning of verbs of the type
'to quiver, shake' (Lith. vizgu, vizgkii 'to tremble', vizgoti 'to waver') is too far off.
Only the Germanic forms in *uisk- are close enough to inspire some confidence in
their connection with virga. The adj. virgultus, attested as a noun from Cato onwards,
presupposes the dim. virgula, which is only attested from Cicero onwards.
Bibl.: WH II: 797f., EM 739, IEW 1133f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: virgo, -inis 'girl of marriageable age; virgin' [f. (m.) n] (Andr.+)
Derivatives: virgindlis 'of a girl of marriageable age' (P1.+), virginarius 'concerned
with girls of a marriageable age' (P1-+), virgineus 'of a girl; virgin' (Lucr.+).
WH interpret virgo as an individuation of virga 'twig, rod', implying a shift from
'young, virgin' > 'young plant'. Ledo-Lemos 2002 posits a compound *wir-gwdn~
'young woman', with an unacceptable formal and semantic analysis of the word for
'man' (recte PIE *uiHro-) and formally of PIE 'woman'.
Bibl.: WH II: 799, EM 739f., IEW 1133f.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: virus 'venom, poison' [n. o] (Lucil.+; almost restricted to nom.acc.sg.)
Derivatives: virosus 'with an unpleasant smell or taste' (Cato+).
Pit. *weis-o~(s-) [n.] 'poison'.
PIE *ueis [nom.], *uis-os [gen.] [n.] 'poison'. IE cognates: Mir. fi [gender and stem
unknown], W. gwy 'poison' < PCI. *uiso- 'poison', Skt. visa- [n.], YAv. visa- [n.]
'venom, poison'; YAv. m [n.] 'poison, poisonous juice'; Gr. foq [m.]'poison', To A
was, ToB wase 'poison, venom' (< *uiso~).
I
I
vitis 683
The restriction to nom.acc.sg. may mean that virus is an original n. s-stem *uiros-\ the
origin of the s-stem inflection is unclear. Szemerenyi reconstructs *ueisos for Latin,
and argues that the Greek word (Pindar+) has secondary lengthening in hiatus.
Matasovic argues that the length of -/- is best accounted for by positing an original
root-noun *ueis / *uis-os; Lat. preserved the stem of the nominative, whereas the
other languages generalized the stem of the oblique cases.
Bibl.: WH II: 800, EM 740, IEW 1134, Szemerenyi 1989: 91, Schrijver 1991: 232,
Matasovic 2004: 130.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vis, vis 'strength, force' [f.] (Andr.+; acc.sg. vim, nom.pl. vires, gen.pl. virium)
Derivatives: For vindex, see s.v. died.
Pit. *wis.
PIE *uiH-s 'strength'. IE cognates: Skt. vdyas- [n.] 'power, food', Gr. [f.] 'power,
strength', acc. iv(a), instr. T(pi, Hsch. yi; (= pic;) • 'strength' < *uiH-s, -m.
Traditionally analysed as an s-stem *uiH-s- with the same root as in *uiH-ro- 'man'.
The Gr. and Lat. cognates, however, are more easily explained from an original root
noun. If this is correct, the Skt. formation and the Lat. plural forms must be
secondary. Any relation to *uehi- 'to wind' or to *ueih 'to hunt, pursue' is
r r
uncertain.
Bibl.: WH II: 793f, 800f, EM 737, 740, IEW 1123f, Untermann 2000: 146.
died, vir V

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: viscum 'mistletoe; bird-lime from the berries of the mistletoe' [n. o] (P1.+; PI. also
nom.sg. viscus)
Derivatives: viscatus 'smeared with bird-lime' (Lucil.+).
Pit. *wisko-l
IE cognates: Gr. i^oq [m.] 'mistletoe, -berry, the bird-lime prepared from it', OHG
wihsela 'morello', Ru. visnja 'cherry'.
A PIE word or a European loanword indicating 'mistletoe' or another fruit-bearing
tree. The original form may have been *wiks- (in which case Latin has undergone a
metathesis) or fwiKsk-, with various simplifications of the medial cluster in the
different languages.
Bibl.: WH II: 801 f, EM 741, IEW 1134.

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vitis 'grape-vine' [f. i] (Enn.+)
Derivatives: vitilis 'made of wickerwork' (Cato+), viteus 'of a vine' (Varro+),
vitigineus 'of a vine or its wood' (Cato+), viUarium 'nursery for vines' (Cato+),
Pit. *witi-.
PIE *uhii-ti-. IE cognates: see s.v. vied.
Derivative in *-/;- from the root *uhi- 'to weave, wrap', hence 'weaving'. Compare
r
the PIE etymology of vinum.
Bibl.: WH II: 804, EM 741, IEW 1128-22, Schrijver 1991: 245, LIV *ujeh,-. ->
vied, vinum

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vitium 'defect, fault' [n. o] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: vitiare 'to spoil, invalidate' (Ter.+), vitiosus 'faulty, defective' (P1.+),
vitiligo, -inis 'a form of skin eruption, psoriasis' (Lucil.+), vituperare 'to declare
invalid, find fault with' (P1.+).
Pit. *witio- 'deviation, fault'.
PIE *(d)ui-tio- 'apart, wrong'. IE cognates: see s.v. duo.
WH reconstruct a PIE adj. *ui-ti-om 'separation, deviation' to *(d)ui- 'apart',
comparing Skt. nitya- 'down'. This would amount to a similar dissimilation of two
dentals as in PIE *dui-tero- > Ilr, Gm, Slav. * (H)ui-tero- 'second, following'.
Indo-Iranian, Tocharian and Umbrian have a form *du(i)-tio- 'second', but this is an
/o-derivative of *du(i)-to-, and hence does not invalidate the solution for vitium. The
verb vituperare presupposes an adj. *viti-paro- (with regular simplification of the
lo-stem in the first member to -i-) or *vito-paro- 'bringing up a fault'. Vitiligo belongs
to the names of diseases in -igo, but cannot be directly derived from vitium since -/-
would remain unexplained.
Bibl.: WH II: 803f, 808, EM 741 f., IEW 1175f, Leumann 1977: 296, 369, 390,
Lindner 2002: 129. duo

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vito, -are 'to move out of the way, avoid' [v. I] (P1.+)
Derivatives: devitare 'to avoid' (P1.+), evitdre 'to avoid' (Varro+); Vitula 'goddess
of joy' (Varro).
Pit. *wf/o- 'wanted'.
PIE *uihpto- 'wished for'. IE cognates: see s.v. via.
WH assume *vi-ito 'to go out of the way', with a preverb *vi- 'apart' < *dui- as in Ilr.
Yet frequentative verbs in -itare are generally recent, which excludes an early
dissimilation *dui-ita- > *ui-ita-. The alternative connection with Lat. invitus
'unwilling' (< *n-uiH-to~), VFJ 'you want' seems formally more probable to me.
Semantically, we must then assume that *vitus was back-formed to *de-vitus
'avoiding'. Whether vitulari 'to utter a cry of joy' (Naev.+) also belongs here is
uncertain.
Bibl.: WH II: 805, 807, EM 742, LIV *ueih.
r

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vltricus 'stepfather' [m. o) (CIL 1.583, Cic.+)
WH and IEW derive vitricus from PIE *(d)ui-tero- 'second, other' (as attested in Ilr.,
Germanic, Slavic), but this does not explain long f. I have no other solution.
Bibl.: WH II: 805, EM 742, IEW 1175f, Fruyt 1986: 232f.
vitrum 'glass; the plant 'woad' {isatis tinctoria), from whose leaves blue dye was
produced' [n. o] (Lucr.+)
I
Derivatives: vitreus 'resembling glass; made of glass' (Varro+).
Pit. *wedro- 'glass'.
PIE *ued-ro- 'water-like'. IE cognates: see s.v. unda.
Szemerenyi 1989: 24f. derives uitrum from *ued-ro- on the basis of a semantic
parallel in Middle Iranian, where 'glass' is derived from the word for 'water': ap-aka-
'water-like' > 'glass'. The Latin change of *dr > tr is regular. The change of *wet- >
wit- is also found in vitulus 'calf and in other Latin words with a labial preceding *e.
The plant and its dye will have been denominated after the colour of glass (in
antiquity, a transparent green with a yellowish to blueish paleness).
Bibl.: WH II: 805fi, EM 742. -»unda, uter

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vitulus 'calf [m. o] (Cato+)
Derivatives: vitellus 'little calf (PI.), 'yolk of an egg' (Varro+), vitulTmis 'of a calf
(P1-+).
Pit. *wet-e!o- 'yearling, calf. It. cognates: U. vitlu [acc.sg.m.], vitluf, uitlu
[acc.pl.m.], vitlaf, uitla [acc.pl.f.] < *uete!o~, -a- 'calf.
PIE *uet- 'year'. IE cognates: see s.v. vetus.
Bibl.: WH II: 807, EM 742, IEW 1175, Untermann 2000: 859f. vetus

metadata.source_file: de Vaan - Etymological Dictionary of Latin (2008).pdf
metadata.dictionary: de Vaan Etymological Dictionary of Latin 2008
metadata.author: de Vaan

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vlverra 'ferret (or similar animal)' [f. a] (Plin.+) ;
PIE *ue(r)-uer- (vel sim.). IE "cognates: Molr. iora riia 'squirrel', Scottish Gaelic
feorag 'squirrel', OPr. weware 'squirrel', Lith. vaiverjs (vaivaras, vaivarys) 'male
polecat or marten', vaiveri, vovere, Latv. vavere, vaveris, ORu. veverica, Sin.
veverica, Cz. veverka, Bulg. ververica, OE ac-weorna, OHG eihhurno, OIc. ikorni
'squirrel'.
Irish, BSI. and Gm. point to a word *(we(r))-wer- 'squirrel', and Lith. vaiveris shows
that a shift to a meaning 'marten' is conceivable. In spite of their different status as
herbivore vs. carnivore, they may have been compared due to their similar darting
movements and their tree habitat, or on account of the fur for which they were hunted.
Folk etymology may explain the ultimate outcome vi-ver-ra.
Bibl.: WH II: 808, EM 742fi, IEW 1166, EIEC 540.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vivo, -ere 'to live' [v. Ill; pf. vfxT, ppp. vTcturn] (Lex XII+)
Derivatives: vivus 'alive' (Naev.+), vTvescere 'to come to life' (Lucr.+), vivax
'long-lived, energetic' (Afran.+), vividus 'full of vital force' (Lucr.+), victus, -us
'food, way of life' (P1.+), vTctitare 'to keep oneself alive' (P1.+); conviva [m.] 'guest'
(P1.+), convfvium 'dinner-party' (P1.+), convivarT 'to feast' (Enn.+), revivescere 'to
come to life again' (Ter.+); vita 'life' (P1.+), vitalis 'of life, life-giving' (P1.+), evitare
'to kill' (Enn.+).
Pit. *gwiwe/o- 'to live', *gwiwo- 'alive', *gwIta- 'life'. It. cognates: Ven. vivoi
[dat.sg.] 'alive', O. bivus [nom.pi.m.] 'alive' < *gwTwo-, O. biitam [acc.sg.] 'life'.
PIE *gwih -ue/o- 'to live', *gwih -uo- 'alive'. IE cognates: Skt. pr.jivati, aor.jivit
3 3
[3s.inj.act.], pf. jijiva [3s.act,] 'to live1, jTvita- 'living', also [n.] 'living being, life',
jlva- 'alive, living person', Av. juua- 'to live\juua- [adj.] 'alive', YAv. juuaiiant-
'making alive', OP jvva- 'to live', jiva- 'alive'; Gr. epicov, pwbvai [aor.] 'to live' (<
*gwiheh\ pioq 'life' (< *gwtH-o-\ pioxf) 'life', pioioq [m.] 'id.' (< *gwih -eto-);
r r 3
OPr. gijwans [acc.pl.] 'alive', giwasi [2s.pr.] 'you live', Lith. gyvas, Latv. dzivs
'alive', Latv. dzivu [ls.pr.act] 'to live', OCS zivb, Ru. zivoj 'alive'; Lith. gyvata
'life', OCS zivotb [m.] 'life'; Lith. gyti, 3s. gyja 'to recover, heal (intr.)', Latv. dzit 'to
heal (intr.)', OCS ziti, Is. zivg, Ru. zif 'to live' < *gwHi-.
The adj. vivus and the pr. vivere are both inherited. The pf. and ppp.-stem vie- is
analogical after verbs in which *-eugw- yielded *-uw- and then -it-, giving the
impression that the velar stop had disappeared (Meiser 1998: 208), for instance fluo.
The noun vita 'life' could reflect *wfwita < *gwiH-uo-t-a-, cf. Lith. gyvata 'life'; thus
Meiser 1998: 92. But vita could also directly reflect *gyviH-t-h, which would enable
r
us to derive O. biitam from the same Pit. noun (Leumann 1977: 3...

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vix 'hardly' [adv.] (P1.+)
Pit. *wiks [nom.sg.] / *wiksu [loc.pl.].
PIE *uik- 'a turn, bend'.
A derivation from *uiki- 'turn, occasion' seems semantically possible ('only
occasionally' > 'hardly'), but a nom.sg. *uikis is morphologically not the most
straightforward candidate, and we would not expect *-kis > -ks after a short vowel in
an open syllable. We may therefore adopt WH's solution of deriving vix from the root
of vinco. A preform *uik-s could be the nom.sg. of a root noun *uik~ 'bond, tie' or
'victory'; clearly, the former meaning can lead to 'hardly' in a more straightforward
may: 'bond' > adv. 'within bonds' > 'hardly'. As an alternative, we may consider a
loc.pl. *itik-su 'with ties, within bonds', compare mox 'soon' < PIE *moksu for the
loss of the final short vowel.
Bibl.: WH II: 810, EM 743, IEW 1128f. vicis, vincid, vinco

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vola 'hollow in the palm of the hand or the sole of the foot' [f. a] (Varro+)
Pit. *wela-1
PIE *uel-h - 'round'? IE cognates: OIc. valr 'round' < *uol-o-.
2
Semantically, a connection with volvd 'to turn' < PIE *uel-u- is attractive. Lat. vola
can go back to *uela-, not to *uola- (which would yield *vala). The connection
preferred by IEW, viz. with Gr. -yvaXov 'a hollow', is improbable.
Bibl.: WH II: 825, EM 749, IEW 393ff. volvd

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: the will', volterkon [acc.sg.n.] 'voluntary' < *wol-tr-iko- to a noun *wol-tro-. Maybe
O. velliam [acc.sg.] 'legal demand' < *wel-jd-; U. veltu, ehueltu [3s.ipv.II] 'to order',
ehvelklu [acc.sg.] 'vote'.
PIE *uelh [aor.], *ueih 'to strive after, pursue', IE cognates: Skt. vfnite [3s.med.],
r r
aor. \jta [3s.inj.med.], pf. vavjse 'to choose, prefer', vrta- 'chosen, picked, picking',
vara- [m.] 'wish, desire', OAv. var- [pr.] 'to choose', Av.-vairiia- 'preferable', vara-
[n.] 'wish, 6esixe\frauuasi- 'tutelary spirit', OP avmavata [3s.ipf.med.] 'to choose'
< Pllr. *uarH-\ Gr. X&>, Xfjq, Xf\, Xw^ieg 'to want, wish', Xfjpa [n.] 'will, courage,
insolence', Xfjoig (also Xfj'tg Dor.) 'will, choice' (if related, the Greek root reflects
earlier *pXri- which probably originated in the root-aorist); Lith. velti 'to wish, prefer'
(Is. OLith. velmi) , OCS veleti, Is. veljg, Ru. velet' 'to command, order', Sin. veleti
'to want, order, say', Is. velim < PS1. *veleti, OCS voliti 'to want, wish', Cz. vol iti 'to
choose' < PS1. *voliti, OCS volja, Ru. volja 'will, wish, freedom' < PS1. *vdja [f. ja] ,
Go. wiljan 'to want', OHG wala [f.], OIc. val [n.] 'choice'.
The pr. shows the regular change of *~el- > *-ol- before non-front vowels. According
to Dunkel 1998: 97, volo and volumus may continue the pr.sb. *wel-o, *ivel-o-mos.
The conjunction vel retains the original 2s. *welsi > *well > vel. The 2s. vis is
explained from *ueiH-s 'you wish' to the root *uiH- 'to strive after' by Meiser 1998:
224; Cowgill 1978 derives vis by phonetic development from *wells, but this involves
a lot of special pleading. The negated verb nolo is contracted from *ne + volo, vis,
volt, while malo 'I prefer' and its older counterpart mavolo reflect * mags-weld <
*magis-welo 'I want more'.
Bibl.: WH H: 743, 828-830, EM 717f, 750, IEW 1137f., Lejeune 1974: 336, 340£,
Cowgill 1978, Untermann 2000:203,833f„ Weiss 2002:358f., LIV *uelh. ^ valeo, volup
r

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: volpes, -is 'fox' [m. /] (P1.+; CLat. vulpes)
Derivatives: volpirtan 'to behave like a fox' (Varro+).
Pit. *wolp-i- 'fox'.
PIE *(H)ulp-i- 'marten, cat, vel sim.'. IE cognates: Av. raopi- 'fox' < Ilr. *laupi-,
Av. urupi- 'marten' (vel sim.) < Ilr. *lupi~, MP gurbag, MoP gurba 'cat' < *ujpaka~;
Lith. vilpisys 'wild cat' < *ujp-i-; Skt. lopasa- 'jackal', Khot. rruvasa- 'jackal', MP
ropas, Oss. ruvas/robas 'fox' < Ilr. *laupaca- (< *h loup-ek-?). Probably unrelated:
2
Gr. AXTOTRR)^, -EKOC, [f.] 'fox', Arm. alowes, gen. -esow 'fox', Lith. lape, Latv. lapsa
'fox', W. llywarn, MCo. lowarn, Bret, louarn < PCI. *lop-erno- < *h lop- 'fox'.
2
In theory, from *uelp-i- or *u(o)lp-i~. Since we find *ulp- in Baltic and in Persian
'cat', volpes probably continues the zero grade of the root. In Ilr., metathesis to
*lup-i- took place (> Av. urupi-), whence *laupi- was derived.
Bibl.: WH II: 830, EM 751, IEW 1179, Clackson 1994: 95, Schrijver 1998, de Vaan
2000. -»lupus

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: voltus, -us 'facial expression, face' [m. u] (P1.+; CLat. vultus)
Derivatives: volta, -drum [n.pl.] 'face, look' (Enn, Lucr.).
Pit. *we/oltu-.
PIE *u(e)l-tu- 'gaze, view'. IE cognates: Olr. fil, feil/'fail 'there is', MW gwelet,
MCo. gweles, OBret. guelet 'to see' < PCI. *uel-e/o-; Olr. fili, filed 'seer' < *uel-et-
(Irslinger 2002: 56); Go. wulpus [m.] 'wealth' < *uftu- 'respect'.
Probably from *wel-tu- 'gaze, view' > 'look' > 'face', thus Hamp 1984a: 149.
Bibl.: WH II: 831, EM 751, IEW 1136fi, Schumacher 2004: 669-675, LIV 3.*uel-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: volup 'with pleasure' [adv.] (Naev.+; Naev. volop)
Derivatives: voluptas 'pleasure' (P1.+), voluptabilis 'causing pleasure' (PI.),
voluptarius 'of or devoted to pleasure' (P1.+), Volupia 'goddess of pleasure' (Varro+),
Pit. *we/olpi.
PIE *u(e/o)lp-i [n.] 'hope, pleasure'. IE cognates: Gr. eXjiopat 'to expect, hope,
think', pf. eoJara, eXTrwpfj 'hope' (for *EXJIXOXT}?[J e),forit;, -ISoq [f.] 'hope'.
The ms. variant volupe has probably arisen in volupest 'volup est', cf. Torzi 1991.
Nevertheless, volup might reflect Pit. *w(e/o)lpi 'hopefully', which could continue
the n. of a PIE /-stem 'hope' (which also Gr. eAmS- could reflect). An apocopated
Proto-Latin form *we/olp could have acquired an anaptyctic vowel between I and p.
Admittedly, there is no anaptyxis in volt 'he wants', but 3s. *wel-t could have been
restored on the model of the other paradigmatic forms. In order to explain -it- from a
real Pit. vowel, Hamp 1980 reconstructs *uel-hp- 'desire reaching', i.e. 'attaining
s
one's wish', a cp. from the root of volo (*uelh) and the root of apio 'to reach'. In a
r
syntagm *uel-hip *hesti 'there is desire-reaching' with enclitic copula, Pit.
t
*welap-est would have given *velup(esi) > volup. Yet in the absence of any evidence
for the combination of *uelh plus *h|p-, of a root noun *uelh, and of the forced
r r
semantics, this etymology does not seem likely.
Bibl.: WH II: 834, EM 752, IEW 1137f., LIV *uelp-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: volva 'womb' [f. a] (Varro+; CLat. vulva)
Derivatives: volvula 'small womb' (Naev.).
Pit. *we/olwa-.
PIE *u(e/o)l-u-h -. IE cognates: see s.v. volvo.
2
The connection with PIE *gwelbh- 'womb' (Gr. SEAxpuc;) is only possible for the
spelling volba, bulba, but this appears late in Imperial times, and cannot be relied on.
Hence, we need an etymon in VOLat. *-w-. Since the womb 'envelopes' the foetus, a
derivation from volvo is only natural, and it also explains the suffix *-w~. The ablaut
can be assumed to have been the same as in the verb.
Bibl.: WH II: 831f„ EM 751, IEW 1140-44, LIV 2.*uel-. valva, volvo

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: volvo, -ere 'to (cause to) go round, roll' [v. Ill; pf. voluT, ppp. voliitum] (Naev.+)
Derivatives: volubilis 'rolling, spinning' (Pac.+), voliimen 'roll of papyrus' (Varro+),
volucra / -is 'caterpillar on vines' (Col.+), involucre/-um [n.] 'cover, wrap' (P1.+),
volutare 'to think over, roll' (P1.+); convolvulus 'caterpillar' (Cato+), involvulus
'caterpillar' (P1.+); convolvere 'to roll up, chum' (Varro+), evolvere 'to evict, unfold,
roll out' (P1.+), involvere 'to wrap up, cover' (Varro+), pervolvere 'to roll over'
(Ter.+),provolvere 'to roll forward' (Ter.+), revolvere 'to roll back, relapse* (Ter.+).
Pit. *welu-e/o~.
PIE *uel-u- 'to wind'. IE cognates: Oh.fillid < PCI. *uel-n-o- 'bend', Bret, goalenn
'green twig' < *uel~, Gr. etlijco 'to wind round', pf. efr.iifj.ai < *p£-pXai-p.ai, £M5-xpov
'envelop, shell, container' (<PGr. *pe?a)-xpov), eXtipa 'plough-beam', aXumi; 'chain',
eiXeco 'to rull, revolve' < *uel-n-; Arm. egel 'he turned', Go. walwjan, OE wealwian
'revolve (onself)', ToB wal- 'to curl (intr./tr.)'.
Volumen, involucre and volubilis have the productive lengthening of the stem vowel
*-it- in derivatives. Short -u- appears to be preserved in volucra / -is with its
specialized meaning 'caterpillar' < *'wrapping itself up'.
Bibl.: WH II: 832-834, EM 752, IEW 1140-44, Schrijver 1991: 296, LIV 2.*uel-.
volva

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: v5mer 'ploughshare' [m. r] (Cato+; also nom.sg. vomis Verg. Col., vomeris Cato)
Pit. *woywsmi-7
PIE *uogwh-ni- ' coulter, ploughshare'? IE cognates: Gr. ocpvtq 'plough(share)'
(Hsch.), OPr. wagnis 'coulter', OIc. vangsni, OHG waganso 'ploughshare'.
Greek and Old Prussian point to a possible preform *uogwhni-; one could then posit
*uogwhmi- for Latin. Yet the latter would probably yield *uoumi- > *uumi- (unless
there was a phonological constraint on *uu~, and *uoumi~ regularly yielded *vomi-).
Alternatively, one might posit a suffix *~smi-, hence *uogwh-smi- > *uogzmi- >
*uozmi- > vomi-. We more often find an s-stem for an earlier j-stem, cf. cinis. The
nom.sg. vomis seems to retain the old nom.
Bibl.: WH II: 835, EM 752, IEW 1179f.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vos 'you (pi.)' [pron. pers. 2p.] (Lex XII, Andr.+; nora.acc. vos, databl. vdbts; gen.
vestrum P1.+, vestri Ter.+, vos tr drum [m.], vostrarum [f.])
Derivatives: voster (before 150 BC) / vester (after 150 BC) 'your' [pron. poss. 2p.]
(Naev.+).
Pit. *wos, *wo-f-, *wos-tero~. It. cognates: Fal. ues 'you'; Pael. mts [nom.], mis
[dat.] 'you' (pi.) < *vms [nom.], *wo-fos [dat.]; U. uestra [abl.sg.f] 'your' (pi.) <
*wes-tero~.
PIE acc. *uos, gen. *uo/es, adj.poss. *us-tero-? IE cognates: Skt. vas 'you' [acc.pl,
gen.pl, dat.pl.], OAv. v§ [gen.dat.pl.], YAv. vo (< *uas); OAv. vo [acc.pl.] (< *ijcis)
'you', OCS vy [nom.], vas-b [gen.acc.loc.], varnb [dat.].
The original nom. *iuH(s) was replaced by the stressed acc. form *wos, apparently
already in Pit. The gen. vestrum is originally the gen.pl. of vester, it was replaced by
vestri. The e of U. uestra probably shows Pit. *westero-, while Latin has analogical
voster after noster. In Faliscan, the e spread to the accented personal pronoun.
Bibl.: WH II: 836f, EM 753, IEW 513f, Giacomelli 1963: 257f, Leumann 1977:
463-466, Sihler 1995: 379-383, Beekes 1995: 208, Meiser 1998: 156-159, Untermann
2000:851,865.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: voveo 'to promise, vow' [v. II; pf. vdvT, ppp. votum] (Pl +)
5
Derivatives: votum [vootum in a Fal. inscr.] 'vow, prayer' (Naev.+); convovere 'to
join in taking a vow' (SCBac.), devotare 'to put a spell on' (P1.+).
Pit. *wow-eje/o- 'to promise'. It. cognates: maybe U. vufru [acc.sg.m.] 'fulfilling an
oath' < *uogwh-ro-\ also U. vufetes [dat.abl.pl.n.] 'votive?' < *uogvheto~r>
PIE *h|UOgwll-eie- 'to promise'. IE cognates: Skt. pr. ohate [3p.med.], ohana-
[ptc.med.] 'to praise, announce', oha- [m.] 'praising', ohas- [n.] 'praise', OAv.
aogzda [3s.inj.med.], pairiiaoyza [2s.inj.pr.med.], YAv. aoxta [3s.inj.med.], aojana-
[ptc.med.] 'to say, announce' < Ilr. *Ha(H)ugh- < PIE *hehug<u')h- [pr.red.]; Skt.
l l
vaghdt- [m.] 'singer, priest' < PIE *h]Uogwh-et-/-nt-m, Gr. eir/opai 'to pray, vow, boast'
< *h|eugwtl-e-, Myc. e-u-ke-to 'zvyemi 'to declare', Gr. pret. EUKTO; Gr. EUX0^
'glory'; Arm. gog 'say!'.
The causative voveo < *uogwh-eie- shows that the lenition of medial *-gwh- > *-w-
postdates the change of PIE *-ow- > *-aw-. Since PIE *uo- generally developed into
va- in open syllable, voveo must have restored the o-vowel after this sound change
took place, on the model of other causative verbs. The ppp. votum was contracted
from *wowito- in which -i- is the unsyncopated reduced medial vowel.
}
Bibl.: WH II: 837, EM 753, IEW 348, Giacomelli 1963: 258f, Schrijver 1991: 76,
279, 472, Untermann 2000: 861-63, LIV *h,ueg-h-.

content:
de Vaan Latin Etymology: vox, vocis 'voice' [f. k] (P1.+)
Derivatives: vocula 'soft voice' (Titin.+), voc alls 'able to speak, with a voice'
(Varro+), vocare 'to call, summon' (Lex XII+), vocdbulum 'name, noun' (P1.+),
vocdmen 'designation' (Lucr.+), vocitare 'to call' (P1.+); vociferdrefi 'to shout, cry'
(Varro+), vociferatio 'clamour' (Afran.+), vocijicare 'to utter loud sounds' (Varro+);
advocare 'to call upon, summon' (PL+}, arvocitat 'summons often' (Paul, ex F.),
convocare 'to convoke' (PI+), evocare 'to evoke, summon' (PI.+), invocdre 'to call
upon, pray for' (Naev.+), provocdre 'to call forth, challenge' (Andr.+), provocator
'challenger' (P1.+), revocdre 'to summon back, recall' (P1.+).
Pit. *wokws *wokw-. It. cognates: U. suboco [acc.sg. or pi.] 'invocation' <
i
*sub-wok-(o-); U. subocauu, subocau 'I invoke' < *sub-wok-a-je-.
PIE *uokw-s, *uokw-m [f.] 'voice'. IE cognates: Skt. vak [nom.sg.], vdcatn [acc.],
vdcas [gen.] [f.] 'voice, speech', vdgvin- 'eloquent', OAv. vaxs [nom.sg.], vacam
[acc.], vaco [gen.], YAv. vaxs [nom.sg.], vdcim [acc.], vaca [ins.], vacqm [gen.pl.]
'voice', Gr. oir- [f.] 'voice, sound, word': bm. [acc.sg.], OTCOI; [gen.], om [dat.]; ToB
wek [m.sg.], ToA wak 'voice, noise'.
The verb vocare must be a denominative from the oblique stem *uokw- of vox.
Possibly, it went through an intermediate derivative *uokw-a- 'voices, call'.
Bibl.: WH II: 823f, EM 753f, IEW 1135f., Schrijver 1991: 472, Untermann 2000:
707f, LIV *ueky-.
